subject: Orthopedics
question: Needle-shaped crystals negatively birefringent on polarized microscopy is characteristic of which crystal-associated arthropathy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. CPPD: CPPD, or pseudogout, is characterized by the deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals in the joints. These crystals are rhomboid-shaped and are positively birefringent under polarized light.
• Option C. Neuropathic arthropathy: Neuropathic arthropathy is associated with nerve damage, often seen in conditions such as diabetes. It is characterized by joint damage and deformities due to the loss of sensation and proprioception. Crystal deposition is not a typical feature of neuropathic arthropathy.
• Option D. Hemophilic arthropathy: Hemophilic arthropathy is a condition that affects joints in individuals with hemophilia. It is characterized by recurrent bleeding into the joints, leading to chronic inflammation, synovial hypertrophy, and joint damage. Crystals are not typically associated with hemophilic arthropathy.
• Educational Objective:
• Gout is a form of inflammatory arthritis caused by the deposition of monosodium urate crystals in the joints. These crystals appear as needle-shaped and are negatively birefringent when viewed under polarized light.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Joint erosion is not a feature in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Gout. Gout is characterized by the deposition of urate crystals in joints, leading to inflammation. If left untreated and chronic, gout can cause joint damage and erosion over time.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis. Joint erosion is a prominent feature of rheumatoid arthritis. Chronic inflammation in RA can lead to the destruction of cartilage and bone in affected joints.
• Option D. Psoriasis. Psoriatic arthritis, associated with psoriasis, can cause joint damage, including joint erosion. Joint involvement in psoriatic arthritis may lead to erosive changes similar to those seen in rheumatoid arthritis.
• Educational Objective:
• While joint involvement can occur in SLE, erosive changes are generally less common compared to conditions like rheumatoid arthritis or psoriatic arthritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The most commonly involved joint in pseudogout is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Great toe . While pseudogout can affect various joints, the great toe is not the most commonly involved joint. Great toe is most commonly involved in the gout.
• Option C. Hip. The hip joint can be affected by pseudogout, but it is not the most commonly involved joint.
• Option D. Elbow. The elbow joint can also be affected by pseudogout, but, similar to the hip, it is not the most commonly involved joint.
• Educational Objective:
• Pseudogout can affect various joints, the knee is the most commonly involved joint. The knee joint is often a primary site of crystal deposition and subsequent inflammation in pseudogout.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Multiple loose bodies are seen maximum in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteochondritis dissecans. This condition involves the separation of a fragment of bone and cartilage from the underlying bone. While it can result in loose bodies, they are typically not numerous.
• Option C. Osteoarthritis . Osteoarthritis is characterized by the breakdown of cartilage in joints, but it typically doesn't result in multiple loose bodies.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis . Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune condition that primarily affects the synovial lining of joints. While it can lead to joint damage, it's less likely to result in multiple loose bodies compared to synovial chondromatosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of multiple loose bodies is most commonly associated with synovial chondromatosis. It involves the synovium, the lining of the joint, which undergoes metaplasia and forms cartilaginous nodules that may detach and become free-floating within the joint space.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Epiphyseal enlargement is seen in:

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Rickets . Rickets is a condition caused by a deficiency of vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate, leading to impaired mineralization of growing bones. In rickets, the epiphyses of the long bones may become enlarged, leading to deformities.
• Option B. Haemophilia. Hemophilia is a bleeding disorder, and its effects on bones are often related to bleeding into joints, which can lead to joint damage and deformities. Epiphyseal enlargement typical feature of hemophilia.
• Option C. Septic Arthritis . Septic arthritis is an infection of the joint, and it may lead to destruction of the joint structures. Epiphyseal enlargement is characteristic of septic enlargement.
• Educational Objective:
• Epiphyseal enlargement refers to an abnormal increase in the size of the epiphysis, which is the rounded end of a long bone that forms part of a joint. Epiphyseal enlargement is seen in rickets, hemophilia and septic arthritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not affected in gout?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Skin . In advanced cases of gout, tophi may develop under the skin. Tophi are nodular masses composed of urate crystals and can be found in various tissues, including the skin. These tophi can lead to skin changes, such as nodules or lumps, and may be visible or palpable.
• Option C. Cartilage. Gout can affect cartilage indirectly by causing joint damage. Urate crystals can accumulate in the joints, leading to inflammation and damage to the cartilage over time. Joint erosion and deformities may occur as a result of chronic gout, especially if the condition is not adequately managed. They most commonly affect the cartilage of the ear i.e., the pinna.
• Option D. Tendon. Tendons around the joints can be affected in gout. Urate crystal deposition in and around the tendons can lead to inflammation, a condition known as tenosynovitis . This inflammation can cause pain, swelling, and difficulty with joint movement.
• Educational Objective:
• Muscle tissue is not a primary target of urate crystal deposition in gout. The condition can affect the skin (through tophi), cartilage (indirectly causing joint damage), and tendons (leading to tenosynovitis).

subject: Orthopedics
question: Calcification of menisci is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Hyperparathyroidism. Calcification of menisci can be seen in hyperparathyroidism but pseudogut is the commonest cause.
• Option C. Renal osteodystrophy. Renal osteodystrophy is a bone disorder that occurs in the context of chronic kidney disease (CKD). It is characterized by abnormalities in bone metabolism and mineralization due to impaired kidney function. While renal osteodystrophy can affect bone density and structure, calcification of the menisci is not a common feature of this condition.
• Option D. Acromegaly. Acromegaly is a disorder caused by excessive growth hormone production, often due to a pituitary tumor. Calcification of menisci can be seen in acromegaly but it is less common than pseudogout.
• Educational Objective:
• Calcification of menisci is seen in pseudogout. It can be seen in hyperthyroidism and acromegaly also but pseudogout is the commonest cause.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In uncemented arthroplasty of the hip, the stem remains attached to the bone by:
options: Bone Ingrowth/overgrowth over the surface of the stem
Mechanical bonding between the stem and bone
Press fitting of the stem in the tight canal
Adhesion between the stem and bone due to adhesive properties of the stem

explanation.short: Ans. A) Bone ingrowth/overgrowth over the surface of the stem
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Mechanical bonding between the stem and bone . While the concept of mechanical bonding is related to achieving stability between the stem and bone, in uncemented arthroplasty, the primary mechanism is bone ingrowth. Mechanical stability is an important aspect, but it is secondary to achieving biological fixation through bone ingrowth.
• Option C. Press fitting of the stem in the tight canal. Press fitting involves placing the implant into the bone without using cement, relying on the interference fit between the implant and the bone. While press fitting is a component of uncemented arthroplasty, the long-term stability is mainly achieved through bone ingrowth onto the implant surface.
• Option D. Adhesion between the stem and bone due to adhesive properties of the stem. Adhesion is not the primary mechanism for fixation in uncemented arthroplasty. The goal is to promote biological fixation through bone ingrowth, where bone tissue grows onto the surface of the implant.
• Educational Objective:
• In uncemented hip arthroplasty, the goal is for the bone to grow onto the surface of the implant, creating a stable and durable fixation. This process is known as osseointegration . Bone ingrowth helps provide long-term stability and support for the artificial hip joint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 58-year-old female patient presents with one-year history of anterior knee pain on climbing stairs. On examination, crepitus was present. There was severe restriction of movements beyond 110 degrees. Examination of hip and back was normal. Diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Psoriatic arthritis. Inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis. Symptoms can involve various joints and may present with pain, swelling, and morning stiffness. Psoriatic arthritis may affect the knees, but other joints, skin, and nails are often involved. X-ray will show joint erosion and new bone formation.
• Option C. Osteonecrosis. Death of bone tissue due to impaired blood supply. Pain and limited joint mobility are common. X-ray may show normal or non-specific changes.
• Option D. Gout. Inflammatory arthritis caused by the deposition of urate crystals in joints. Sudden onset of severe pain, swelling, and redness in the affected joint (usually the big toe). X-ray shows joint space narrowing, tophi may be visible.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteoarthritis of knee commonly presents with symptoms of knee pain, pain on climbing stairs or knee bending, crepitus on knee movement, severe restriction of movement. X-ray findings in OA may include joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following are radiological features of Rheumatoid arthritis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Decreased Joint space. In RA, joint space narrowing is a common finding due to cartilage destruction and erosion.
• Option B. Articular Erosion. Erosions of joint surfaces are a hallmark of RA.
• Option C. Periarticular Osteopenia. Periarticular osteopenia, or bone density reduction around affected joints, is commonly seen in RA.
• Educational Objective:
• Radiographic features of Rheumatoid arthritis is joint space narrowing, articular erosion and periarticular osteopenia. While joint stiffness and ankylosis can occur in RA, the more common form is bony ankylosis rather than fibrous ankylosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Boutonniere deformity has:
options: Hyperextension of PIP joints & flexion of DIP joints
Hyperextension of DIP joints & flexion PIP joints
Flexion of DIP joints & Extension at metacarpophalangeal joints

explanation.short: Ans. B) Hyperextension of DIP joints & flexion PIP joints
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Boutonniere deformity is the flexion contracture of PIP joints and hyperextension of DIP joints. It is due to rupture of extensor tendon.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about ankylosing spondylitis is/are: Pain relieves on rest Association with HLA B-27 Enthesitis may occur 30% cases have acute posterior uveitis Cardiac conduction defect may present

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Ankylosing spondylitis is associated with HLA-B27 in > 90% patients. Enthesis of the spine and pelvis is the most common articular manifestation. 30% patients have acute anterior uveitis – the most common extra-articular manifestation. Upto 60% patients have 3 rd degree heart block and cardiac dysfunction/aortic incompetence. With rest or a period of inactivity, the pain reappears and worsens.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A burn patient develops claw hand. Joint affected will be: Flexion at proximal interphalangeal joint Flexion at distal interphalangeal joint Thumb abduction Flexion at metacarpophalangeal joint Extension at metacarpophalangeal joint

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Post burn, the claw hand developed shows flexion at proximal interphalangeal joint, flexion at distal interphalangeal joint and extension at metacarpophalangeal joint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about reactive arthritis is/are: Triad of arthritis, Urethritis and Conjunctivitis is seen Hyperkeratotic lesions on palms & soles are seen It is related to infections by chlamydia, shigella Anterior Uveitis is seen

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Reactive arthritis is an inflammatory arthritis caused by bacterial infection from chlamydia and shigella. It involves a triad of arthritis, urethritis and conjunctivitis, which are classical of this condition. The most common ocular manifestation seen is the anterior uveitis. The most common musculoskeletal manifestations are the oligoarthritis involving the lower extremities, lumbar spine and the SI joint; and enthesitis in the Achilles tendon. The most common dermatological manifestation is the presence of hyperkeratotic lesions on the palms and the soles.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 65-year-old man had H/o of back pain since 3 months. ESR is raised. He also has dorsolumbar tenderness on examination and mild restriction of chest movements. On X-ray, syndesmophytes are present in vertebrae. Diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Ankylosing spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis is present in young age group. Sacroiliitis is always present. Chest expansion is marked but not reliable in elderly. ESR will be high. Syndesmophytes will be present. Lumbar spine is more commonly involved.
• Option B. Degenerative osteoarthritis of spine. Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the breakdown of cartilage in joints, leading to pain and stiffness. It is more commonly associated with aging and wear and tear on joints. Upper cervical and lumbar spine are more commonly involved and are accompanied with radicular symptoms.
• Option D. Lumbar canal stenosis . Lumbar canal stenosis involves the narrowing of the spinal canal in the lumbar (lower back) region, which can lead to compression of the spinal cord and nerves. Symptoms may include back pain, leg pain, and neurogenic claudication (pain and weakness in the legs during walking). While lumbar canal stenosis can cause back pain, the presence of syndesmophytes and the inflammatory nature of the symptoms in the given case make ankylosing hyperostosis a more relevant diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The given complaints of the patient of increased ESR, dosrolumbar region pain and tenderness with mildly restricted chest movements and, the elder age group of the patient are suggestive of presence of ankylosing hyperostosis, which is different from ankylosing spondylitis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Pencil-in-cup deformity is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the joints. The "pencil-in-cup" deformity is not a classic feature of RA. Instead, RA is often associated with joint erosions, synovial inflammation, and deformities like ulnar deviation of the digits with radial deviation of the wrist, swan neck deformity, boutonniere deformity of the fingers.
• Option B. Ankylosing spondylitis . Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of inflammatory arthritis that mainly affects the spine. The hands are not commonly involved in ankylosing spondylitis. Deformities like bamboo spine, dagger sign, trolley track sign are seen in the spine in the advanced stages of the disease.
• a. Trolley track sign; b. Bamboo spine; c. Dagger sign
• Option C. AVN . Avascular necrosis is the death of bone tissue due to a lack of blood supply. It can occur in various joints. AVN is typically associated with bone collapse and joint destruction. With the advancement of the necrosis, flattening of the femoral head can become evident on a plain radiograph.
• Educational Objective:
• The "pencil-in-cup" deformity is a classic radiological finding in psoriatic arthritis. It refers to erosive changes at the joint margins, resembling a pencil eroding into the cup-like joint space.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are true regarding psoriatic arthritis except:
options: Lengthening of digits called as telescoping

explanation.short: Ans. D) Lengthening of digit known as telescoping
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Arthritis mutilans. It is a rare form of inflammatory arthritis that causes severe damage to joints and bone tissue. It most commonly develops in people with psoriatic arthritis.
• Option B. DIP involvement. Psoriatic arthritis is known for staring at the DIP joints, which are the points closest to the fingernails and toenails.
• Option C. Ankylosis of small joints . Psoriatic arthritis can lead to ankylosis, which is the fusion of bones in a joint, especially in the small joints of the hands and feet.
• Educational Objective:
• The statement which is incorrect about psoriatic arthritis is lengthening of digits known as telescoping. Psoriatic arthritis is associated with shortening of digits, known as telescoping. This occurs due to osteolysis of the bone due to inflammation.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common muscle for pseudotumor-like growth in hemophiliac arthropathy is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Quadriceps femoris. The quadriceps femoris is a group of four muscles located at the front of the thigh. In hemophilic arthropathy, chronic bleeding into the quadriceps muscles can lead to the formation of pseudotumor-like growths, causing swelling and deformities in the affected area. This is the second most commonly involved muscle after iliopsoas from pseudotumors.
• Option B. Hamstring muscle and Option C. Gastrocnemius are not commonly involved in pseudotumor in hemophilic arthropathy.
• Educational Objective:
• The iliopsoas is a group of muscles that includes the psoas major, psoas minor, and iliacus, and it is located in the hip region. Pseudotumor-like growths are most commonly seen in the iliopsoas muscles in hemophiliac arthropathy. Note : Iliopsoas > Quadriceps femoris

subject: Orthopedics
question: Clutton’s joints are:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. End stage tuberculous joints. Clutton’s joints are effusion joints that are sterile and non-tubercular.
• Option C. Associated with trauma. Clutton's joints are not associated with trauma. They are associated with congenital syphilis.
• Option D. Usually painful. This statement is false. Clutton's joints are usually painless. They are characterized by symmetrical effusions in the knee joints that are not accompanied by significant pain. The absence of pain is a key characteristic that helps differentiate Clutton's joints from other joint conditions.
• Educational Objective:
• Clutton's joints are associated with syphilis. Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum. In the context of syphilis, Clutton's joints refer to painless effusions, particularly involving the knee joints.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Drug of choice for the treatment of acute gout in patients in whom NSAIDs are contraindicated is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Allopurinol. Allopurinol is not typically used for the treatment of acute gout attacks. It is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor and is primarily used for long-term management to lower uric acid levels in the blood, preventing the formation of urate crystals. Allopurinol is more commonly indicated for chronic gout treatment rather than for immediate relief during an acute attack.
• Option C. Xyloric acid. Xyloric acid is not typically used for the treatment of acute gout attack.
• Option D. Paracetamol . Paracetamol (acetaminophen) is a pain reliever but is generally not the first-line choice for the treatment of acute gout attacks.
• Educational Objective:
• Colchicine is an anti-inflammatory medication that is effective in the treatment of acute gout attacks. It works by inhibiting the inflammatory response to urate crystals, reducing pain and inflammation. Colchicine is commonly used when NSAIDs are contraindicated or not well-tolerated.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is not true about gout?
options: Abrupt increase in serum urate levels is a more common cause for acute gout than an abrupt fall in urate levels
Patient may be asymptomatic with high serum uric acid for years
Development of arthritis correlates with level of serum uric acid
Uric acid crystals are best seen by polarizing light microscope

explanation.short: Ans. A) Abrupt increase in serum urate levels is a more common cause for acute gout than an abrupt fall in urate levels
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Patient may be asymptomatic with high serum uric acid for years. Patients with elevated serum uric acid levels may remain asymptomatic for years before experiencing their first gout attack. Not everyone with hyperuricemia develops gout, and factors such as genetics, diet, and comorbidities play a role in the manifestation of gout.
• Option C. Development of arthritis correlates with level of serum uric acid. There is a correlation between the development of arthritis in gout and the level of serum uric acid. Higher levels of serum uric acid increase the risk of urate crystal deposition in joints, leading to gouty arthritis.
• Option D. Uric acid crystals are best seen by polarizing light microscope. Uric acid crystals are best visualized under a polarizing light microscope, where they appear as needle-shaped crystals with negative birefringence.
• Educational Objective:
• An abrupt fall in serum urate levels, which can occur due to factors like rapid initiation of urate-lowering therapy, is more commonly associated with acute gout attacks.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 40-year-old female presents with urine darkening on standing, joint stiffness, and pigment deposition in joints. What is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Phenylketonuria. Phenylketonuria is a genetic disorder caused by a deficiency of the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase, leading to the accumulation of phenylalanine in the blood. If not managed early, it can result in intellectual disabilities. Reduced Tyr can cause decreased melanin synthesis and light skin and hair. Signs and symptoms of the disease that become more pronounced when untreated include delayed developmental milestones, microcephaly, hypopigmentation, hyperactivity/behavior problems, seizures, and a musty odor to skin and urine. PKU is not associated with urine darkening on standing or pigment deposition in joints.
• Option B. Tyrosinemia. Tyrosinemia is a group of rare genetic disorders characterized by the accumulation of tyrosine in the body. There are different types of tyrosinemia, and they can affect the liver, kidneys, and nervous system. However, like PKU, tyrosinemia is not typically associated with urine darkening or pigment deposition in joints.
• Option D. Hemosiderin deposition. Hemosiderin is a pigment derived from hemoglobin breakdown and is typically seen in conditions involving chronic bleeding or iron overload. While hemosiderin deposition can cause discoloration of tissues, it's less likely to present with urine darkening and joint stiffness compared to alkaptonuria.
• Educational Objective:
• Alkaptonuria is a rare metabolic disorder characterized by the deficiency of the enzyme homogentisate 1,2-dioxygenase, leading to the accumulation of homogentisic acid. This acid, when exposed to air, darkens and causes urine to turn dark upon standing.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The pathognomonic finding in pseudogout is:

explanation.short: Ans. A) CPPD crystals under microscope
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Polyarthritis with urinary sediment. Polyarthritis refers to inflammation affecting multiple joints. However, urinary sediment is not a characteristic finding of pseudogout. Instead, it may be more associated with conditions like systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) or other autoimmune disorders.
• Option C. Juxta-articular osteopenia. Juxta-articular osteopenia, or decreased bone density near joints, is not a pathognomonic finding for pseudogout. It may be seen in various joint conditions, including inflammatory arthritis, but it is not specific to pseudogout.
• Option D. Bone spurs . Bone spurs, also known as osteophytes, are bony outgrowths that can develop on the edges of joints. While pseudogout can contribute to joint damage and degeneration, the presence of bone spurs is not pathognomonic for pseudogout.
• Educational Objective:
• Pseudogout, or calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition disease (CPPD), is characterized by the deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals in the joints. These crystals are rhomboid-shaped and exhibit positive birefringence under polarized light microscopy. The identification of CPPD crystals in synovial fluid is a key diagnostic feature of pseudogout.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Chondrocalcinosis is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Hypoparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism and not hypoparathyroidism is associated with chondrocalcinosis.
• Option C. Rickets . Rickets is a condition primarily associated with vitamin D deficiency or impaired vitamin D metabolism, leading to problems with bone mineralization. Chondrocalcinosis is not a characteristic feature of rickets.
• Option D. Hypervitaminosis . Hypervitaminosis refers to excessive intake of vitamins. While excessive vitamin intake can have various effects on the body, chondrocalcinosis is not a typical manifestation of hypervitaminosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Chondorocalcinosis or the deposition of calcium salt in the articular cartilage is associated with pseudogout most commonly, followed by alkaptonuria.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Choose the correct statement about articular cartilage. Zone 2 contains articular cartilage progenitor cell Zone 4 contains calcified cartilage Zone 3 has highest proteoglycans content Zone 1 has high water content Zone 3 has chondrocytes in low density

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Zone 2 contains articular cartilage progenitor cells. False. The progenitor cells are found in the zone 1 of the articular cartilage. Zone 4 contains calcified cartilage. True. The zone 2 of the articular cartilage is called the calcified or tidemark zone because at this zone, the articular cartilage is calcified and provides the structural support. Zone 3 has highest proteoglycans content . True. Both zone 2 and 3 of the articular cartilage have a higher amount of the proteoglycan content. Zone 1 has high water content . True. Zone 1 has the higher amount of water and collagen content. Zone 3 has chondrocytes in low density. False. Low chondrocyte content is present in the zone 2 of the articular cartilage.
• Educational Objective
• Zone 2 contains low chondrocytes but high proteoglycans. Zone 4 contains calcified cartilage. Zone 3 has highest proteoglycans content. Zone 1 has high water content.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Synovial fluid of low viscosity is seen in all except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gout, Option B. Septic arthritis, and Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis are characterized by decreased viscosity of the synovial fluid.
• Educational Objective:
• Low viscosity of synovial fluid is seen in Rheumatic fever, Rheumatic arthritis, gout, Pyogenic arthritis, Tubercular arthritis. Osteoarthritis is normal synovial fluid viscosity.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not true about osteoarthritis?
options: Narrowing of the joint space occurs

explanation.short: Ans. A) Inflammation of synovial joints
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Most common disease of joints . True. Osteoarthritis is indeed the most common joint disorder. It affects millions of people worldwide, especially as they age. The degeneration of joint cartilage and changes in underlying bone are characteristic features.
• Option C. Affects shoulder joint . True. While osteoarthritis is more commonly associated with weight-bearing joints like the knees and hips, it can affect any joint in the body, including the shoulder. In the shoulder joint, osteoarthritis may lead to pain, stiffness, and reduced range of motion.
• Option D. Narrowing of the joint space . True. Radiographically, osteoarthritis is characterized by the narrowing of the joint space. As the cartilage in the joint wears away, the space between the bones decreases. This narrowing is a hallmark feature seen on X-rays in individuals with osteoarthritis.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteoarthritis is primarily characterized by degeneration of the joint cartilage and changes in the underlying bone. Unlike rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis is not characterized by significant inflammation of the synovial membrane. Instead, it involves a more gradual and wear-and-tear type of joint damage.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is true about Articular cartilage?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Has no nerve supply
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Very vascular structure. False. Articular cartilage is avascular i.e., it lacks blood vessels. It relies on diffusion from the synovial fluid to obtain nutrients and oxygen. The absence of blood vessels contributes to the limited regenerative capacity of articular cartilage.
• Option B. Surrounded by thick perichondrium. False. Articular cartilage does not have a perichondrium. Perichondrium is a dense connective tissue layer that surrounds most cartilage types, but articular cartilage lacks this outer layer. This absence contributes to the limited ability of articular cartilage to repair and regenerate.
• Option D. Fibrocartilage . False. Articular cartilage is predominantly hyaline cartilage, not fibrocartilage. Fibrocartilage is a different type of cartilage that contains more collagen fibres and is found in structures like the intervertebral discs and certain joint structures.
• Educational Objective:
• Articular cartilage is avascular and aneural i.e., it lacks blood vessels and nerves.

subject: Orthopedics
question: CASPAR criteria is used in diagnosis of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rheumatoid arthritis . Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the joints, causing inflammation, pain, and joint damage. Diagnosis of RA is based on clinical features, laboratory tests (such as rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies), and imaging studies. The 1987 ACR/EULAR classification criteria is used for RA diagnosis. It requires presence of at least 4 out of 7 symptoms for at least 3 months.
• Morning stiffness , in and around joint lasting 1 hour before maximal improvement. Arthritis of 3 or more joint areas, observed by a physician simultaneously, have soft tissue swelling or joint effusion, not just bony over growth. The 14 possible joint areas involved are right or left PIP, MCP, wrist, elbow, knee, ankle and MTP joints. Arthritis of hand joints, e.g. wrist, MP or PIP joints. Symmetrical arthritis. Rheumatoid nodules (Pathognomonic): Subcutaneous nodules over bony prominences, extensor surfaces or juxta-articular region. Serum rheumatoid factor. Radiological changes : bony erosion or unequivocal bony decalcification, periarticular osteoporosis and narrowing of articular (joint) space.
• Option C. Ankylosing spondylitis . Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a type of inflammatory arthritis that mainly affects the spine, causing stiffness, pain, and eventually fusion of the vertebrae. Diagnosis of AS involves clinical features, imaging studies (like X-rays showing syndesmophytes), and sometimes blood tests. A diagnosis of AS can usually be confirmed if an X-ray shows sacroiliitis and there is at least 1 of the following :
• at least 3 months of lower back pain that gets better with exercise and doesn't improve with rest limited movement in lumbar spine limited chest expansion compared
• Option D. Reactive synovitis. Reactive synovitis refers to inflammation of the synovial membrane in response to an infection elsewhere in the body, often in the gastrointestinal or genitourinary tract. It is not a specific term used for a well-defined rheumatological condition. Diagnosis involves assessing the clinical context, including symptoms and potential triggers of the inflammation. According to American College of Rheumatology, there are 2 major and 2 minor diagnostic guidelines of reactive arthritis. Major guidelines includes a symmetric oligo or monoarthritis involving lower extremities, and either enteritis or urethritis symptoms preceding the onset of arthritis by a time interval of 3 days to 6 weeks. Minor guidelines includes presence of a triggering infection as evidenced by culture positivity, and presence of persistent synovial involvement.
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriatic arthritis is an inflammatory arthritis that occurs in some individuals with psoriasis, a chronic skin condition characterized by red, scaly patches on the skin. CASPAR criteria (Classification Criteria for Psoriatic Arthritis) include the presence of inflammatory articular disease (joint involvement), along with points for other features such as psoriasis, nail changes, family history, and negative tests for rheumatoid factor.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 65-year-old male underwent total hip replacement surgery due to severe osteoarthritis. The surgery went uneventful, but on 3 rd post-operative day, he started complaining of breathlessness which worsens on increase in activity, chest discomfort. Lab investigations reveals raised d-dimer levels. What is the definitive management of this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Bronchodilators. Bronchodilators are primarily used in conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) to relieve airway constriction. In the context of a pulmonary embolism, bronchodilators would not be the first-line treatment, as the primary issue is a blood clot affecting blood flow to the lungs.
• Option C. Steroids. Steroids are generally not the primary treatment for a pulmonary embolism. They would not address the underlying cause of the symptoms, which is likely a blood clot. Steroids might be used for certain inflammatory conditions but are not indicated as a definitive treatment for pulmonary embolism.
• Options D. Oxygen . Oxygen is often given to patients experiencing breathlessness or hypoxia (low oxygen levels). While providing oxygen can support the patient's respiratory function, it does not directly address the underlying cause of a pulmonary embolism. Oxygen is supportive care and is often used while other definitive treatments, such as anticoagulation, are initiated.
• Educational Objective:
• Thrombolysis is commonly used in cases of severe pulmonary embolism where there is a significant compromise of blood flow to the lungs. Thrombolysis aims to rapidly break down the clot and restore normal blood circulation.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In OA, one of this is beneficial:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Ketones. Ketones are organic compounds produced when the body breaks down fat for energy in the absence of sufficient glucose. Ketones are not typically associated with direct benefits in osteoarthritis.
• Option C. Glucose. It is a simple sugar and a primary source of energy for the body. While maintaining stable blood glucose levels is important for overall health, glucose itself is not specifically considered a treatment or beneficial intervention for osteoarthritis.
• Option D. Citric acid . Citric acid is a weak organic acid found in citrus fruits. Citric acid is not typically associated with direct benefits in osteoarthritis.
• Educational Objective:
• Glucosamine is a natural compound found in the body, particularly in the fluid that surrounds the joints. It is commonly taken as a dietary supplement. It helps to reduce pain and improve function in individuals with osteoarthritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following statements is true about Articular Cartilage?
options: It is a type of elastic cartilage
It is hyaline cartilage on the articular surface of bones
It is type 1 cartilage providing compressive strength
It is hyaline cartilage found in growth plate of bones

explanation.short: Ans. B) It is hyaline cartilage on the articular surface of bones
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Is a type of elastic cartilage. Articular cartilage is not a type of elastic cartilage. Elastic cartilage contains more elastic fibres and is found in structures like the external ear and epiglottis. Articular cartilage is predominantly hyaline cartilage.
• Option C. Is type 1 cartilage providing compressive strength . Articular cartilage provides a smooth and low-friction surface for joint movement .
• Option D. Is hyaline cartilage found in growth plate of bone. T he hyaline cartilage found in the growth plate (epiphyseal plate) of bones is a different type of cartilage. It is involved in longitudinal bone growth and is eventually replaced by bone tissue as a person reaches skeletal maturity. Articular cartilage is found at the articulating surface of the joints.
• Educational Objective:
• The statement true about articular cartilage is that it is hyaline cartilage on the articular surface of bones. It is not an elastic cartilage. It provides smooth surface at the joint for movements.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nutrient and oxygen reach the chondrocytes across perichondrium by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Capillaries. Capillaries are small blood vessels that bring nutrients and oxygen to tissues. In the context of cartilage, the perichondrium is vascularized with capillaries.
• Option C. Along Neurons. Neurons are not the primary means by which nutrients and oxygen are transported to chondrocytes.
• Option D. Active transport. Active transport is a cellular process that requires energy to move molecules against their concentration gradient. In the context of nutrient and oxygen supply to chondrocytes, passive processes like diffusion is more relevant .
• Educational Objective:
• Diffusion is a process by which molecules move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. Since articular cartilage is an avascular structure, it gets its nutrients from the blood supply of the perichondrium. So nutrients and oxygen reach the chondrocytes across perichondrium by the process of diffusion.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following cause erosive arthritis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disorder that primarily affects the joints. Rheumatoid Arthritis is known for causing erosive arthritis, as chronic inflammation in the synovium leads to the destruction of cartilage and bone within the joints.
• Option C. Tuberculous arthritis. Tuberculous arthritis, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, can lead to joint destruction. It can cause erosive arthritis.
• Option D. Septic arthritis. Septic arthritis is caused by a bacterial infection within a joint, leading to inflammation and potential joint damage.
• Educational Objective:
• Lupus Arthritis/Jaccoud arthritis does not cause erosive arthritis. Lupus arthritis, also known as Jaccoud arthritis , is a form of arthritis associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an autoimmune disease. Jaccoud arthritis is generally considered non-erosive inflammatory arthritis. It involves joint deformities that are reversible and does not typically result in the erosive changes.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In the examination of synovium aspirate of a patient with arthritis, numerous lymphocytes, plasma cells and histiocytes are found. What type of arthritis is it?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute pyogenic arthritis. Acute pyogenic arthritis is caused by a bacterial infection in the joint, and the synovial fluid in this condition typically shows an increased number of neutrophils (polymorphonuclear leukocytes).
• Option B. Chronic arthritis. Chronic arthritis is a broad term that can encompass various chronic inflammatory joint conditions like osteoarthritis.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis . Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis characterized by the presence of synovitis. The synovial fluid contains > 50% neutrophils, 5000-50,000 cells, T-lymphocytes, plasma cells.
• Educational Objective:
• In synovial fluid of patients with granulomatous arthritis, numerous lymphocytes, plasma cells and histiocytes are found.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Articular cartilage, most active chondrocytes are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Zone 1 . The chondrocytes in this zone are flat and oriented parallel to the articular surface. These cells in this zone are involved in lubrication and reducing friction within the joint.
• Option B. Zone 2. The chondrocytes in this zone are arranged in columns or clusters. They play a crucial role in growth and maintenance of the cartilage tissue.
• Option D. Zone 4 . The chondrocytes in this zone are embedded within the calcified cartilage. This zone is the transition between cartilage and bone, with chondrocytes undergoing apoptosis as the cartilage mineralizes and becomes ossified.
• Educational Objective:
• In Articular cartilage, Chondrocytes are more active synthetically in middle zone or radial zone or deep zone (zone 3).

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following statements about synovial fluid are true, except:
options: Secreted primarily by type A synovial cells

explanation.short: Ans. A) Secreted primarily by type A synovial cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Follows Non-Newtonian fluid kinetics. True. Synovial fluid exhibits non-Newtonian behaviour, i.e., its viscosity changes with the rate of shear applied. This property allows the fluid to become less viscous during joint movement, facilitating smooth articulation.
• Option C. Contains hyaluronic acid. True. Synovial fluid contains hyaluronic acid, a glycosaminoglycan that contributes to the lubrication and viscosity of the fluid. Hyaluronic acid helps in maintaining the integrity of the synovial fluid film on joint surfaces.
• Option D. Viscosity is variable. True. The viscosity of synovial fluid is variable and influenced by factors such as hydration, the presence of hyaluronic acid, and the overall health of the joint. Viscosity tends to increase with rest and decrease with joint movement.
• Educational Objective:
• Synovial fluid follows Non-Newtonian fluid kinetics. It contains hyaluronic acid. Its viscosity is variable. It is an ultra-dialysate of blood plasma transudated from synovial capillaries to which hyaluronic acid protein complex (mucin) has been added by synovial B cells.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following statements about changes in articular cartilage with aging is not true?
options: Enzymatic degradation of proteoglycans is increased
Total water content of cartilage is decrease

explanation.short: Ans. C) Enzymatic degradation of proteoglycans is increased
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Total proteoglycan content is decreased. True. Proteoglycans are the molecules in the extracellular fluid that help maintain its structure and function. With ageing, the total proteolytic enzyme degradation decreases with increasing age.
• Option B. Synthesis of proteoglycans is decreased. True. With aging, there is a decrease in the synthesis of proteoglycans by chondrocytes. This reduction in synthesis contributes to the decline in total proteoglycan content observed in aging cartilage.
• Option D. Total water content of cartilage is decreased . True. With aging, the total water content of the cartilage is decreased. In osteoarthritis, it is increased, but decreased in advanced OA.
• Educational Objective:
• Enzymatic degradation of proteoglycans is normal with ageing. Total proteoglycan content is decreased. Synthesis of proteoglycans is decreased. Total water content of cartilage is decreased.

subject: Orthopedics
question: After knee replacement surgery, proprioceptors of joints are altered. Effect is:
options: Complete loss of sensation at joint position at resting stage
Loss of sensation of joint position at dynamic stage

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Complete loss of sensation at joint position at resting stage. Complete loss of sensation at the joint position, particularly at the resting stage could result in challenges for the patient in maintaining a sense of joint position when the knee is at rest.
• Option C. Loss of sensation of joints position at dynamic stage. This suggests that the alteration in proprioception is more prominent during dynamic movements rather than at rest.
• Option D. All types of sensation lost. T his implies a complete loss of sensation, which would include not only proprioception but also other types of sensory feedback.
• Educational objective:
• After knee replacement surgery, proprioceptors of joints are altered and the effect will occur on Normal movements. Normal movement may be retained.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Metal on metal articulation should be avoided in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteonecrosis. Osteonecrosis is the death of bone tissue due to a lack of blood supply. Metal-on-metal articulation is not specifically contraindicated in osteonecrosis.
• Option C. Inflammatory arthritis. Metal-on-metal articulation is generally not recommended in patients with inflammatory arthritis, such as rheumatoid arthritis. Inflammatory arthritis patients may have an increased risk of adverse reactions to metal debris, making alternative implant materials, like ceramic or polyethylene, more suitable.
• Option D. Revision surgery. Metal-on-metal articulation is sometimes used in revision surgery, but its use has declined due to concerns about metal ion release. In revision surgeries, the choice of implant material depends on various factors, including the reason for revision and the condition of the surrounding bone.
• Educational Objective:
• Metal-on-metal articulation is often avoided in young, active females due to concerns about wear and the potential for metal ion release. This patient population may have a longer life expectancy, and concerns about the long-term effects of metal ions make alternative implant materials more favourable. They are also avoided in females in a child bearing age as the metal ions can cross the placenta, causing damage to the fetus.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient developed breathlessness and chest pain on second postoperative after a total hip replacement. Echocardiography showed right ventricular dilatation and tricuspid regurgitation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Acute MI. Acute MI refers to the sudden death of part of the heart muscle due to a lack of blood supply. Symptoms include chest pain, shortness of breath, and can sometimes lead to complications such as heart failure with right ventricular dilatation and tricuspid regurgitation, is more indicative of a condition affecting the pulmonary circulation rather than a primary cardiac event. Myocardial infarction can occur after hip replacement surgery but it is less commonly seen than pulmonary thromboembolism.
• Option C. Hypotensive shock. Hypotensive shock refers to a state of insufficient blood flow to meet the body's needs, resulting in low blood pressure. While it can cause symptoms like breathlessness, it usually doesn't present with right ventricular dilatation and tricuspid regurgitation on echocardiography. Total knee arthroplasty can cause severe blood loss that can lead to hypovolemic shock.
• Option D. Cardiac Tamponade. Cardiac tamponade is a condition where fluid accumulates in the pericardial sac, compressing the heart. This can lead to symptoms such as chest pain and shortness of breath. However, it typically presents with other signs, and echocardiography in cardiac tamponade may reveal features like diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle, rather than right ventricular dilatation and tricuspid regurgitation. This condition is rarely seen, but can be seen after posterior pedicle screw fixation of the spine.
• Educational Objective:
• Pulmonary embolism occurs when a blood clot, usually originating from the legs (deep vein thrombosis), travels to the lungs and blocks a pulmonary artery. Symptoms can include sudden onset of breathlessness, chest pain, and in severe cases, right heart strain as evidenced by right ventricular dilatation and tricuspid regurgitation on echocardiography. This is seen as a common complication of total hip replacement surgeries.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is the most common cause of death after total hip replacement?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Infections. Infection is a potential complication after total hip replacement, but it is not typically the most common cause of death. Infections can lead to local complications, such as joint infections (septic arthritis), which may require surgical intervention. Systemic spread leading to death is less common.
• Option B. Deep Vein Thrombosis. It is a significant concern after total hip replacement due to the risk of blood clots forming in the deep veins, usually in the legs. While Deep Vein Thrombosis itself is a serious condition, the most life-threatening complication arises when a clot breaks loose and travels to the lungs, causing a pulmonary embolism (PE).
• Option D. Pneumonia. Pneumonia, an infection of the lungs, can occur after surgery due to factors such as immobility, anesthesia, and decreased lung function. While pneumonia is a concern, it is generally less common as the primary cause of death after total hip replacement compared to complications like pulmonary embolism.
• Educational Objective:
• Pulmonary embolism occurs when a blood clot, often originating from the deep veins of the legs, travels to the lungs, causing a blockage in the pulmonary arteries. This can be a severe and life-threatening complication, especially after orthopedic surgeries where immobility and surgical trauma contribute to the risk of clot formation.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Aseptic loosening in cemented total hip replacement occurs as a result of hypersensitivity response to:

explanation.short: Ans. B) High density polyethylene debris
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Titanium debris. Titanium is a biocompatible material commonly used in orthopedic implants. While titanium debris can be generated from the wear and tear of the implant, it is not typically associated with aseptic loosening. Titanium is generally well-tolerated by the body, and adverse reactions to titanium debris are uncommon.
• Option C. N, N-Dimethyltryptamine. N, N-Dimethyltryptamine (DMT) is a naturally occurring psychedelic compound, and it is not relevant to hip replacement or aseptic loosening. It is not used in implant materials, and there is no association between DMT and hip replacement complications.
• Option D. Free Radicals. Free radicals are reactive molecules that can contribute to oxidative stress and tissue damage. While oxidative stress can play a role in the inflammatory response to wear debris, the direct association of free radicals as a cause of aseptic loosening is not as specific as the association with the wear debris itself.
• Educational Objective:
• Particles of metal (titanium, nickel, chromium, etc.), cement (PMMA) and polyethylene all can produce periprosthetic osteolysis (aseptic loosening). Polyethylene particles appear to be the major particles causing the damage. Since high density polyethylene is used in the acetabular component of the implant, its wearing out and friction generates small particles that can cause inflammatory response in tissues, leading to loosening of the implant.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Major indication(s) for arthroplasty are: Osteoarthritis of hip Ankylosis of elbow Un-united tibial fracture Un-united femoral neck fracture TB spine

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Arthroplasty means construction of a new movable joint. Indications of arthroplasty is osteoarthritis of the hip and knee, ankyloses of the elbow, un-united femoral neck fracture.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Heberden's nodes are seen in:
options: Osteoarthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints
Osteoarthritis of the proximal interphalangeal joints
Osteoarthritis of the proximal interphalangeal joints
Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1st carpometacarpal joint

explanation.short: Ans. A) Osteoarthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Osteoarthritis of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Osteoarthritis of the PIP joint of the hand presents as Bouchard’s nodes.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1st carpometacarpal joint. Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1 st CMC joint of the hand i.e. the base of the thumb presents as the swan neck deformity of the thumb. This involves hyperextension of the interphalangeal joint and flexion of the MCP joint with a consequent loss of thumb mobility and pinch.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints. RA of the DIP joint may present as swan neck deformity i.e., hyperextension of PIP joint and compensatory flexion of the DIP joint.
• Educational Objective:
• Heberden's nodes are a classic feature of osteoarthritis, particularly affecting the DIP joints.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bouchard's nodes are seen in:
options: Osteoarthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints
Osteoarthritis of the proximal interphalangeal joints
Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1st carpometacarpal joint
Rheumatoid arthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints

explanation.short: Ans. B) Osteoarthritis of the proximal interphalangeal joints
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Osteoarthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints Heberden's nodes are associated with OA at the DIP joints, not the PIP joints.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1st carpometacarpal joint. Rheumatoid arthritis of the 1 st CMC joint of the hand i.e. the base of the thumb presents as the swan neck deformity of the thumb. This involves hyperextension of the interphalangeal joint and flexion of the MCP joint with a consequent loss of thumb mobility and pinch.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis of the distal interphalangeal joints. RA of the DIP joint may present as swan neck deformity i.e., hyperextension of PIP joint and compensatory flexion of the DIP joint.
• Educational Objective:
• Bouchard's nodes are associated with osteoarthritis, particularly affecting the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints. The characteristic bony enlargements or osteophytes at the PIP joints are a hallmark feature of osteoarthritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which one of the following bursae always communicates with knee joint cavity?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Prepatellar. It is s ituated in front of the patella, between the skin and the patellar ligament. It reduces friction between the skin and the patella during knee movement. It does not communicate directly with the knee joint cavity. It is a separate bursa.
• Option C. Superficial infrapatellar. Found just below the skin and above the patellar ligament, it aids in reducing friction between the skin and the patellar ligament. It does not directly communicate with the knee joint cavity.
• Option D. Deep infrapatellar. Situated deep within the knee joint, beneath the patellar ligament, it helps to protect the joint and absorb shocks during movements. It does not directly communicate with the knee joint cavity.
• Educational Objective:
• Located above the patella, between the quadriceps tendon and the femur, suprapatellar bursa helps in reducing friction between the quadriceps tendon and the distal femur during knee movements. It communicates with the knee joint cavity. An opening called the " suprapatellar pouch " extends into the joint.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 60 years old female has a previous case history of fever, fatigue, weight loss and polyarthralgia. She had morning stiffness and bilateral hand pain. The radiological findings are given below. What may be your diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/11/picture33.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the breakdown of joint cartilage and the underlying bone. X-ray findings in osteoarthritis include joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Osteoarthritis is not associated with systemic symptoms like fever, fatigue, and weight loss.
• Option C. Gout. Gout is an inflammatory arthritis caused by the deposition of urate crystals in joints, leading to sudden attacks of pain and swelling. In chronic gout, X-ray may show joint erosions and the presence of tophi (deposits of urate crystals). Gout typically presents as acute, intermittent attacks rather than chronic, progressive joint involvement.
• Option D. Psoriatic Arthritis. Psoriatic arthritis is an inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis. May include joint erosions and new bone formation (ankylosis). Psoriatic arthritis often involves the axial skeleton and may have distinct radiological features compared to RA.
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of symptoms like fever, weight loss, fatigue and the involvement of multiple joints, including the hands, is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Radiological findings, such as joint erosions and periarticular osteopenia, are commonly associated with RA.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is the difference between Rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis?
options: Systemic symptoms are seen in osteoarthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis is uncommon in hands and feet

explanation.short: Ans. A) Osteophytes are seen in osteoarthritis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Osteophytes are seen in osteoarthritis. True. Osteoarthritis is characterized by the presence of osteophytes which are the osteocartilaginous outgrowth at the joint margins where the synovium attaches to the edge of the articular cartilage and merges with the periosteum.
• Option B. Systemic symptoms are seen in osteoarthritis . False. Systemic symptoms such as fever, fatigue are seen in rheumatoid arthritis and are not associated with osteoarthritis.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis is uncommon in hands and feet . False. Rheumatoid arthritis often affects small joints, including those in the hands and feet. Osteoarthritis is more commonly seen in the large joints like the knees and the hips.
• Option D. Osteoarthritis is an autoimmune disease . False. Osteoarthritis is not considered as an autoimmune disease. It is an age-related, mechanical, degenerative disease of the joints.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteophytes, also known as bone spurs , are commonly seen in osteoarthritis. They form at the joint margins as a result of the degeneration of cartilage and are a characteristic feature of OA and not RA.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most specific antibody seen in RA is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rheumatoid factor. Rheumatoid factor is an antibody directed against the Fc portion of immunoglobulin G (IgG). It can be positive in a subset of RA patients, but its sensitivity and specificity are lower compared to anti-CCP antibodies.
• Option C. ANA (Antinuclear Antibodies). Antinuclear antibodies target components of the cell nucleus. While ANA can be positive in a variety of autoimmune conditions, it is not specific to RA.
• Option D. Anti dsDNA antibodies. Anti-dsDNA antibodies target double-stranded DNA. Elevated levels of anti-dsDNA antibodies are often seen in SLE and are part of the criteria for diagnosing lupus.
• Educational Objective:
• The anti-CCP, rheumatoid factor, ANA have a high specificity for RA, and their presence is often considered an important diagnostic marker, especially in the early stages of the disease. Out of the given options, Anti-CCP antibodies are the most specific marker for RA diagnosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A person has injury on dorsal surface of proximal interphalangeal joint of right middle finger. Which of the following can occur? Rupture of lateral ligament Buttonhole deformity Mallet finger Laceration of the central slip of the extensor Swan Neck deformity

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Both the Buttonhole deformity and laceration of the central slip can occur due to the described injury on the PIP joint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A middle aged female with rheumatoid arthritis on treatment develops upper motor neuron signs in her limbs. The investigation required to evaluate her further is:
options: Spine lateral view flexion and extension views

explanation.short: Ans. A) Spine lateral view flexion and extension views
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Open mouth view. The open mouth view, also known as the odontoid view . Open mouth view is for atlantoaxial area specially dens but it does not demonstrate instability .
• Option C. Swimmer’s View . The swimmer's view is another radiographic projection that visualizes the cervicothoracic junction, particularly the lower cervical spine and upper thoracic spine.
• Option C. Broden’s View . Broden's view is a specialized radiographic view that is obtained with the patient in the supine position, designed to assess atlantoaxial stability. Broden’s view is for subtalar joint.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of upper motor neuron signs in the limbs in a patient diagnosed with RA suggests the presence of atlantoaxial joint instability. The assessment for the same involves flexion-extension lateral view of cervical spine. Other views like open mouth and swimmer’s views do not provide the clear image of this instability while the Broden’s view is used for the assessment of the subtalar joint of the foot.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding Rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Typically involves small and large joints symmetrically but spares the cervical spine
Causes pleural effusion with low sugar

explanation.short: Ans. B) Causes pleural effusion with low sugar
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Typically involves small and large joints symmetrically but spares the cervical spine. Rheumatoid arthritis typically involves small and large joints symmetrically but in the spine, the upper cervical spine is most commonly affected. The lumbar spine is usually spared in RA.
• Option C. Pulmonary nodules are absent. Rheumatoid arthritis can have pulmonary manifestations, and pulmonary nodules are one of them . Pulmonary nodules in RA can be seen on chest imaging, and they are considered a known complication.
• Option D. Enthesopathy prominent. Enthesopathy (inflammation at sites of tendon or ligament attachments) is not a prominent feature of RA. Enthesopathy is more commonly associated with spondyloarthropathies rather than RA.
• Educational Objective:
• RA causes pleural effusion with low glucose and pH. Enthesopathy is not seen in RA, it is a feature of Ankylosing Spondylitis. Most common part of spine affected in RA is upper cervical .

subject: Orthopedics
question: “Wind-swept deformity” is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Ankylosing Spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints. Ankylosing spondylitis can lead to spinal deformities like kyphosis and lack of spinal mobility.
• Option B. Scurvy. Scurvy is a nutritional deficiency disorder caused by vitamin C deficiency. Wind-swept deformity is not associated with scurvy.
• Option C. Rheumatoid Arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis primarily affects the joints, including the spine. Wind-swept deformity is not a classic term associated with rheumatoid arthritis. It is seen in RA but is more common in rickets.
• Educational Objective:
• Rickets is a condition caused by vitamin D deficiency, leading to impaired bone mineralization. Wind-swept deformity is associated with rickets, specifically a deformity in which the lower limbs are bowed outward while the upper limbs are bowed inward.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A young male having pain with daily morning stiffness of spine for 30 minutes and reduced chest movements. Most probable diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rheumatoid arthritis. RA is a systemic autoimmune disease that primarily affects peripheral joints. Morning stiffness is common in RA but typically lasts for a shorter duration compared to AS. While RA can affect the cervical spine, it is not the primary site of involvement, and chest movements are not typically significantly reduced.
• Option C. Gouty Arthritis . Gout primarily affects peripheral joints and is characterized by recurrent attacks of inflammatory arthritis. Morning stiffness is less characteristic of gout, and spine involvement is not a typical feature.
• Option D. Osteoarthritis. OA is a degenerative joint disease that commonly affects weight-bearing joints. Morning stiffness in OA is usually brief and improves with movement. While OA can affect the spine, the described pattern of morning stiffness and reduced chest movements is not typical for OA.
• Educational Objective:
• Ankylosing spondylitis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy characterized by inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, and reduced chest movements due to involvement of the spine and costovertebral joints.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A young man complaints of pain in lower back. His X-rays are shown below. Most likely diagnosis is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/12/picture4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Spondylolisthesis. Spondylolisthesis is a condition where one vertebra slips forward over the vertebra below it. On X-ray, spondylolisthesis may appear as a forward displacement of one vertebra over another, and it is often associated with specific changes like "step-off" or "spondylolytic defects."
• Option C. Paget’s disease. Paget's disease of bone is a chronic disorder that results in enlarged and misshapen bones. X-rays of the spine may show areas of increased bone density, presenting as ivory vertebra.
• Option D. Osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is a condition characterized by weakened bones with an increased risk of fractures. Osteoporosis in the spinal x-ray can be seen as fish mouth vertebra.
• Educational Objective:
• According to the x-ray provided in the question, the patient is diagnosed with ankylosing spondylitis. It is chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the spine and sacroiliac joints. It often presents as bamboo spine and dagger sign on X-rays as shown in the question.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Resorption of distal phalanx is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• All of the provided options i.e., scleroderma, hyperparathyroidism and Reiter’s syndrome are associated with resorption of the distal phalanx or terminal tuft of the fingers.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is true about HIV-related arthritis?
options: Cutaneous and mucosal lesions are rare
Hip is the commonest joint involved

explanation.short: Ans. A) Cutaneous and mucosal lesions are rare
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Enthesopathy is common . False. Enthesopathy & Mucocutaneous involvement is rare in HIV-related arthritis.
• Option C. Associated with HLA B27. False. Unlike other forms of arthritis like ankylosing spondylitis, HIV-related arthritis is not typically associated with HLA-B27. It is a seronegative arthropathy with RA factor, ANA and HLA B27 being negative.
• Option D. Hip is the commonest joint involved. False. The joints commonly affected in HIV-related arthritis are typically large joints, with the knee being the most frequently involved, followed by the ankle joint.
• Educational Objective:
• Cutaneous and mucosal lesions are rarely seen in HIV-related arthritis. Enthesopathy is also rare. It is not associated with HLA B27, RA factor or ANA. Knee joint followed by ankle joint are the commonest joint involved.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Hemophiliac arthropathy, which of the following is not seen?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Subchondral bone cyst formation . In hemophilic arthropathy, recurrent bleeding into the joints can lead to the formation of subchondral bone cysts. This occurs as a result of the breakdown of the joint structures and the presence of blood in the joint space, contributing to the destruction of the subchondral bone.
• Option B. Increase in intercondylar distance. Widening of intercondylar notch of femur and squaring of distal end patella is seen in hemophilic arthropathy.
• Option D. Subchondral thinning. Subchondral thinning refers to the reduction in thickness of the bone just beneath the joint cartilage. It is seen in Hemophiliac arthropathy.
• Educational Objective:
• Juxta- articular osteopenia and not osteosclerosis is seen in the radiographic of the hemophilic arthropathy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Arthroscopy is contraindicated in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chronic joint disease. Arthroscopy is generally performed to diagnose and treat various joint conditions. However, in advanced stages of chronic joint diseases, where there is significant joint destruction and deformity, arthroscopy may be effective.
• Option B. Loose Bodies. Arthroscopy is commonly used to address loose bodies within a joint. Loose bodies are fragments of cartilage or bone that may interfere with joint movement and cause pain. Arthroscopy allows for the visualization and removal of these loose bodies, helping to improve joint function and alleviate symptoms.
• Option D. Meniscal tear. Arthroscopy is often used to diagnose and treat meniscal tears. In this procedure, a surgeon can visualize the inside of the joint, identify the torn meniscus, and often repair or trim the damaged tissue. Arthroscopy is a common and effective method for managing meniscal tears.
• Educational Objective:
• Arthroscopy is generally a valuable tool for diagnosing and treating various joint conditions. It is contraindicated in individuals with hemophilia due to the increased risk of uncontrolled bleeding during the procedure.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Charcot’s joint is another name for joint affected by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteoarthritis . Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the breakdown of cartilage in the joints. It is a result of wear and tear on the joints over time. Charcot's joint is not associated with osteoarthritis.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disorder that primarily affects the synovium (lining of the joints), causing inflammation and joint damage. Charcot's joint is not typically associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option D. Ankylosing spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of inflammatory arthritis that primarily affects the spine. It is characterized by inflammation, fusion of joints, and eventual bone formation. Charcot's joint is not commonly associated with ankylosing spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis has distinct features, such as axial involvement and bamboo spine, which are different from the characteristics of Charcot's joint.
• Educational Objective:
• Charcot's joint is most commonly associated with neuropathy, especially in conditions such as diabetic neuropathy or neurosyphilis. It is characterized by progressive degeneration, deformity, and instability of the affected joint due to impaired proprioception and sensory feedback caused by neuropathic conditions.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common cause of neuropathic joints is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Leprosy. Leprosy, also known as Hansen's disease, can lead to neuropathic joint involvement. Leprosy affects peripheral nerves and can cause sensory and motor deficits. Neuropathic joints may develop in leprosy due to the loss of sensation and proprioception, leading to joint destruction and deformities.
• Option B. Tabes dorsalis. Tabes dorsalis is a manifestation of tertiary syphilis that affects the dorsal columns of the spinal cord. It leads to sensory ataxia, impaired proprioception, and can result in Charcot joints. The loss of sensation and coordination contributes to the development of joint damage.
• Option D. Nerve injury. Nerve injury, including traumatic injuries or surgical procedures that damage nerves, can contribute to the development of neuropathic joints. However, diabetes is more commonly associated with neuropathic joint than direct nerve injury.
• Educational Objective:
• While various conditions can lead to neuropathic joint (Charcot joint), diabetes is recognized as the most common cause, primarily due to the widespread prevalence of diabetic neuropathy and its impact on sensory and motor function in peripheral nerves.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A lady presents with right knee swelling, aspiration was done in which CPPD crystals were obtained. Next best investigation is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. ANA. ANA testing is often used in the evaluation of autoimmune disorders, such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) or other connective tissue diseases. However, it may not be the first-line investigation in the context of pseudogout, where CPPD crystals have already been identified.
• Option B. RF. Rheumatoid factor (RF) is a blood test commonly used to help diagnose rheumatoid arthritis. This is not the investigation used in pseudogout.
• Option C. CPK. CPK is an enzyme that can be elevated in various conditions, including muscle damage. While CPK may be relevant in specific clinical scenarios, it is not a routine investigation for evaluating pseudogout.
• Educational Objective:
• Knee swelling and presence of CPPD crystals in aspirate are suggestive of presence of pseudogout in the patient. Since pseudogout is associated with hypothyroidism most commonly, it is best advised to investigate for TSH levels in the patient.

subject: Orthopedics
question: X-ray of a young man shows heterotopic calcification around bilateral knee joints. Next investigation would be:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Serum Calcium , Option C. Serum PTH, and Option D. Serum alkaline phosphatase are normal in level in tumoral calcinosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Heterotopic calcification refers to the abnormal deposition of calcium in soft tissues outside the skeletal system. It can occur in various conditions, and assessing the serum levels of phosphate is important for the evaluation of metabolic and hormonal factors influencing calcium homeostasis. The given complaint of presence of the calcification in bilateral knees is characteristic of tumor calcinosis. In this rare condition, the serum calcium, PTH and ALP levels remains normal. Since, hyperphosphatemia is the primary defect in this condition, it is important to investigate the serum phosphate levels to rule out the condition.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True statement regarding Mseleni joint disease is:
options: Shoulder, elbow wrist involvement is characteristic
Endemic to northern Kwazulu Natal area in South Africa

explanation.short: Ans. D) Endemic to northern Kwazulu Natal area in South Africa
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Shoulder, elbow wrist involvement is characteristic. This statement is incorrect. While Mseleni joint disease does involve joints, the specific involvement of shoulder, elbow, and wrist is not characteristic.
• Option B. Elderly males are commonly involved. This statement is incorrect. Mseleni joint disease affects females more commonly than males.
• Option C. Height is unaffected . This statement is not specific to Mseleni joint disease. The condition primarily causes decreased height of the affected individual i.e., there is short stature of the patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Mseleni joint disease is endemic to the northern Kwazulu Natal area in South Africa. The condition is characterized by chronic joint pain and swelling, typically involving large joints such as the hip. It has been associated with the consumption of contaminated maize containing mycotoxins produced by fungi. It is more common in females. It leads to severe short stature.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The following is the commonest cause of loose body in knee joint:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteochondral fracture . An osteochondral fracture involves damage to both the cartilage and the underlying bone in a joint.
• Option C. Synovial chondromatosis. Synovial chondromatosis is a benign condition where cartilaginous nodules or loose bodies develop within the synovial membrane of a joint.
• Option D. Osteochondritis dissecans . Osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) is a joint condition where a small segment of bone and its overlying articular cartilage lose blood supply.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the gradual breakdown of cartilage in the joints and subsequent changes in the underlying bone. It is the most common cause of loose body in knee joint in elderly patients and overall.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Indications of arthroplasty are: Osteoarthritis Rheumatoid arthritis Ankylosing spondylitis Gout Fracture neck femur

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Arthroplasty can be done for any disease any joint except active infection. It can be done in Osteoarthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Ankylosing spondylosis, Gout and fracture neck femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bechterew’s Disease is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rheumatoid Arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the joints. It involves inflammation of the synovium, which can lead to joint damage and deformities. Unlike Bechterew's Disease (AS), RA typically affects smaller joints, such as those in the hands and feet.
• Option C. Paget’s disease . Paget's disease of bone, also known as osteitis deformans is a chronic disorder that results in enlarged and deformed bones. It typically affects the spine, pelvis, long bones, and skull.
