• Option A. Stimulates bone marrow. Vitamin D does not directly stimulate bone marrow. The primary role of vitamin D is related to calcium metabolism, bone health, and other physiological processes. It helps regulate calcium absorption in the intestines, calcium reabsorption in the kidneys, and maintains calcium and phosphate levels in the blood.
• Option B. Vitamin D actually has the opposite effect on calcium. It promotes the absorption of calcium from the intestines, reabsorption of calcium in the kidneys, and overall helps to maintain adequate levels of calcium in the blood. Therefore, it does not increase calcium loss.
• Educational Objective:
• Action of vitamin D is it stimulates absorption of calcium and stimulation of osteoclasts.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Salt pepper skull is a feature of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Paget’s disease. Paget's disease is a chronic bone disorder characterized by excessive and abnormal bone remodeling. It is characterized by cotton wool skull appearance.
• Option B. Eosinophilic granuloma . Eosinophilic granuloma is a localized form of Langerhans cell histiocytosis, a disorder involving the proliferation of certain white blood cells. It is characterized by Geographical lesion (Bevelled margins) in the skull.
• Option D. Multiple Myeloma . Multiple myeloma is a cancer of plasma cells in the bone marrow. The skull gives a punched out appearance in this condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Salt and pepper skull is a radiographic feature of primary hyperparathyroidism.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Brown tumor is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Hypoparathyroidism. Hypoparathyroidism is characterized by low levels of parathyroid hormone (PTH), leading to decreased calcium levels in the blood. In hypoparathyroidism, the bone resorption is typically reduced, and brown tumors are not a feature of this condition.
• Option C. Hypothyroidism . Hypoparathyroidism is not associated with the development of brown tumors. Brown tumors are more commonly linked to hyperparathyroidism.
• Option D. Hyperthyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism is characterized by overactivity of the parathyroid glands, leading to increased production of parathyroid hormone (PTH).
• Educational Objective:
• Brown tumors are not actual tumors but rather focal areas of bone resorption that can occur in the setting of hyperparathyroidism.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Hyperphosphatemia is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Vitamin D intoxication . Vitamin D intoxication can produce hyperphosphatemia as a result of excessive gastrointestinal absorption and increased renal reabsorption.
• Option B. Renal failure . Renal failure, especially in the context of chronic kidney disease (CKD), is a well-known cause of hyperphosphatemia. In normal kidney function, phosphorus is excreted in the urine, but in renal failure, there is a decreased ability to eliminate phosphorus, leading to its accumulation in the blood.
• Option C. Tumor Lysis syndrome. Tumor lysis syndrome is a medical emergency that can occur after the treatment of certain cancers, especially those with high cell turnover rates. The rapid breakdown of cancer cells releases intracellular contents into the bloodstream, including phosphorus.
• Educational Objective:
• Hyperphosphatemia is abnormally high serum phosphate levels—can result from increased phosphate intake, decreased phosphate excretion, or a disorder that shifts intracellular phosphate to extracellular space. Hyperphosphatemia is seen in Vitamin D intoxication, renal failure and tumor lysis syndrome.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Pseudofracture can be seen in the following conditions:

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hereditary Hypophosphatasia. Hereditary hypophosphatasia is a genetic disorder characterized by low levels of alkaline phosphatase, leading to impaired bone mineralization. Pseudofractures can be observed in individuals with hypophosphatasia due to the inadequate mineralization of bone, which may result in the appearance of radiolucent lines on X-rays.
• Option B. Paget’s Disease . Paget's disease of bone is a condition characterized by abnormal bone remodeling, leading to enlarged and deformed bones. Pseudofractures can be associated with Paget's disease, occurring at sites of increased bone turnover and altered bone architecture.
• Option C. Fibrous dysplasia . Fibrous dysplasia is a bone disorder where normal bone is replaced by fibrous tissue. Pseudofractures can be seen in fibrous dysplasia due to the weakened bone structure and altered bone matrix.
• Educational Objective
• Milkman's/Increment fractures also known as looser's zones or osteoid zones are pseudofracture, seen at the sites of mechanical stress or nutrient vessel entry (pulsation). Pseudofractures can be seen in hereditary hypophosphatasia, paget’s disease and fibrous dysplasia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Looser’s zone are seen in which of the following conditions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteoporosis. Looser zones are not typically associated with osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is characterized by a reduction in bone density and quality, leading to an increased risk of fractures.
• Option C. Rickets. Rickets is a disorder primarily affecting children, characterized by impaired mineralization of growing bones.
• Option D. Scurvy. Scurvy is a nutritional deficiency disorder caused by a lack of vitamin C. While scurvy can lead to various bone and connective tissue abnormalities, it is not typically associated with the formation of Looser zones on X-rays.
• Educational Objective:
• Milkman’s/increment fractures also known as looser’s zones or osteoid zones are pseudofractures, seen at the site of mechanical stress or nutrient vessel entry. Looser’s zone can be seen commonly in osteomalacia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Looser’s zone/Pseudofractures are commonly seen in the following areas except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Scapula. Looser zones can be seen in the scapula. These radiolucent lines may appear in various bones as a result of conditions like osteomalacia or rickets.
• Option B. Ribs. Looser zones are commonly seen in the ribs. The ribs, being part of the skeletal system, can show these pseudofractures in conditions associated with impaired bone mineralization.
• Option C. Pelvis . Looser zones can be observed in the pelvis. The pelvic bones, like other weight-bearing bones, may exhibit pseudofractures in conditions such as osteomalacia or rickets.
• Educational Objective:
• The common locations for pseudofractures are Femoral Neck, Pubic Rami, Axillary edge of scapula immediately below the glenoid, Scapula lateral and superior border, Ribs and clavicle and Pelvis. It is not seen in Radius.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Milkman’s fracture is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Fractures of clavicle in children. Clavicle fractures are relatively common in children, often occurring due to falls or trauma. The clavicle is a bone that connects the shoulder to the chest, and fractures can happen at various points along its length.
• Option C. Fracture Humerus . Fractures of the humerus, the long bone of the upper arm, can occur in various locations and patterns. These fractures can happen due to trauma, falls, or other injuries.
• Option D. Fracture first metacarpal. A fracture of the first metacarpal, often referred to as a "Bennett's fracture" or "Rolando fracture," involves the base of the thumb. It is a specific type of fracture that can occur in the first metacarpal bone.
• Educational Objective:
• Milkman’s fracture is the pseudofracture in adults. These are incomplete stress fractures that have healed by callus consisting of osteoid tissue lacking calcium.

subject: Orthopedics
question: “Rugger Jersey spine” is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fluorosis. Fluorosis is a condition caused by excessive intake of fluoride, leading to changes in bone and teeth. However, the "Rugger Jersey spine" is not typically associated with fluorosis.
• Option B. Achondroplasia. Achondroplasia is a genetic disorder that affects bone growth, resulting in dwarfism. The "Rugger Jersey spine" is not a characteristic finding in achondroplasia.
• Option D. Marfan’s syndrome. Marfan's syndrome is a genetic disorder that affects the connective tissue, leading to various skeletal, cardiovascular, and ocular abnormalities. The "Rugger Jersey spine" is not typically associated with Marfan's syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• "Rugger Jersey spine" is a radiological description of the appearance of the spine, particularly the vertebral bodies, seen on imaging studies. It is seen in renal osteodystrphy in which alternate bands of osteosclerosis and radiolucency can be seen.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Rugger Jersey spine in Chronic Renal Failure is due to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteomalacia. Osteomalacia is a condition characterized by softening of the bones due to impaired mineralization. It is not typically associated with the "Rugger Jersey spine" appearance.
• Option B. Trauma. Trauma alone is not a common cause of the "Rugger Jersey spine" appearance. This radiological finding is more related to metabolic and mineral disorders affecting bone density.
• Option D. Aluminium osteodystrophy. Aluminium osteodystrophy is a form of renal osteodystrophy associated with chronic kidney disease. Exposure to high levels of aluminum in patients with impaired kidney function can lead to bone abnormalities.
• Educational Objective
• The "Rugger Jersey spine" appearance seen in chronic renal failure (CRF) is primarily due to increased bone density, particularly in the vertebral end plates. It is due to hyperparathyroidism.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Wimberger ring sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Syphilis. Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum. In congenital syphilis, Wimberger corner sign is seen which refers to the localized bilateral metaphyseal destruction of the medial proximal tibia.
• Option C. Paget’s disease . Paget's disease of bone is a chronic disorder characterized by abnormal bone remodeling. The Wimberger ring sign is not commonly associated with Paget's disease.
• Option D. Hemophilia . Hemophilia is a genetic disorder that affects blood clotting, but it is not typically associated with the Wimberger ring sign.
• Educational Objective:
• Wimberger’s ring sign is a radiographic feature of scurvy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Vitamin C deficiency leads to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Defective mineralization. Vitamin C is essential for the synthesis of collagen, a key component of bones and connective tissues. Deficiency of vitamin C can impair collagen formation, which, in turn, may affect mineralization of the bone matrix. This can lead to impaired bone density and strength.
• Option C. Normal collagen and bone matrix. Vitamin C is crucial for the synthesis of collagen, and collagen is a fundamental component of bone matrix. Therefore, a deficiency in vitamin C can indeed lead to abnormal collagen and bone matrix formation.
• Option D. X-ray shows normal evidence. X-rays may reveal abnormalities associated with vitamin C deficiency, such as changes in bone density, characteristic findings in scurvy, or alterations in the growth plates. Therefore, X-ray evidence may not be normal in the presence of vitamin C deficiency.
• Educational Objective
• Vitamin C deficiency primarily affects collagen synthesis, including the collagen found in bone matrix, leading to defective osteoid formation. This can result in various skeletal abnormalities, such as impaired mineralization and weakened bone structure.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A young patient presents with enlargement of costochondral junction and with the white line of Frankel at the metaphysis. The diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rickets. Rickets is a condition primarily caused by a deficiency of vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate. It affects the mineralization of bones and can lead to deformities. In the growing skeleton, the deficiency of normal mineralization is most evident at metaphyseal zones of provisional calcification where there is an excess of non-mineralized osteoid resulting in growth plate widening and abnormal configuration of the metaphysis in the form of fraying (indistinct margins of the metaphysis), splaying (wide metaphyseal ends), cupping (concave metaphysis), and pseudofracture on the compression side of bone. They are most prominent at the bones where growth is greatest i.e., distal femur, proximal tibia, wrist especially the ulna, and anterior rib ends (rachitic rosary).
• Option C. Hyperparathyroidism . Hyperparathyroidism involves an overactive parathyroid gland leading to excessive levels of parathyroid hormone (PTH). This can result in increased calcium reabsorption from bones, causing bone resorption. Radiographically, there is subperiosteal bone resorption seen in the 2 nd and 3 rd phalanges, tibia, femur and humerus; subchondral resorption in lateral end clavicle, pubic symphysis and SI joints; subligamentous resorption in ischial tuberosity, trochanters, calcaneus and clavicle, rugger jersey spine, osteopenia, brown tumors, salt-pepper skull are seen.
• Option D. Osteomalacia . Osteomalacia is a condition characterized by softening of the bones due to inadequate mineralization. It is often caused by a deficiency of vitamin D, resulting in impaired calcium and phosphate metabolism. Radiogrphically, it shows osteporotic-like pattern of demineralization, blurred trabeculae, looser’s zone.
• Educational Objective:
• Enlargement of costochondral junction (rosary) and white line of Frankel is seen in scurvy. Scurvy is caused by vitamin C deficiency It affects collagen synthesis and leads to: White line of Frankel Trümmerfeld zone (lucent band below Frankel line) Pelkan spurs Wimberger’s sign Subperiosteal hemorrhages White line of Frankel = Scurvy Cupping/fraying/widened epiphysis = Rickets.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 75-year-female has chronic backache. X-ray spine is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/27/screenshot-2024-02-27-173049.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pott’s spine. Pott's spine, or spinal tuberculosis, can cause chronic backache, particularly in the elderly. It may be associated with vertebral collapse and destruction seen on X-rays. Radiographic manifestations of tuberculous spondylitis include intraosseous and paraspinal abscess formation, subligamentous spread of infection, vertebral body destruction and collapse, and extension into the spinal epidural space.
• Option C. Spondylolisthesis. Spondylolisthesis involves the displacement of one vertebra over another. It can result in chronic back pain, and X-rays may show the abnormal alignment of the vertebrae.
• Option D. Spondylodiscitis. Spondylodiscitis refers to an infection of the intervertebral disc and adjacent vertebral bodies. It can cause chronic back pain, and X-rays may show disc space narrowing and vertebral destruction. MRI is the imaging modality of choice that shows faint bone marrow edema, paravertebral soft tissue change, acute Schmorl’s node, disc extrusion, malignancy, or acute endplate injury.
• Educational Objective
• The given image shows codfish vertebra, which is diagnostic of osteoporosis. Codfish vertebrae are seen due to compression fracture of the spine.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 70-year-old lady got compression fracture of L1 vertebra. There is no neurological deficit to this patient. What advise will you give for this patient?
options: Go for screw fixation of L1 vertebra

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Do MRI scan. An MRI scan is not typically the first-line investigation for uncomplicated vertebral compression fractures. MRI is indicated in osteoporotic collapse in case of neural deficit.
• Option C. Go for screw fixation of L1 Vertebra. Surgical intervention, such as screw fixation, is generally reserved for cases with severe instability, neurological deficits, or failure of conservative management. Decompression and fixation is done for unstable spine and compromised spinal canal.
• Option D. To take dexamethasone. Dexamethasone, a corticosteroid, is not typically used as the first-line treatment for uncomplicated vertebral compression fractures. Steroids are given in spinal injuries to reduce edema associated with the trauma.
• Educational Objective:
• Vitamin D is crucial for bone health and calcium absorption. In the context of a compression fracture, ensuring adequate vitamin D levels is important for overall bone strength and healing. Therefore, vitamin D supplementation may be recommended to support bone health.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which bisphosphonate is approved for the treatment of osteoporosis and is given on yearly basis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alendronate. Alendronate is a bisphosphonate commonly used for the treatment of osteoporosis. However, the standard dosing regimen for alendronate is usually once weekly.
• Option C. Risedronate. Risedronate is another bisphosphonate used for the treatment of osteoporosis. The standard dosing regimen for risedronate is often once weekly or once monthly.
• Option D. Ibandronate. Ibandronate is available in both oral and intravenous forms. It is given in a monthly dose.
• Educational Objective:
• Bisphosphonate which is approved for the treatment of osteoporosis and is given on yearly basis is Zolendronate. It increases the risk of Acute Kidney Injury. It is given on yearly basis and the patient should be properly hydrated before its administration. Bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast to decrease the bone resorption.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All the following are used in osteoporosis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Denosumab. Denosumab is a medication used to treat osteoporosis by inhibiting bone resorption.
• Option B. Strontium. Strontium ranelate is another option used for the treatment of osteoporosis.
• Option C. PTH. Parathyroid hormone (PTH), specifically teriparatide, is also used in the management of osteoporosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Out of the given options, the drug which is not used in osteoporosis is Milnacipran. It is primarily used as an antidepressant and for the management of fibromyalgia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Osteoporosis, which of these is seen?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Normal calcium, normal ALP
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Normal calcium, decreased ALP. This combination is not typical for osteoporosis. In osteoporosis, calcium levels are usually normal or slightly decreased, and ALP levels are typically normal.
• Option B. Decreased Calcium, increased ALP. This combination is not characteristic of osteoporosis. Osteoporosis itself is not associated with a significant decrease in calcium levels. Additionally, ALP levels are usually normal in osteoporosis.
• Option D. Decreased Calcium, decreased ALP . This combination is not commonly seen in osteoporosis. While there may be a slight decrease in calcium levels in osteoporosis, the ALP levels typically remain normal.
• Educational Objective:
• In osteoporosis, the typical laboratory findings are generally within the normal range or may show a slight decrease in calcium levels. However, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) levels are usually normal.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Z-score measures the bone mineral density compared to:
options: Age, race and sex matched individuals

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Race and sex matched individuals. The Z score is more comprehensive than just comparing to race and sex. It includes age as well because bone mineral density can vary with age. Therefore, the Z score accounts for age, race, and sex.
• Option C. Sex matched individuals . While sex is an important factor in bone density, the Z score goes beyond comparing only with individuals of the same sex. It also considers age and race to provide a more accurate assessment of bone mineral density.
• Educational Objective:
• Z score measures the bone mineral density is compared to age, race and sex matched individuals. Z score compares the patients BMD with that of adult of the same race, age and sex. It indicates possible secondary osteoporosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The bodies of spine are biconcave and are called “codfish spine”. This is most commonly seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Scurvy. Scurvy is a condition caused by vitamin C deficiency. In scurvy, the collagen synthesis is impaired, leading to weakened connective tissues. This can result in various musculoskeletal manifestations, including bone abnormalities. However, the classic biconcave appearance of the vertebral bodies, are not typically associated with scurvy. The spine is rarely affected in scurvy.
• Option C. Fluorosis. Fluorosis is a condition caused by excessive fluoride intake, leading to skeletal and dental abnormalities. However, the classic biconcave appearance of the vertebral bodies, are not associated with fluorosis. Vertebral osteophytosis with soft-tissue calcification and ossification is common but non-specific feature of fluorosis. This sign can also be seen in diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis, ankylosing spondylitis, psoriasis, acromegaly, neuropathy.
• Option D. Hyperparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism is a condition where there is an excess of parathyroid hormone, leading to increased calcium levels in the blood. Severe and prolonged hyperparathyroidism can cause bone resorption and deformities. It is not associated with the classic biconcave appearance of the vertebral bodies. The spine in this condition gives a ruggery jersey sign which is the osteosclerosis of the end plates and radiolucency of the middle of the vertebral body.
• Educational Objective:
• The biconcave appearance of vertebral bodies, known as "codfish spine," is most commonly associated with osteomalacia, which is caused by vitamin D deficiency and results in inadequate mineralization of the bone matrix.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Gold standard for diagnosis of osteoporosis:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Single beam densitometry. Single Beam Densitometry is an older and less accurate method compared to DEXA. It measures bone density using a single X-ray beam, but it is not as precise as DEXA in assessing bone mineral density.
• Option C. Quantitative computed tomography. QCT is another imaging technique that measures bone mineral density using computed tomography scans. While QCT provides detailed three-dimensional images of bone density, it is not as commonly used as DEXA for routine osteoporosis diagnosis.
• Option D. Bone histomorphometry. Bone histomorphometry involves the microscopic examination of bone tissue obtained through a bone biopsy. It provides detailed information about bone structure and mineralization. However, bone histomorphometry is an invasive procedure and is not typically used as a routine diagnostic tool for osteoporosis.
• Educational Objective:
• DEXA remains the gold standard for the diagnosis of osteoporosis in routine clinical practice due to its precision, non-invasiveness, and widespread availability.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common site of osteoporosis:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Humerus. While osteoporosis can affect various bones in the body, fractures in the humerus (arm bone) are not as common as fractures in other areas such as the spine, hip, or wrist.
• Option C. Scapula. The scapula (shoulder blade) is not a typical site for osteoporosis-related fractures. Osteoporotic fractures more commonly occur in weight-bearing bones such as the spine and hip.
• Option D. Flat bone. Flat bones, such as the ribs and certain bones in the skull, are not as commonly affected by osteoporosis-related fractures.
• Educational Objective:
• The vertebrae, particularly the thoracic and lumbar spine, are commonly affected by osteoporosis-related fractures. These fractures can lead to a loss of height, spinal deformities and back pain. Vertebral fractures are often associated with osteoporosis, especially in older individuals.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Denosumab – a monoclonal antibody against RANKL receptor, is used in treatment of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis. Denosumab is not typically used as a primary treatment for rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis involves inflammation of the joints and is usually managed with disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs) like methotrexate, and biological agents targeting inflammatory pathways.
• Option C. Osteoarthritis . Denosumab is not a primary treatment for osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease, and its management involves pain relief, lifestyle modifications, and sometimes joint replacement surgery in severe cases.
• Option D. SLE. Denosumab is not a standard treatment for systemic lupus erythematosus. SLE is an autoimmune disease, and its management typically involves immunosuppressive medications, corticosteroids, and other targeted therapies depending on the specific manifestations.
• Educational Objective
• Denosumab is approved for the treatment of osteoporosis in postmenopausal women and in men at increased risk of fractures. It helps to increase bone density by inhibiting bone resorption, making it a valuable option for the management of osteoporosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The most common manifestation of osteoporosis is:
options: Asymptomatic, detected incidentally by low serum calcium

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Asymptomatic, detected incidentally by low serum calcium. Osteoporosis can be asymptomatic in its early stages, and individuals may not experience noticeable symptoms. It is not typically detected by low serum calcium levels. Instead, diagnosis is often based on bone mineral density (BMD) tests like dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scans, which measure bone density and assess the risk of fractures. The serum calcium level is normal in cases of osteoporosis.
• Option C. Bowing of legs . Bowing of the legs is not a common manifestation of osteoporosis. Bowing of the legs is more commonly associated with conditions like rickets or osteomalacia, which involve a softening of the bones due to vitamin D deficiency.
• Option D. Loss of weight. Weight loss is not a direct manifestation of osteoporosis. Instead, the patient or their family members may have noticed a gradual decrease in height of the patient by few cms.
• Educational Objective
• Osteoporosis often results in decreased bone density and strength. This can lead to compression fractures in the vertebrae of the spine, especially in the thoracic and lumbar regions.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Increased bone density with hyperostosis seen in skeletal fluorosis is likely to occur when fluorine concentration in drinking H 2 O is above:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 6ppm. T his is a lower concentration of fluoride in drinking water. At this level, the risk of skeletal fluorosis is relatively low, and hyperostosis (increased bone density) is less likely to occur.
• Option C. 15ppm . With a fluoride concentration of 15 ppm, the risk of developing skeletal fluorosis, including hyperostosis, becomes more significant. Prolonged exposure to such high levels of fluoride in drinking water can lead to increased bone density and other skeletal abnormalities.
• Option D. 20ppm. This is an even higher concentration of fluoride in drinking water.
• Educational Objective:
• In some parts of India and Africa, where fluorine concentration in the drinking water maybe above 10 ppm, chronic fluorine intoxication (fluorosis) is endemic and results in skeletal anomalies.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Increased bone density occurs in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cushing syndrome. Cushing syndrome is a disorder characterized by prolonged exposure to high levels of cortisol, a hormone produced by the adrenal glands. There is abnormal bone turnover, decreased bone density, and increased risk of fracture due to excessive glucocorticoids.
• Option B. Hypoparathroidism. Hypoparathyroidism is a condition where the parathyroid glands produce insufficient parathyroid hormone (PTH), leading to low calcium levels in the blood. In hypoparathyroidism, bone density may be normal or slightly decreased, but it is not typically associated with increased bone density.
• Option D. Hyperthyroidism. Hyperthyroidism is a condition where the thyroid gland produces an excess of thyroid hormones. Hyperthyroidism is associated with accelerated bone remodelling, reduced bone density, osteoporosis and increased fracture rate.
• Educational Objective:
• Fluorosis is caused by excessive accumulation of fluoride, usually from sources like drinking water. It is characterized by increased bone density and changes in bone structure.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is the diagnostic radiological finding in skeletal fluorosis?
options: Ossification of ligaments of knee joint

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sclerosis of sacroiliac joint. Sclerosis refers to abnormal hardening or thickening of tissues.
• Option C. Osteosclerosis of vertebral body. Osteosclerosis in the vertebral bodies refers to the increased density or hardening of the bones in the spine.
• Option D. Ossification of ligaments of knee joint. In skeletal fluorosis, there can be abnormal calcification and ossification of ligaments around joints, including the knee joint.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnostic radiological finding in skeletal fluorosis is Interosseous membrane ossification. Osteosclerosis, osteophytosis and ossification of ligamentous and fascial attachments is characteristic of fluorosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Caffey’s disease is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Renal osteodystrophy. Renal osteodystrophy refers to bone changes that occur in individuals with chronic kidney disease. It is not synonymous with Caffey's disease.
• Option C. Osteomyelitis of jaw in children . While Caffey's disease can involve the jaw, it is not caused by bacterial infection, which is a characteristic feature of osteomyelitis.
• Option D. Chronic osteomyelitis in children. Caffey's disease is not a form of chronic osteomyelitis. Chronic osteomyelitis typically involves persistent infection and inflammation of the bone.
• Educational Objective:
• Caffey's disease, or Infantile Cortical Hyperostosis (ICH), is characterized by inflammation of the periosteum (the outer layer of bone) leading to cortical bone thickening. Affected infants may experience swelling and tenderness of the affected bones, often the jaw, clavicles, and other long bones.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Caffey’s disease occurs in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Above 5 years. Caffey's disease is not commonly seen above the age of 5 years. It is considered an infantile disorder, and its onset is usually within the first few months of life.
• Option C. Above 10-20 years. Caffey's disease does not typically occur in individuals above the age of 5, let alone 10-20 years. The condition is specific to infancy.
• Option D. 20-40 years. Caffey's disease is not observed in individuals aged 20-40 years .
• Educational Objective:
• The onset of disease occurs before 6th month of life with resolution by 3 years of age.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A bald child with swollen abdomen, hyperosseous bones has:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hypervitaminosis C. Hypervitaminosis C, or excess vitamin C, is not associated with hyperosseous bones. Instead, it is commonly associated with scurvy, a condition characterized by weakness, swollen and bleeding gums, and joint pain.
• Option C. Down's syndrome . Down's syndrome is a genetic condition caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21. It is not typically associated with hyperosseous bones or a swollen abdomen.
• Option D. Tuberous Sclerosis. Tuberous sclerosis is a genetic disorder that can affect various organs, including the skin, brain, and kidneys.
• Educational Objective:
• A bald child with swollen abdomen, hyperosseous bones has hypervitaminosis D. 1,25 (OH) Vit D has an anti-proliferative effect on several cell types, including keratinocytes, breast cancer cells, and prostate cancer cells.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Hypervitaminosis of which of the following will cause bony abnormalities? Vit. A Vit. D Vit. C Vit. E Vit. K

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Excessive vitamin A intake can lead to a condition known as hypervitaminosis A. While it can cause various symptoms, such as nausea, headache, and dizziness, bony abnormalities are not a prominent feature. Hypervitaminosis D can lead to an increased absorption of calcium from the intestines, resulting in hypercalcemia. Prolonged hypercalcemia can cause bony abnormalities, such as hyperostosis and calcification of soft tissues.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Osteosclerosis is a feature of which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rickets . Rickets is a condition characterized by soft and weakened bones in children due to a deficiency of vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate. Osteosclerosis is not a feature of rickets; instead, rickets is associated with decreased bone density.
• Option B. Hyperthyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism is a condition where the parathyroid glands produce excessive parathyroid hormone (PTH), leading to increased levels of calcium in the blood. While hyperparathyroidism can cause bone resorption and result in weakened bones, osteosclerosis is not a prominent feature.
• Option D. Osteogenesis imperfect. Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic disorder characterized by brittle bones and a tendency to fracture easily. Osteosclerosis is not a typical feature of osteogenesis imperfect
• Educational Objective:
• Paget's disease of bone is a chronic disorder characterized by abnormal bone remodeling. Osteosclerosis is a feature of Paget's disease, along with other changes such as bone enlargement, deformities, and increased risk of fractures.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Paget’s disease commonly develops in which age group?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 1 st decade. Paget's disease is extremely rare in the 1st decade of life. The condition is more commonly seen in older individuals, and its prevalence increases with age.
• Option B. 3 rd decade. Paget's disease is still uncommon in the 3rd decade. Most cases are diagnosed in individuals who are older than 40 years.
• Option C. 5 th decade. Paget's disease is more commonly diagnosed in the 5th decade of life. Individuals in their 50s are more likely to exhibit symptoms and signs of Paget's disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Paget’s disease commonly develops in 7 th decade. It affects >50years of age.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Blades of grass lesion is found in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Thalassemia. Thalassemia is a genetic blood disorder and is not typically associated with the "blades of grass" lesion in bone.
• Option C. Osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is characterized by reduced bone density and an increased risk of fractures, but it does not present with the "blades of grass" lesion.
• Option D. Carcinoma prostate. Prostate cancer can metastasize to the bones, causing lytic lesions, but the "blades of grass" appearance is not a typical radiological finding associated with prostate cancer metastasis.
• Educational Objective:
• Blades of grass lesion is found in Paget’s Disease. Long bone X-ray shows deformity, enlargement or expansion of bone with cortical thickening coarsening of trabecular markings with sclerotic changes.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Picture frame vertebra is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteopetrosis. Osteopetrosis, also known as marble bone disease or Albers-Schönberg disease, is a group of rare genetic disorders characterized by impaired bone resorption, leading to increased bone density. Radiographically, it presents with “bone within a bone” pattern within the pelvis and long bones and vertebrae, as well as the characteristic “sandwich vertebrae” appearance in the spine.
• Option C. Osteoporosis. X-ray of osteoporossis spine shows codfish vertebra.
• Option D. Ankylosing spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of inflammatory arthritis primarily affecting the spine. Diffuse syndesmophytic ankylosis give a "bamboo spine" appearance.Linear ossification along the central spine representing interspinous ligament ossification can give a "dagger spine" appearance on frontal radiographs.
• Educational Objective:
• Picture frame vertebra is seen in Paget’s disease.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Disproportionate increase in Alkaline phosphatase levels are seen in which of the following condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Osteoporosis . Alkaline Phosphatase is the marker of bone formation. It is normal in Osteoporosis.
• Option B . Multiple myeloma . All parameters of Calcium metabolism are raised in Multiple Myeloma except alkaline phosphatase levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Disproportionate increase in Alkaline phosphatase levels are seen in Paget’s disease.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Paget’s disease after 10 years develops into:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Fibrous cortical defect. Fibrous cortical defect is a benign bone lesion that is not typically associated with Paget's disease progression. These defects are usually small, non-cancerous areas of bone.
• Option C. Osteoid osteoma. Osteoid osteoma is a benign bone tumor characterized by the formation of osteoid tissue. It is not a typical progression of Paget's disease. These tumors are usually small and can cause localized pain.
• Option D. Ankylosing Spondylitis. Ankylosing spondylitis is a separate inflammatory arthritis condition primarily affecting the spine. It is not a typical progression of Paget's disease.
• Educational Objective
• Paget’s disease after 10 years develops into osteosarcoma in < 1% cases and has poorest prognosis. Other malignancies are benign GCT or chondrosarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Pain in Paget’s disease is relieved best by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Simple analgesics. Simple analgesics, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like ibuprofen, may provide relief from mild to moderate pain associated with Paget's disease. They can help reduce inflammation and alleviate discomfort.
• Option B. Narcotic analgesics. Narcotic analgesics, or opioids, may be considered for more severe pain that is not adequately controlled by simple analgesics.
• Option C. Radiations . Radiation therapy may be considered in cases where localized pain is not responsive to medications.
• Educational Objective:
• Bisphosphonates are drug of choice and calcitonin is used to relieve pain.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Hooked vertebrae seen in:

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Achondroplasis. Achondroplasia is a genetic disorder that primarily affects bone growth, leading to dwarfism. Hooked vertebrae is a feature of achondroplasia.
• Option B. Osteogenesis Imperfecta. Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is a genetic disorder characterized by brittle bones that are prone to fractures. Hooked vertebrae is a feature of Osteogenesis Imperfecta.
• Option C. Congential Hypothyroidism. Congenital hypothyroidism refers to an underactive thyroid gland present from birth. It can have effects on bone development and growth.
• Educational Objective
• Hooked vertebra can be seen in Achondrplasis, Ostegenesis Imperfecta and Congential hypothyroidism.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The following is false about Achondroplasia:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Autosomal dominant. T his statement is true. Achondroplasia is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a person with achondroplasia has a 50% chance of passing the condition on to their offspring.
• Option C. Due to gene mutation. This statement is true. Achondroplasia is caused by a mutation in the FGFR3 gene, which leads to abnormal bone growth.
• Option D. Shortening of limbs present. This statement is true. One of the hallmark features of achondroplasia is short stature, particularly in the limbs. The trunk is usually of normal length, while the arms and legs are shorter than average.
• Educational Objective:
• This given statement is false. Mental retardation is not a characteristic feature of achondroplasia. Individuals with achondroplasia typically have normal intelligence.

subject: Orthopedics
question: “Trident hand” is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Mucopolysaccharidosis. Mucopolysaccharidoses are a group of metabolic disorders characterized by the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans. This condition involves Carpal tunnel syndrome which restricts the hand mobility and function.
• Option C. Diaphyseal aclasis. Hereditary multiple exostoses/osteochondromas , also known as diaphyseal aclasis , osteochondromatosis , or simply multiple osteochondromas , is an autosomal dominant condition, characterized by the development of multiple osteochondromas. This involves formation of lesions in the bones. There is broadening of the shaft of the end of long bones.
• Option D. Cleidocranial Dysostosis. Cleidocranial dysostosis is a genetic disorder that affects the development of bones, particularly the skull and collar bones. This involves pes planus, genu valgum, scoliosis and osteoporosis.
• Educational Objective
• Trident Hand is a characteristic finding in some genetic or developmental disorders like Achondroplasia where there is a separation between the middle and ring fingers.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Absent lateral 1/3rd of clavicle is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hyperparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism is characterized by overactivity of the parathyroid glands, leading to increased levels of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and elevated calcium levels. It is not typically associated with absent lateral 1/3rd of the clavicle.
• Option B. Turner’s syndrome. Turner's syndrome is a genetic condition that affects females and is characterized by the absence or underdevelopment of the X chromosome. In some cases, there can be skeletal abnormalities, but the absent lateral 1/3rd of the clavicle is not a classic feature.
• Option C. Fibrous dysplasia. Fibrous dysplasia is a bone disorder where normal bone is replaced with fibrous tissue. It is not typically associated with absent lateral 1/3rd of the clavicle.
• Educational Objective:
• Absent lateral 1/3rd of clavicle is seen in Cleidocranial dysostosis. Cleidocranial dysostosis is a genetic disorder that affects the development of bones, particularly the skull and collarbones (clavicles). In individuals with cleidocranial dysostosis, the clavicles may be underdeveloped or absent, contributing to the characteristic appearance.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Osteogenesis imperfecta, true is:
options: Marble bone disease is another name of it
Hyperlaxity of ligaments with hypermobile joints

explanation.short: Ans. B) Hyperlaxity of ligaments with hypermobile joints
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Marble bone disease is another name of it. Marble bone disease is a term more commonly associated with osteopetrosis, which is a different genetic disorder characterized by increased bone density. Osteogenesis imperfecta is often referred to as "brittle bone disease."
• Option C. Cranial nerve compression may occur. This statement is not correct as in osteogenesis imperfecta, cranial nerve compression may not occur. The auditory nerve can be compressed, leading to conductive or nerve type deafness.
• Option D. Treatment involves bone marrow transplantation. Treatment for osteogenesis imperfect is Bisphosphonates in growing age, and replacement of COLIA1 or COLIA2 gene.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteogenesis imperfecta is also called as brittle bones disease. Hyperlaxity of ligaments with hypermobile joints is common in this disease. Auditory nerve compression may occur, leading to deafness. Treatment involves use of bisphosphonates.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the below is a feature of osteogenesis imperfecta?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cataract. Cataracts, or clouding of the lens of the eye, are not typically associated with osteogenesis imperfecta.
• Option C. Anterior uveitis. Anterior uveitis, or inflammation of the front part of the uvea (the middle layer of the eye), is not a characteristic feature of osteogenesis imperfecta.
• Option D. Retinal detachment. Retinal detachment is not a typical feature of osteogenesis imperfecta. Osteogenesis imperfecta primarily affects the bones and is not directly associated with eye conditions like retinal detachment.
• Educational Objective:
• Blue sclera is a characteristic feature of osteogenesis imperfecta. The connective tissue in the sclera (the white part of the eye) is thinner and less opaque, allowing the underlying choroid to show through, giving the appearance of a blue or gray tint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Osteogenesis imperfecta has abnormality in which type of collagen?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Collagen 3 . Collagen type III is a fibrillar collagen found in tissues such as skin, lungs, and blood vessels. It is involved in providing structural support and integrity to these tissues.
• Option B. Collagen 2. Collagen type II is a fibrillar collagen that is a major component of cartilage.
• It provides structural support and tensile strength to cartilage, which is essential for its function in joints.
• Option C. Collagen 4. Collagen type IV is a non-fibrillar collagen found in basement membranes, a specialized form of extracellular matrix. It plays a crucial role in maintaining the integrity and structure of basement membranes.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteogenesis imperfecta has abnormality in type 1 collagen. It is a genetic disorder of connective tissue determined by quantitative qualitative defect in type I collagen formation. So, there is alteration in the structural integrity, or a reduction in the total amount of type I collagen, one of the major components of fibrillar connective tissue in skin, ligaments, bones, sclera, and teeth.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A child presented with hip pain. What is the diagnosis based on the given radiograph?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/28/picture13_ydJatOj.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is a condition characterized by low bone density, which leads to an increased risk of fractures. It is more common in older adults, particularly postmenopausal women, but can occur in children as well. It is commonly asymptomatic until a fracture occurs.
• Option C. Osteopetrosis. Osteopetrosis is a rare genetic disorder characterized by increased bone density due to impaired bone resorption by osteoclasts. It can cause bone pain, fractures, and skeletal abnormalities.
• Option D. Osteopoikilocytosis. Osteopoikilocytosis is a term usually associated with a rare genetic disorder affecting red blood cells, not bones. It involves abnormalities in the shape of red blood cells (poikilocytes) and is not directly related to hip pain or bone disorders.
• Educational Objective:
• A child presented with hip pain with the given radiograph, has Osteogenesis imperfecta. Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic disorder characterized by brittle bones that are prone to fractures. It can lead to skeletal deformities and is associated with mutations in the genes responsible for collagen type 1 synthesis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Prenatal determination of osteogenesis imperfecta is done by:
options: Abnormal Pro-alpha chain of collagen molecule

explanation.short: Ans. C) Abnormal Pro-alpha chain of collagen molecule
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alkaline phosphatase. Acid phosphatase is an enzyme found in various tissues, including bone. However, it is not specifically used for prenatal determination of osteogenesis imperfecta. Acid phosphatase assays are more commonly associated with certain bone disorders and conditions affecting the prostate.
• Option B. Alkaline Phosphatase . Alkaline phosphatase is an enzyme found in various tissues, including the bones and liver. It plays a role in bone mineralization. While elevated levels of alkaline phosphatase can be associated with certain bone disorders, it is not a direct method for prenatal determination of osteogenesis imperfecta.
• Option D. FGF3 mutation. Fibroblast Growth Factor 3 (FGF3) is not directly associated with osteogenesis imperfecta.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteogenesis imperfecta is primarily caused by mutations in the genes that encode the pro-alpha chains of collagen type I. The abnormal pro-alpha chains result in defective collagen synthesis, leading to brittle bones.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are features of osteogenesis imperfecta except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Blue Sclera. Blue sclera is a distinctive feature in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta. It results from the translucency of the connective tissues in the eye, allowing the underlying choroidal veins to be visible through the thin sclera.
• Option B. Multiple Fractures. Multiple fractures are a hallmark of osteogenesis imperfecta. Due to the genetic mutations affecting collagen type I synthesis, individuals with OI have bones that are brittle and prone to fractures, even with minimal trauma .
• Option D. Hearing loss. Conductive and sensorineural hearing loss can occur in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta. The hearing loss is often related to abnormalities in the middle ear bones (ossicles) or inner ear structures, which can be affected by the same collagen deficiencies responsible for bone fragility.
• Educational Objective:
• Multiple fractures, deafness (hearing loss) due to otosclerosis and blue sclera is seen. Cataract is not a feature.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Wormian bones are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Scheuermann’s disease. Scheuermann's disease, also known as Scheuermann's kyphosis, is a skeletal disorder that typically affects the thoracic spine, causing abnormal curvature (kyphosis). It is characterized by wedging of the vertebral bodies and can result in a rounded or hunched back. Wormian bones are not a characteristic feature of Scheuermann's disease.
• Option C. Paget’s disease. Paget's disease of bone is a chronic condition that results in the abnormal remodeling of bone. It can lead to enlarged and deformed bones, but the presence of Wormian bones is not a typical feature of Paget's disease.
• Option D. Osteosarcoma. Osteoclastoma, also known as giant cell tumor of bone, is a type of benign tumor that primarily affects the epiphyses (ends) of long bones. While osteoclastomas can cause bone destruction and abnormalities, the presence of Wormian bones is not a characteristic finding associated with this tumor.
• Educational Objective:
• Wormian bones are seen in Osteogenesis imperfecta, a genetic disorder characterized by brittle bones. The presence of Wormian bones is in the skull.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is false about Osteopetrosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Low levels of PTH
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Low levels of serum calcium. In osteopetrosis, the defective bone resorption can lead to impaired calcium release from bones into the bloodstream.
• Option B. Raised levels of alkaline phosphastase. Alkaline phosphatase is often elevated in osteopetrosis due to increased bone turnover and remodelling.
• Option C. Low levels of serum phosphate. Osteopetrosis is not typically associated with low levels of serum phosphate.
• Educational Objective:
• The statement which is false about osteopetrosis is low levels of parathyroid hormones. Laboratory parameters are serum calcium low, serum PTH high, serum phosphate low and serum ALP high.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Albers Schonberg disease is also known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteoporosis . Osteoporosis is also known as silent disease because it is asymptomatic until a bone is fractured.
• Option C. Osteogenesis imperfecta . It is also known as brittle bone disease because the bones are more prone to fracture at any time of the life, in utero, during birth, or after birth.
• Option D. Paget’s disease . It is also known as osteitis deformans because it involves formation of bones which are abnormally large and weak, more prone to break easily.
• Educational objective:
• Albers Schonberg disease is another name for osteopetrosis or marble bone disease.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Regarding osteopetrosis, all the following statements are true except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pancytopenia. Osteopetrosis can lead to pancytopenia, which is a condition characterized by a reduction in the number of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. The increased bone density in osteopetrosis can cause bone marrow crowding, leading to reduced hematopoiesis. Bone encroachment on marrow results in bone marrow failure with resultant pancytopenia.
• Option C. Cranial nerve compression. The increased bone density in osteopetrosis can cause narrowing of the cranial foramina, leading to compression of cranial nerves. This compression can result in various neurological symptoms, including vision and hearing impairment.
• Option D. Osteomyelitis in Mandible. Osteopetrosis can predispose individuals to osteomyelitis, especially in the mandible. Osteomyelitis of the mandible is common due to pancytopenia.
• Educational Objective:
• Cranial nerve compression due to bone encroachment on foramina may occur. Osteomyelitis of the mandible is common due to pancytopenia. Bone encroachment on marrow results in bone marrow failure with resultant pancytopenia. Fractures usually heal at slower rates in osteopetrosis but few studies have shown fracture healing is normal. Thus, all 4 options are correct. In case we have to choose one, it will be delayed healing of fracture as there is no debate about other features.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 3-year-old male presented with progressive anemia, hepatosplenomegaly and osteomyelitis of jaw with pathological fracture. X-ray shows chalky white deposits on bone. Probable diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteopoikilocytosis. Osteopoikilocytosis is not a term commonly associated with a specific bone disorder. It is more commonly used in the context of certain red blood cell abnormalities. It is not a feature of osteopetrosis.
• Option C. Alkaptonuria. Alkaptonuria is a metabolic disorder characterized by the accumulation of homogentisic acid, leading to dark pigmentation in urine and connective tissues. This is not seen in osteopetrosis.
• Option D. Myositis ossificans progressiva. Myositis ossificans progressiva, also known as fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP), is a rare genetic disorder where soft tissues progressively turn into bone. This is also not a feature of osteopetrosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Osteopetrosis is a genetic disorder characterized by increased bone density due to defective bone resorption by osteoclasts. Common features include brittle bones, increased fracture susceptibility, pancytopenia due to bone marrow crowding, and, in some cases, cranial nerve compression. Pancytopenia may lead to progressive anemia, hepatosplenomegaly and mandibular infections. X-ray with chalky white deposits on bone can be seen in osteopetrosis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is the most common muscle to be congenitally absent?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Semimembranosus. The semimembranosus is one of the hamstring muscles located in the posterior thigh. Congenital absence of the semimembranosus is relatively uncommon, and specific conditions associated with the absence of this muscle are less frequently reported.
• Option C. Teres Minor. The teres minor is a small muscle in the rotator cuff of the shoulder. Congenital absence of the teres minor is rare and might be associated with other shoulder or rotator cuff abnormalities.
• Option D. Gluteus maximus . The gluteus maximus is the largest muscle in the buttocks and is involved in hip extension. Congenital absence of the gluteus maximus is extremely rare, and there are limited reports of such cases.
• Educational Objective:
• While congenital absence of certain muscles is associated with specific conditions (e.g., Poland syndrome for pectoralis major), the absence of muscles like semimembranosus, teres minor, or gluteus maximus is uncommon and may be part of rare congenital anomalies. The most common muscle to be congenitally absent among the options provided is the pectoralis major.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nerve biopsy in leprosy is usually taken from:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ulnar nerve. The ulnar nerve is not commonly biopsied in cases of leprosy. While it is a major nerve in the forearm and hand, it is not typically involved in leprosy-related neuropathies.
• Option B. Median nerve. The median nerve is also not commonly biopsied in leprosy cases. Leprosy typically affects peripheral nerves more distally in the limbs, and the median nerve may not be as frequently involved.
• Option C. Lateral popliteal nerve. The lateral popliteal nerve is commonly affected by leprosy neuropathy. It supplies the muscles of the anterior compartment of the leg and provides sensory innervation to the dorsum of the foot and the web space between the first and second toes. Biopsy of this nerve is often undertaken in cases of suspected leprosy neuropathy to confirm the diagnosis.
• Educational objective:
• The sural nerve, located in the lower leg, is a common site for nerve biopsy in cases of leprosy. It is a sensory nerve that provides innervation to the lateral aspect of the foot and ankle. Biopsy of the sural nerve can provide valuable diagnostic information in cases of suspected leprosy neuropathy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Carpal tunnel syndrome, nerve compressed is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Ulnar nerve . The ulnar nerve is compressed in the cubital tunnel syndrome.
• Option C: Superficial radial nerve . Cheiralgia paresthetic is compression of the superficial branch of the radial nerve in the forearm.
• Option D: Musculocutaneous nerve . Isolated musculocutaneous nerve syndromes are relatively uncommon. Most significant entrapment occurs within the coracobrachialis muscle, leading to biceps brachii and brachialis weakness and atrophy with accompanying loss of sensation in the lateral forearm.
• Educational objective:
• In carpal tunnel syndrome, the median nerve is compressed under the flexor retinaculum at the wrist.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is seen in popliteal entrapment syndrome?
options: Abnormal relation between popliteal artery and lateral head of gastrocnemius
Decreased ankle pulses with ankle extension

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Evidence of atherosclerosis: Atherosclerosis is a condition characterized by the buildup of plaque in the arteries, leading to narrowing and reduced blood flow. While atherosclerosis itself might not be the primary cause, popliteal entrapment syndrome can occur in individuals with existing atherosclerosis, increasing the risk of complications.
• Option C. Abnormal relation between popliteal artery and lateral head of gastrocnemius: This anatomical abnormality is indeed common in people with popliteal entrapment syndrome. However, it is not the symptom that defines the condition. It is the abnormal relation between the popliteal artery and the medial head of gastrocnemius and not with the lateral head.
• Option D. Decreased ankle pulses with ankle extension: This finding can be present in severe cases of popliteal entrapment syndrome, but it is not a consistent symptom. The primary symptom remains the calf pain and cramping during exercise. Decreased ankle pulses might be more indicative of a more advanced stage of the condition or other underlying vascular problems.
• Educational Objective:
• Calf claudication is a symptom typically associated with peripheral artery disease (PAD), where there's a generalized narrowing of the arteries in the legs due to atherosclerosis. While PAD can coexist with popliteal entrapment syndrome, the classic symptom of popliteal entrapment is pain or cramping in the calf and foot that occurs specifically during exercise and improves with rest. This is different from claudication, which can occur even at rest in severe PAD cases.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Guyon’s canal, which nerve is affected?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Median nerve . The median nerve is compressed in the flexor retinaculum of the wrist. This leads to carpal tunnel syndrome.
• Option C: Radial nerve . The radial nerve is compressed most commonly in the proximal forearm.
• Option D: Musculocutaneous nerve . It is entrapped commonly within the corachobrachialis muscle, leading to biceps brachii and brachialis muscle weakness and atrophy, and loss of sensation in the lateral forearm.
• Educational objective:
• In Guyon’s canal, the ulnar nerve is most commonly compressed as the nerve passes through the canal to innervate the hand.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cheiralgia paresthetica involves:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Ulnar nerve. The ulnar nerve can be entrapped in the wrist or elbow. Its compression at the wrist is called Guyon Canal Syndrome while that at the elbow is called Cubital Tunnel Syndrome.
• Option B: Median nerve. The median nerve is compressed in the flexor retinaculum of the wrist. This leads to carpal tunnel syndrome.
• Option D: Musculocutaneous nerve. It is entrapped commonly within the corachobrachialis muscle, leading to biceps brachii and brachialis muscle weakness and atrophy, and loss of sensation in the lateral forearm.
• Educational objective:
• Cheiralgia paresthetica involves entrapment of the superficial radial nerve in the forearm. Other entrapment syndromes involving the peripheral nerves are:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Entrapment neuropathies commonly affect the following nerves except:

subject: Orthopedics
question: While doing sudden hyperflexion of thigh over abdomen (McRobert’s procedure) during childbirth, which of the following nerve is commonly affected?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Common peroneal nerve: The common peroneal nerve is primarily responsible for innervating the muscles of the lower leg and foot, as well as providing sensory innervation to parts of the leg and foot. It does not get directly affected during the McRobert’s procedure. However, if there were complications during childbirth, such as prolonged pressure on the lower leg or foot, there could be indirect effects on the common peroneal nerve. For example, prolonged pressure or stretching of the leg during childbirth could potentially result in nerve compression or injury. However, compared to the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh, the common peroneal nerve is less directly involved in the maneuver described in the question.
• Option B. Obturator nerve: The obturator nerve provides sensory and motor innervation to the muscles of the medial thigh and the skin of the inner thigh. While the obturator nerve does pass through the pelvic region and could theoretically be affected by pressure or stretching during the McRobert’s procedure, it is less likely to be directly involved compared to the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh. The McRobert’s procedure primarily involves hyperflexion of the thigh over the abdomen, which puts more direct pressure on nerves in the anterior and lateral aspects of the thigh rather than those in the medial aspect.
• Option C. Lumbosacral trunk: The lumbosacral trunk is formed by the merging of the fifth lumbar nerve root and the first sacral nerve root. It contributes to the formation of the sciatic nerve and does not directly supply the muscles or skin of the thigh. Therefore, it is unlikely to be directly affected during the McRobert’s procedure. This nerve primarily plays a role in the lower back and pelvic region rather than the thigh itself.
• Educational objective:
• One of the complications of the McRobert’s procedure is the compression of femoral nerve due to its compression beneath the inguinal canal. Among the given options, the lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh is affected by compression beneath the inguinal ligament.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest cause for neuralgic pain in foot is:
options: Compression of communication between medial and lateral plantar nerves

explanation.short: Ans. A) Compression of communication between medial and lateral plantar nerves
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Exaggeration of longitudinal arches . Exaggeration of the longitudinal arches of the foot, also known as pes planus or flat feet , can lead to various foot problems, including pain and discomfort. However, it is not related to the neuralgic pain in the foot. Flat feet may cause strain on various structures of the foot, but neuralgic pain is more commonly associated with nerve-related issues rather than structural abnormalities of the foot arches.
• Option C: Injury to deltoid ligament . The deltoid ligament is a strong ligament on the medial aspect of the ankle that provides stability to the joint. Injury to this ligament, known as deltoid ligament sprain , can lead to medial ankle pain and instability. However, neuralgic pain in the foot is not typically associated with injury to the deltoid ligament.
• Option D: Shortening of plantar aponeurosis . The plantar aponeurosis, also known as the plantar fascia , is a thick band of connective tissue that supports the arch of the foot. Shortening or tightening of the plantar aponeurosis can lead to conditions like plantar fasciitis, which commonly causes heel pain rather than neuralgic pain throughout the foot.
• Educational objective:
• The commonest cause of neuralgic pain in the foot is the compression of the communication between medial lateral plantar nerves.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tarsal tunnel syndrome involves:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh . Meralgia paresthetica is a clinical condition that involves pain and dysesthesia in the anterolateral thigh associated with compression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve.
• Option C: Common peroneal nerve . This nerve is injured by trauma at the neck of the fibula most commonly, leading to classic foot drop deformity.
• Option D: Sciatic nerve . Sciatic nerve is compressed in herniated or bulged lumbar intervertebral disc, causing symptoms commonly called as sciatica .
• Educational objective:
• Tarsal tunnel syndrome involves compression of the posterior tibial nerve beneath the flexor retinaculum as it passes behind the medial malleolus. It may arise from a space-occupying lesion within the tarsal tunnel or distally against medial or lateral plantar nerves.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common cause of tarsal tunnel syndrome is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: In a patient with a history of burning pain localized to the plantar aspect of the foot, the differential diagnosis must include:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Peripheral vascular disease . Peripheral vascular disease (PVD) includes peripheral arterial disease (PAD) and venous disease. PAD is a chronic progressive atherosclerotic disease leading to partial or total peripheral vascular occlusion. PAD typically affects the abdominal aorta, iliac arteries, lower limbs, and occasionally the upper extremities. There is atypical pain characterized by pain unrelated to physical activity, that occurs both at rest and exertion, and lasting longer than 10 minutes after exercise cessation. Intermittent claudication is the most classic symptom of PAD, characterized by an exercise-induced cramping sensation with associated fatigue, weakness, and or pressure. Symptoms are exacerbated by leg elevation and relieved by placing the limb in a dependent position. Paresthesias, lower extremity weakness, stiffness, and cool extremities may also be present.
• Option B: Tarsal coalition . Tarsal coalition is the complete or partial union between two or more bones in the midfoot and hindfoot. It is a developmental fusion and not that acquired secondary to rheumatoid arthritis, trauma or post-surgery. It can present as osseous (synostosis), fibrous (syndesmosis), or cartilaginous (synchondrosis) connections between the tarsal bones. Adolescents present with the insidious onset of hind foot pain with or without recurrent ankle sprains. Some complain of stiffness in the hind foot. The pain is usually described as coming from the lateral side of the hind foot and ankle. A twisting injury may stretch, tear, or disrupt the coalition, causing the coalition to become painful.
• Option D: Plantar fibromatosis . Plantar fibromatosis is a benign fibroblastic, connective tissue proliferative disorder of the superficial plantar aponeurosis of the foot. Onset is slow, and patients usually present after the disease becomes locally aggressive, causing pain and swelling in the medial non-weight bearing plantar surface of the feet. The classic presentation is that of a slow-growing, painless nodule in the medial or central plantar aponeurosis of the foot. Smaller nodules can present as local pressure points. Locally invasive nodules can lead to pain and swelling, and trouble with ambulation. Patients commonly present after experiencing increased pain in the plantar surface of the foot after long walks. Activities such as long walks, prolonged standing, and walking barefoot can exacerbate the symptoms.
• Educational objective:
• Three most common causes of pain in plantar aspect of foot are plantar fasciitis, tarsal tunnel syndrome, and posterior tibial tendinopathy. Burning pain is characteristic of neural pain. Hence, tarsal tunnel syndrome is preferred here.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
Identify the given X-ray abnormality.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/11/picture3.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Costochondritis . Costochondritis is a common, usually self-limiting, painful inflammation of one or multiple costochondral junctions and/or the costosternal articulation. There is usually absence of swelling, and chest wall palpation usually reproduces the pain.
• Option C: 2 nd – 3 rd rib fracture . 1 st -3 rd rib fractures are associated with brachial plexus or subclavian vessel injuries. These ribs are most commonly injured in high-energy traumas. Plain radiograph may miss up to 50% of rib fractures.
• Educational objective:
• The given image shows the cervical rib arising from the transverse process of the C7 vertebra.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nerve Roots of Long Thoracic Nerve of Bell: C5 C6 C7 C8 T1

subject: Orthopedics
question: Positive Adson’s test is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Buerger disease. Buerger's disease or thromboangitis is an inflammatory non atherosclerotic, inflammatory disease that affects small and medium arteries and veins of the upper and lower limbs and more common in tobacco use patients, mostly men than women < 45 years of age. To test the blood flow in this disease, the Allen’s test is done.
• Option C: Varicose veins. For clinical examination of the varicose veins, Trendelenburg test, Schwartz test, and Perthe’s test are done.
• Option D: Radial nerve injury. Radial nerve palsy is tested by wrist drop test, supination test, and thumb extension test.
• Educational objective:
• The Adson’s test is positive in thoracic outlet syndrome. It is a provocative test.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Iliotibial band contracture in patients of poliomyelitis will lead to:
options: Flexion at hip, extension at knee
Extension at hip, flexion at knee

explanation.short: Ans. A) Flexion at hip and knee

subject: Orthopedics
question: The most common cause of Sciatic nerve palsy is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Injections . Injections can occasionally cause sciatic nerve injury, it is not as common as fractures. Sciatic nerve injury from injections typically occurs due to inadvertent needle trauma during procedures such as intramuscular injections, nerve blocks, or other medical interventions. In cases where injections lead to sciatic nerve injury, symptoms may include pain, numbness, or weakness in the distribution of the sciatic nerve.
• Option C: Idiopathic . Idiopathic causes refer to those cases where the exact cause of sciatic nerve palsy is unknown, but fractures are more commonly identified as the cause. While idiopathic cases do exist, they are relatively less common compared to cases where a specific cause, such as fractures or other identifiable injuries, is present. Idiopathic sciatic nerve palsy may occur due to various factors, including spontaneous nerve inflammation or compression, but these cases often require thorough investigation to rule out other potential causes.
• Option D: Lumbar plexus injury . The lumbar plexus is a network of nerves in the lumbar region of the spine, giving rise to several major nerves, including the sciatic nerve. Injury to the lumbar plexus can potentially lead to sciatic nerve dysfunction. However, lumbar plexus injury causing sciatic nerve palsy is less common compared to direct trauma or compression of the sciatic nerve itself, such as in cases of fractures. Lumbar plexus injury may occur due to various causes, including trauma, surgery, or compression by tumors, but it is not typically the most common cause of sciatic nerve palsy.
• Educational objective:
• The most common cause of sciatic nerve palsy is the presence of fracture usually in the pelvis or proximal femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Winging of scapula is due to palsy of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Nerve to lattisimus dorsi . The injury of this nerve is characterized by paresis or paralysis of the latissimus dorsi muscle. The patients are unable to perform full extension, rotation, and adduction of the arm. In addition, the thoracodorsal nerve is often used as a nerve graft when the long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) is injured.
• Option C: Spinal accessory nerve . The most common and primary complaint of spinal accessory nerve injury is pain and weakness, mostly involving the shoulder. Radiation of pain is to the upper back, neck, and ipsilateral arm. Pain is exacerbated with weight on the injured shoulder without being supported. The traction and straining of the muscles, such as rhomboids and levator scapulae that work to compensate for the nerve injury, could lead to more pain and decreased strength on the injured side. The combination of the pain and decreased strength would limit the range of motion of the involved shoulder.
• Option D: Nerve to rhomboid . Compression of the dorsal scapular nerve (DSN) is a condition in which the dorsal scapular nerve entraps in the middle scalene muscle causing pain and stiffness in the shoulder girdle and limited end range motion, along with winging of scapula. The most common source of DSN entrapment is a hypertrophied middle scalene muscle.
• Educational objective:
• Winging of scapula is due to the damage to the long thoracic nerve that supplied the serratus anterior muscle, which leads to protrusion of the medial border of the scapula while moving the shoulder.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 45-year-old man presents with weakness, pain and fatigue in both lower limbs. He gives history of both limb paralysis 20 years back. What is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Polymyositis . Polymyositis, a relatively uncommon autoimmune disorder that develops due to abnormal activation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CD8 cells) and macrophages against muscular antigens that result in rhabdomyolysis and ultimately presents as a proximal myopathy. Polymyositis is characterized by symmetrical proximal muscle weakness due to the involvement of endomysial layers of skeletal muscles versus dermatomyositis, which involves the perimysial layers of muscles along with dermatological presentations. Polymyositis develops over the months. Polymyositis rarely presents in childhood and usually affects people above the age of 20 years. The hallmark of polymyositis is a progressive disease with symmetric involvement of shoulder and pelvic girdle muscles and neck flexors. Hip extensors can be involved making it difficult for patients to climb stairs. Patients may complain of the inability of overhead abduction or to get up from the seated position. Disease progression to distal muscles can result in abnormal fine movements such as writing and playing musical instruments. Constitutional symptoms include low-grade fever, anorexia, arthralgia, and weight loss. Polymyositis can cause interstitial lung disease (ILD), especially in anti-Jo-1 positive patients, presenting as shortness of breath and dry cough. Other presenting complaints are chest tightness and exertional dyspnea due to restrictive cardiomyopathy, constipation, or bloating due to gastrointestinal involvement and tightening and discoloration of fingers due to Raynaud phenomenon.
• Option B: Muscular dystrophy . It is a hereditary disorder that leads to progressive and generalized disease of the muscle due to inadequate or missing glycoproteins in the muscle cell plasma membrane. Gracilis, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and sartorius muscles can be affected. Feet can exhibit an equinovarus deformity. The pelvis can tilt. There may be contractures throughout the body. Spinal deformities may produce lordosis or scoliosis. The eye can exhibit cataracts and bilateral ptosis. Onset usually occurs in the third to fourth decades. There is accelerated deterioration near the age of onset. Parents may present with a concern that their child is not walking as other children their age. There is massive calf muscles and proximal lower limb muscle weakness. The individuals utilize their arms and hands to aid in rising from sitting. Other complaints can involve a history of delayed ambulation, toe walking, calf hypertrophy, and proximal hip girdle muscle instability. There can be asymptomatic elevation of serum creatine kinase, exertion intolerance, dilated cardiomyopathy, malignant hyperthermia, quadriceps myopathy, language delay, and Turner syndrome (Duchenne in X chromosome monozygotic females).
