Since the inheritance can be X-linked, the majority of patients are male. Scoliosis and sustained alveolar hypoventilation can cause severe problems.
• Option D: Neuropathy . Neuropathy occurs when nerve damage leads to pain, weakness, numbness or tingling in one or more parts of body. It may be because of disease, infection, injury, medicines, or long-term alcohol abuse. Sometimes, no cause is found. Different typed of neuropathies found are autonomic neuropathy, diabetic neuropathy, peripheral neuropathy, focal or mononeuropathy, and polyneuropathy. Abnormal sensations like numbness, tingling, burning; muscle weakness; pain; loss of movement; problem with digestion, bladder and bowel function, sexual response; sweating, dizziness are some common symptoms associated with neuropathies.
• Educational objective:
• The effect on bilateral lower limbs in the form of fatigue and a history of paralysis 20 years back, are descriptive of post-polio syndrome because the symptoms of poliomyelitis appears 15-20 years after recovery from the initial paralytic episode. There is also characteristic chronic fatigue, post viral infection which is progressive and nature and leads to significant loss of muscle strength.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 35-year-old Female with hypothyroidism, on treatment, complaints of heaviness and tingling in left index and middle fingers. The pain often increases in night and she often has to get up. Which of the following is not a clinical test for this condition?

subject: Orthopedics
question: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome is best diagnosed by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: CT scan. CT scans can be useful in identifying bony abnormalities such as cervical ribs, bony spurs, or other anatomical variations that may contribute to thoracic outlet syndrome. However, CT scans may not always clearly visualize soft tissues or adequately assess vascular compression, which are important considerations in diagnosing TOS. Therefore, while CT scans can be a valuable adjunct to diagnosis, they are not typically considered the primary diagnostic modality for TOS.
• Option B: MRI. MRI is particularly useful for evaluating soft tissue structures and can help identify abnormalities such as nerve compression, muscle atrophy, or vascular anomalies that may contribute to thoracic outlet syndrome. It can also visualize the spinal cord and nerve roots, which can be helpful in ruling out other potential causes of symptoms. MRI is often considered one of the preferred imaging modalities for diagnosing TOS due to its ability to provide detailed anatomical information without using ionizing radiation.
• Option C: Digital subtraction angiography. DSA is an invasive imaging technique used to visualize the blood vessels by injecting a contrast dye into the bloodstream and taking X-ray images. It is particularly useful for assessing vascular abnormalities such as arterial compression that may occur in thoracic outlet syndrome. DSA provides dynamic, real-time images of blood flow and can help identify areas of vascular compression or obstruction. While DSA can be valuable in cases where vascular involvement is suspected, it is an invasive procedure associated with potential risks such as allergic reactions to contrast dye, blood vessel injury, or infection. Therefore, it is typically reserved for cases where non-invasive imaging modalities are inconclusive or when intervention (such as angioplasty or surgical decompression) is being considered.
• Educational objective:
• While imaging studies such as CT scan, MRI, and digital subtraction angiography (DSA) can sometimes be useful in supporting the diagnosis and identifying the underlying cause of TOS such as bony abnormalities, vascular compression, or soft tissue abnormalities, they are not typically considered the primary diagnostic tool. These imaging modalities may be ordered in cases where the clinical presentation is ambiguous or to rule out other potential causes of the symptoms. Thus, clinical examination using history of the patient and physical examination tests is the preferred investigation tool for diagnosing thoracic outlet syndrome.

subject: Orthopedics
question: You have treated the simple and undisplaced fracture of shaft of right tibia in a nine-year-girl with above knee plaster cast. Her parents want to know the prognosis of union of the fractured limb which was affected by poliomyelitis four years ago. What is the best possible advice will you offer to the parents?
options: Fracture will unite on attaining puberty

explanation.short: Ans. A) Fracture will unite slowly
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Fracture will not unite. This scenario is highly unlikely, especially for a simple and undisplaced fracture in a child. Fractures in children have a good prognosis for healing, even with complicating factors such as a history of poliomyelitis. While healing may be delayed or the process may be more challenging, it is rare for a fracture to completely fail to unite, particularly with appropriate medical management.
• Option C: Fracture will unite normally. This option suggests that despite the history of poliomyelitis, the fracture will heal in a typical manner without significant delay or complications. While this scenario is possible, it may not fully account for the potential impact of poliomyelitis on the healing process. Fracture healing may indeed occur, but it might be slower than usual due to factors such as compromised muscle strength or altered biomechanics resulting from poliomyelitis.
• Option D: Fracture will unite on attaining puberty. While fractures may heal at different rates depending on various factors, it is not typical for a fracture to require puberty for union, especially in a child with appropriate treatment and care. Fracture healing usually occurs within weeks to months, rather than years, and puberty is not typically a prerequisite for this process.
• Educational objective:
• In the given scenario, the parents must be advised that the fracture will unite slowly, provided the slow healing process due to the history of poliomyelitis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In a 3-year-old child with polio paralysis, tendon transfer operation is done at:

explanation.short: Ans. B) 2 years after the disease
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: 2 months after the disease. Performing tendon transfer operation just two months after the onset of polio paralysis is very early in the course of the condition. At this stage, the child's muscles and nervous system may still be undergoing significant changes and adaptation following the acute phase of polio. The muscles affected by paralysis may not have stabilized, and there may still be potential for spontaneous recovery or improvement in muscle function. Additionally, operating too early may risk surgical complications or suboptimal outcomes due to the lack of adequate time for assessment and planning. Therefore, 2 months is unlikely to be the correct timing for tendon transfer operation in a child with polio paralysis.
• Option C: 6-12 months after the disease. This timing allows for a reasonable period of observation and recovery following the acute phase of the disease. By waiting for 6 to 12 months, medical professionals can assess the stability of the child's muscle function and determine whether surgical intervention is necessary to improve function and prevent deformities. This timeframe also aligns with the concept of early intervention to address muscle weakness and prevent secondary complications, such as joint contractures. Therefore, option C is a plausible timing for tendon transfer operation in a child with polio paralysis.
• Option D: After skeleton maturation. Delaying surgery until skeletal maturity may be considered when there are concerns about the child's growth potential, joint development, or when the risks of surgery outweigh the benefits at an earlier age. However, postponing surgery until skeletal maturation may also result in prolonged functional limitations, increased risk of joint deformities, and missed opportunities for early intervention to improve muscle function and mobility.
• Educational objective:
• In a 3-year-old child with polio paralysis, the tendon transfer option is done 2 years after the disease i.e., when the child turns 5 years old to observe the patient’s recovery and give time for the muscles to stabilize.

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is the age of tendon transfer in post-polio residual paralysis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: < 6 months. This timing is highly unlikely and generally not considered appropriate due to skeletal immaturity and anesthesia risk.
• Option B: 1 year. While this timing may be slightly more feasible compared to option A, it still raises similar concerns regarding skeletal immaturity, muscle function, and anesthesia risks.
• Option C: 2 years. This timing represents a slightly older age compared to options A and B but still raises concerns regarding skeletal immaturity, muscle function, and anesthesia risks, although to a lesser extent.
• Educational objective:
• > 5 years of age is considered as an appropriate age for tendon transfer in post-polio residual paralysis, taking into account factors such as skeletal growth, muscle maturity, functional assessment, and patient cooperation.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tinel’s sign is used:
options: To assess the severity of damage of nerve
To classify the type of nerve injury
To locate the site of nerve injury
To assess the regeneration and recovery

explanation.short: Ans. D) To assess the regeneration and recovery

subject: Orthopedics
question: Following nerve injury, the injured nerve regenerates at a rate of

subject: Orthopedics
question: About Seddon’s Classification, all are true except:
options: Complete anatomic division of nerve is classified as Neurotmesis
Axonotmesis has Tinel’s sign positive and progressive
Neurotmesis has complete recovery with/without surgical intervention
Saturday night palsy involves radial nerve

explanation.short: Ans. C) Neurotmesis has complete recovery with/without surgical intervention

subject: Orthopedics
question: Sunderland classification is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Muscle injury . The British Athletics muscle injury classification ( BAMIC ) is a five-point MRI-based system that is based on extent and site. It has been primarily based on hamstring injuries but is used in other muscle injuries.
• Option C. Tendon injury . Based on the anatomy of the tendon, it is possible to describe four pathologic conditions i.e., Peritenonitis, Peritenonitis with tendinosis , Tendinosis, and Tendinitis.
• Option D. Ligament injury. They are graded from 1 st to 3 rd degree.
• Educational objective:
• Sunderland classification is used to classify the nerve injuries.

subject: Orthopedics
question: While performing flexor tendon graft repair, graft is taken from:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Plantaris. The plantaris tendon is a small, thin tendon located in the calf. It is not commonly used for flexor tendon graft repair due to its relatively small size and limited function. Additionally, harvesting the plantaris tendon may not provide adequate strength or length for effective tendon grafting in flexor tendon repair.
• Option C. Extensor digitorum. The extensor digitorum tendon is located on the dorsal aspect of the forearm and hand. They are responsible for extending the fingers and wrist. This tendon is not typically used for flexor tendon graft repair because it serves the opposite function of flexion.
• Option D. Extensor indicis. The extensor indicis tendon is also located on the dorsal aspect of the forearm and hand. It is responsible for extending the index finger. Like the extensor digitorum, this tendon is not commonly used for flexor tendon graft repair due to its role in finger extension rather than flexion.
• Educational objective:
• The palmaris longus tendon is a slender, relatively superficial tendon located in the forearm. It is commonly used as a graft for flexor tendon repair due to its accessibility, adequate length, and minimal functional deficit after harvesting. Additionally, many individuals have a palmaris longus tendon that is easily identified, making it a convenient choice for tendon grafting in flexor tendon repair surgeries.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient woke up in the morning with an inability to extend digits. The rest of the sensory and motor examination of hand was normal. Which nerve is involved in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. C8-T1 roots . It supplies the medial 1 ½ fingers and controls flexion at MCP joints and extension at IP joints. Injury to these roots result in Ulnar Claw Hand.
• Option C. Radial nerve. Injury to Radial nerve will not only affect the extensor function but will also cause sensory loss in the 1 st web-space on the dorsum of the hand.
• Option D. Lower brachial plexus. injury will also cause sensory loss.
• Educational objective:
• The given clinical presentation of the patient is suggestive of posterior interosseous nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A politician was shot by a gun in back in political rally at T8 level, after which he developed paraplegia. The fact that the injured nerve is not able to regenerate is due to all the reasons except:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Lack of myelin inhibitors
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. No endoneurial tube . Endoneurial tubes are protective sheaths that surround individual nerve fibers within a peripheral nerve. These tubes provide structural support and contain essential elements for nerve regeneration, such as blood vessels and extracellular matrix components. If these tubes are disrupted or damaged, it can impede nerve regeneration. However, the absence of endoneurial tubes is not a typical reason for the inability of nerves to regenerate after injury.
• Option B. Glial scar formation . After nerve injury, glial cells react by forming a scar tissue composed of glial cells and extracellular matrix components. While this scar tissue can provide structural support and prevent further damage, it can also create a physical and biochemical barrier that inhibits nerve regeneration. Glial scar formation is a significant factor contributing to the failure of nerve regeneration after injury.
• Option C. Absence of growth factors . Growth factors play a crucial role in promoting nerve regeneration by stimulating axonal growth, promoting cell survival, and facilitating the remyelination process. In the absence of adequate growth factors, nerve regeneration can be significantly impaired. Therefore, the absence of growth factors is a valid reason for the failure of nerve regeneration after injury.
• Educational objective:
• Myelin inhibitors are proteins present in the myelin sheath of nerves that inhibit axonal growth. In conditions where these inhibitors are absent or downregulated, such as after nerve injury, axonal growth can occur more readily. Therefore, the lack of myelin inhibitors would not hinder nerve regeneration; rather, it would potentially facilitate it.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is true about nerve injury?
options: In all cases of open wound with clinical signs of nerve injury, nerve exploration should always be done
Nerve conduction velocity is best predictor within 48 hours of injury
Positive Tinel’s sign indicates the accurate location of lesion
Traction nerve injury should be repaired immediately

explanation.short: Ans. A) In all cases of open wound with clinical signs of nerve injury, nerve exploration should always be done.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Nerve conduction velocity is best predictor within 48 hours of injury . False. Nerve conduction velocity usually predicts about nerve injury between days 10 and 14.
• Option C. Positive Tinel’s sign indicates the accurate location of lesion. False. Tinel’s sign can predict the rate of nerve recovery and its speed of regeneration based upon the level of unmyelinated free nerve endings.
• Option D. Traction nerve injury should be repaired immediately . False. Traction nerve injuries are managed with expectant approach of wait and watch for recovery and then if recovery does not take place then it needs exploration and repair.
• Educational objective:
• In cases of an open wound with the clinical signs suggesting nerve injury, nerve exploration should always be done.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tinel’s sign is positive and progressive in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Neurotmesis . In this nerve injury, the Tinel’s sign is positive and non-progressive.
• Option C. Neuropraxia . In this injury, the Tinel’s sign is negative.
• Educational objective:
• Tinel’s sign is positive and progressive in axonotmesis. It is positive and non-progressive in neurotmesis. It is negative in neuropraxia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nerve with best recovery is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Motor march is seen in:

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient after sleeping on chair with hanging arm whole night presents with weakness in muscles supplied by ulnar nerve, causing claw hand. It is managed by:
options: Knuckle bender splint and wait and watch

explanation.short: Ans. B) Knuckle bender splint and wait and watch.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Electrophysiological studies. While electrophysiological studies such as nerve conduction studies and electromyography (EMG) can help diagnose nerve compression or injury, they are not typically used as the primary management for this condition. Instead, they are more useful for confirming the diagnosis and assessing the severity of nerve damage.
• Option C. Exploration of the nerve. Nerve exploration surgery involves surgically accessing the ulnar nerve to identify and address any compression or injury. While this option may be necessary in some cases, it is typically reserved for severe or refractory cases that do not respond to conservative management.
• Option D. Tendon transfer. Tendon transfer surgery can be considered for correcting the claw hand deformity caused by ulnar nerve palsy, but it is usually reserved for cases where conservative measures have failed or when there is permanent nerve damage.
• Educational objective:
• Knuckle splint use and wait and watch allows for conservative management while closely monitoring the patient's progress in this case scenario. If symptoms persist or worsen despite splinting, further interventions such as surgical exploration or tendon transfer may be considered. However, initial management often begins with conservative measures like splinting.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Prognosis after secondary nerve suturing is better in pure than in mixed ones. Based on this criterion, which one of the following nerves should be given the best result after suturing in identical conditions?

subject: Orthopedics
question: A pole vaulter had a fall during pole vaulting and had paralysis of the arm. Which of the following investigations gives the best recovery prognosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Muscle biopsy . Muscle biopsy is typically used to diagnose muscle diseases or disorders, such as muscular dystrophy. While it can provide valuable information about the condition of the muscles, it may not be the most relevant investigation for assessing recovery prognosis in the context of a nerve injury.
• Option C. Strength-duration curve . A strength-duration curve is a graph that represents the relationship between the intensity and duration of an electrical stimulus required to elicit a muscle contraction. This curve is used primarily in electrotherapy to determine optimal parameters for neuromuscular stimulation. While it can provide information about the excitability of nerves and muscles, it may not directly predict recovery prognosis following a nerve injury.
• Option D. Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) levels . Creatine phosphokinase is an enzyme found in muscles, and elevated levels of CPK in the blood can indicate muscle damage or injury. However, in the context of a nerve injury such as paralysis following a fall during pole vaulting, CPK levels may not directly correlate with recovery prognosis.
• Educational objective:
• The most relevant investigation for assessing nerve function and potential for recovery in the given context would be electromyography (EMG). EMG can provide valuable information about the extent of nerve damage and the potential for nerve regeneration, which can help inform the prognosis for recovery.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nerve suturing in a clean-cut injury is done best in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. 12 hours. While still within a relatively early timeframe, delaying repair beyond 6 hours may slightly decrease the likelihood of optimal outcomes compared to earlier repair.
• Option C. After one day. Delaying nerve suturing for more than 24 hours after the injury significantly reduces the chances of successful nerve regeneration and functional recovery. Nerve fibers may begin to degenerate, and the ends of the nerve may retract, making it more challenging to achieve proper alignment during repair.
• Option D. After two days. Further delaying nerve suturing beyond two days greatly diminishes the chances of successful nerve regeneration. By this time, significant degeneration and scarring may have occurred, making it much more difficult to achieve satisfactory outcomes with nerve repair alone.
• Educational objective:
• 6 hours timeframe allows for prompt repair, maximizing the chances of successful nerve regeneration and functional recovery. It is crucial to perform nerve suturing as soon as possible after the injury to optimize outcomes and minimize long-term deficits. Delaying repair beyond this timeframe may compromise the effectiveness of the surgical intervention and the patient's ultimate recovery.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Following indicates better prognosis in nerve injury except:

subject: Orthopedics
question: What is true about Erb’s Palsy? Adduction + Internal rotation Supination seen C5, C6 roots affected Lower trunk injury causes this deformity Claw hand is seen

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Adduction + Internal rotation. True. The deformity in Erb’s palsy is adduction and internal rotation of the shoulder because of the loss of abduction and external rotation movements.
• Supination seen. False. Supination movement is lost in the Erb’s palsy, thus the forearm appears pronated.
• C5, C6 roots affected. True. The cause of Erb’s palsy is the brachial plexus injury. The most commonly affected region of the brachial plexus in this condition is the upper trunk, involving C5-C6 nerve roots.
• Lower trunk injury causes this deformity. False. This injury is caused by the injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus i.e., C5-C6 nerve roots.
• Claw hand is seen. False. The deformity characteristically seen in the Erb’s palsy is the policeman’s/waiter’s/porter’s tip hand.
• Educational objective:
• Erb’s palsy is caused by the injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus, involving C5-C6 nerve roots. This causes loss of shoulder abduction and external rotation, forearm supination movements. This loss of movements leads to the presentation of the shoulder in adduction and internal rotation, and forearm in pronation, called as policeman’s tip hand deformity.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not true about Klumpke’s paralysis?
options: Involves lower trunk of brachial plexus
Intrinsic muscles of hand are paralyzed
Horner’s syndrome can never be associated

explanation.short: Ans. D) Horner’s syndrome can never be associated
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Involves lower trunk of brachial plexus. True. The klumpke’s palsy involved the lower trunk of the brachial plexus i.e., the C8-T1 nerve roots.
• Option B. Intrinsic muscles of hand are paralyzed. True. The wrist and finger flexors become weak, while the intrinsic muscles of the hand are paralyzed.
• Option C. Claw hand is a feature. True. The combination of weakness of the wrist and finger flexors and paralysis of the intrinsic muscles of hand leads to a characteristic claw hand deformity.
• Educational objective:
• Unilateral Horner’s syndrome is an important association of the Klumpke’s palsy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Erb’s palsy involves injury to

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following deformity is evident in case of Erb’s palsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Winging of scapula . In a patient with a winged scapula, the medial or lateral aspect of the scapula appears to be protruding from the back. It can create a winged appearance. The most common etiology of a winged scapula is damaged or impaired innervation to the serratus anterior muscle. The nerve that innervates this muscle is the long thoracic nerve. Some patients may also complain of an inability to raise the affected arm above their head.
• Option C. Claw hand . When the ulnar nerve is injured, the muscles innervated by the nerve begin to weaken. This leads to an imbalance between the strong extrinsic muscles (i.e., extensor digitorum communis) and the weakened intrinsic muscles (i.e., interossei and lumbricals). This imbalance is characterized clinically by metacarpophalangeal (MCP) hyperextension and proximal interphalangeal (PIP), and distal interphalangeal (DIP) flexion, known as claw hand deformity.
• Option D. Wrist drop . Wrist drop is a disorder caused by radial nerve palsy. Because of the radial nerve's innervation of the extensor muscles of the wrist and digits, those whose radial nerve function has been compromised cannot actively extend them. As such, the hand hangs flaccidly in a position of flexion when the patient attempts to bring the arm to a horizontal position. Causes of wrist drop can range for penetrative trauma to external compression (Saturday night palsy) to systemic nutritional deficiencies.
• Educational objective:
• In Erb’s palsy, policeman’s tip deformity is evident which involves shoulder adduction and medial rotation, forearm extension and pronation while the arm hangs limply by the side of the trunk.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Aeroplane splint is used in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Radial nerve injury. For the wrist drop in radial nerve injury, a cock-up splint is used to keep the wrist and fingers in extension.
• Option B. Ulnar nerve injury. For the claw hand due to ulnar nerve injury, knuckle bender splint is used.
• Option D. Scoliosis. The Milwaukee brace, which is the original cervico-thoracic-lumbar-sacral orthosis (CTLSO) is an older and bulkier brace for scoliosis. It is used for curves in the thoracic or cervical spine. The most-commonly prescribed brace for scoliosis today is the Boston brace. It is a type of thoracic-lumbar-sacral orthosis (TLSO).
• Educational objective:
• The aeroplane splint is used in the brachial plexus to keep the arm in abduction.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Muscles paralyzed in Erb’s paralysis are all except:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Klumpke’s paralysis involves:

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 45-year-male present with abrupt onset pain, weakness, loss of contour of shoulder and wasting of muscle of arm on 5th day of tetanus toxoid immunization in deltoid. Likely cause is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Radial nerve entrapment. Radial nerve entrapment is an uncommon diagnosis. The most location of entrapment is the proximal forearm. Radial nerve entrapment often results from overuse but can occur secondary to other causes such as direct trauma, fractures, lacerations, compressive devices, or post-surgical changes. Symptoms usually develop very slowly. Symptoms include pain, sensory and motor changes, paresthesias, and/or paralysis limited to the dorsoradial aspect of the distal forearm and hand. If radial nerve entrapment has resulted in only mild damage (neurapraxia), recovery should be rapid and complete in 2 to 8 weeks. However, more severe injuries (axonotmesis) will recover more slowly.
• Option B. Thoracic outlet syndrome . Thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS) manifests when pressures in the thoracic outlet increase to the point of impinging vessels or nerves. These pressures can result from several anatomical abnormalities, such as the thoracic ribs or space-occupying lesions, including tumors or cysts. Nebulous pain is one of the most common complaints. Venous obstruction can present with upper extremity swelling, venous distention, and pain ranging from the hand to the forearm. Upper extremity deep venous thromboses (DVTs) can also be present. Patients who undergo conservative therapy have their symptoms resolve in about 90% of cases.
• Option D. Hysteria . Histrionic personality disorder (HPD) is characterized by a pervasive pattern of attention-seeking behaviours and a theatrical level of emotional reactions. The condition is usually life-long, though onset is typically in late adolescence or early adulthood. Individuals with HPD are often described as narcissistic, self-indulgent, flirtatious, dramatic, extroverted, and animated. Individuals with HPD may feel underappreciated or disregarded when they are not the center of attention. They may be vibrant, enchanting, overly seductive, or inappropriately sexual. People presenting with HPD typically demonstrate rapidly shifting and shallow emotions that others may perceive as insincere. Several factors are believed to contribute to the development of HPD, including genetic predisposition, childhood experiences, and environmental influences. Ultimately, HPD is unlikely to resolve on its own or with treatment.
• Educational objective:
• The described history and symptoms of the patient after an immunization are suggestive of brachial neuritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are true regarding brachial plexus injury, except:
options: Preganglionic lesions have a better prognosis than postganglionic lesions
Erb’s palsy causes paralysis of the abductors and external rotators of the shoulder
In Klumpke’s palsy, Horner’s syndrome may be present on the ipsilateral side
Histamine test is useful to differentiate between the preganglionic and postganglionic lesions

explanation.short: Ans. A) Preganglionic lesions have a better prognosis than postganglionic lesions.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Erb’s palsy causes paralysis of the abductors and external rotators of the shoulder. True. In Erb’s palsy, the shoulder abduction and external rotation movements are lost, leading to the presentation of shoulder in adduction and internal rotation.
• Option C. In Klumpke’s palsy, Horner’s syndrome may be present on the ipsilateral side. True. Klumpke’s paralysis also manifest as unilateral (ipsilateral) Horner’s syndrome.
• Option D. Histamine test is useful to differentiate between the preganglionic and postganglionic lesions. True. The purpose of the histamine test i s to differentiate preganglionic and postganglionic lesions. The intradermal injection of histamine causes triple response (red reaction due to capillary dilatation, wheal due to fluid extravasation from increased permeability, and flare due to arteriolar dilatation and to axon reflex in sensory nerve). If there is response with flare in an otherwise unconscious skin area, then the lesion has to be proximal to the dorsal root ganglion, which means that it must be a root avulsion injury. On the contrary, when the lesion is postganglionic the test will be negative as the continuity between the skin and dorsal root ganglion will have been interrupted.
• Educational objective:
• The preganglionic brachial plexus lesions have a poor prognosis because the nerve avulses from the spinal cord, so it cannot be repaired. The postganglionic lesions restore spontaneously or may require surgery, but have a better prognosis than preganglionic lesions.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following muscles undergo paralysis after injury to C5 and C6 spinal nerves except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Biceps. The biceps brachii muscle is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve, which originates from the C5-C6 nerve roots. Therefore, injury to the C5 and C6 spinal nerves would lead to paralysis of the biceps muscle.
• Option C. Brachialis. The brachialis muscle is primarily innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve, which arises from the C5-C6 nerve roots. Therefore, injury to the C5 and C6 spinal nerves would result in paralysis of the brachialis muscle.
• Option D. Brachioradialis. The brachioradialis muscle is innervated by the radial nerve, which receives contributions from the C5-C8 nerve roots. Therefore, injury to the C5 and C6 spinal nerves alone would not directly lead to paralysis of the brachioradialis muscle. However, it's worth noting that the brachioradialis can be weakened to some extent due to the loss of support from other muscles if there's significant injury affecting the entire C5-C8 nerve distribution.
• Educational objective:
• Due to the injury to the C5-C6 spinal nerve roots, there will be paralysis of the biceps, brachialis and the brachioradialis but there will be no effect on the corachobrachialis because it is supplied by the C5-C7 nerve roots while the other three are supplied by the C5-C6 nerve roots.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common cause of neurological deficit in upper limb is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Polio. Polio is a viral infectious disease caused by the poliovirus. While polio primarily affects the motor neurons in the spinal cord and can lead to muscle weakness and paralysis, it is not the most common cause of neurological deficit in the upper limb.
• Option C. C1–C2 dislocation. Dislocation of the C1 (atlas) and C2 (axis) vertebrae is a serious injury that can result from trauma, such as a high-speed motor vehicle accident or a severe fall. This type of injury can cause compression or injury to the spinal cord, leading to neurological deficits. However, C1–C2 dislocation is relatively uncommon compared to other causes of neurological deficit in the upper limb.
• Option D. Fracture-dislocation of cervical spine. Fracture-dislocation of the cervical spine involves both a vertebral fracture and displacement of the affected vertebrae, often due to significant trauma such as a motor vehicle accident or a fall from height. This type of injury can result in compression or injury to the spinal cord, leading to neurological deficits not only in the upper limb but also potentially affecting other regions of the body. While it is a serious injury, fracture-dislocation of the cervical spine may not be the most common cause of neurological deficit specifically in the upper limb.
• Educational objective:
• The most common cause of neurological deficit in the upper limb is the erb’s palsy caused due to brachial plexus injury at C5-C6 nerve root level.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following are features of musculocutaneous nerve injury at axilla except:
options: Loss of sensation on radial side of forearm

explanation.short: Ans. A) Loss of flexion of shoulder

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient presented with Paresthesia and loss of sensation over lower part of deltoid. X-ray showed fracture surgical neck of humerus. Likely nerve to be involved is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Musculocutaneous nerve . In its injury, there is loss of flexion of elbow, supination of forearm and sensory loss on lateral aspect of forearm. It is damaged in shoulder dislocation, second common to axillary nerve.
• Option C. Radial nerve . Common causes of radial nerve injuries are fractures of the humerus (spiral fracture along the distal third of the humerus (Holstein-Lewis fracture)), improper use of crutches, overuse of the arm (secondary to manual labor, chronic overuse, or sport-related participation), and work-related accidents. A patient may complain of decreased or absent sensation on the dorsoradial side of hand and wrist with an inability to extend wrist, thumb, and fingers. This is also referred to as " wrist drop .” If damaged at the axilla, there will be a loss of extension of the forearm, hand, and fingers. There will be a sensory loss in the lateral arm and posterior aspect of the forearm, radiating to the radial aspect of the dorsal hand and digits. This is seen commonly with " Saturday night palsy " and improperly using crutches ( crutch palsy ).
• Option D. Ulnar nerve . Repetitive elbow flexion and extension, arthritic changes, and valgus deformities at the elbow increase its vulnerability to injury. The patient may have numbness and paresthesia, radiating distally to the ulnar aspect of the hand, the fifth digit and the ulnar aspect of the fourth digit. It is usually associated with elbow flexion, particularly at night.
• Educational objective:
• Due to the fracture of the surgical neck of humerus, the most commonly affected nerve is the axillary nerve. Injury to the axillary nerve causes paresthesia and loss of sensation over the lower part of the deltoid, known as the regimental badge area .

subject: Orthopedics
question: Axillary nerve injury is likely to be seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Coracoid process fracture. In fractures involving the coracoid process, there may be compression of the brachial plexus and suprascapular nerve paralysis.
• Option C. Humerus shaft fracture. This is involved with injury to the radial nerve.
• Option D. Brachial plexus injury. Injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus leads to Erb’s palsy while that of the lower trunk leads to Klumpke’s palsy.
• Educational Objective:
• An axillary nerve injury is commonly seen after the anterior or inferior dislocation of the shoulder.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A child after a motor vehicle trauma is unable to abduct his arm. X-ray shows fracture around surgical neck humerus. Nerve likely to be injured is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Musculocutaneous nerve . In its injury, there is loss of flexion of elbow, supination of forearm and sensory loss on lateral aspect of forearm. It is damaged in shoulder dislocation, second common to axillary nerve.
• Option C. Radial nerve . Common causes of radial nerve injuries are fractures of the humerus (spiral fracture along the distal third of the humerus (Holstein-Lewis fracture)), improper use of crutches, overuse of the arm (secondary to manual labor, chronic overuse, or sport-related participation), and work-related accidents. A patient may complain of decreased or absent sensation on the dorsoradial side of hand and wrist with an inability to extend wrist, thumb, and fingers. This is also referred to as " wrist drop .” If damaged at the axilla, there will be a loss of extension of the forearm, hand, and fingers. There will be a sensory loss in the lateral arm and posterior aspect of the forearm, radiating to the radial aspect of the dorsal hand and digits. This is seen commonly with " Saturday night palsy " and improperly using crutches ( crutch palsy ).
• Option D. Ulnar nerve . Repetitive elbow flexion and extension, arthritic changes, and valgus deformities at the elbow increase its vulnerability to injury. The patient may have numbness and paresthesia, radiating distally to the ulnar aspect of the hand, the fifth digit and the ulnar aspect of the fourth digit. It is usually associated with elbow flexion, particularly at night.
• Educational objective:
• Due to the fracture of the surgical neck of humerus, the most commonly affected nerve is the axillary nerve. Injury to the axillary nerve causes weakness of shoulder abduction movement up to 90⁰.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 21-year male with fracture surgical neck humerus presented with regimental badge sign and difficulty in abduction. Nerve most likely to be injured is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Suprascapular nerve injury . Suprascapular nerve can be injured in scapulothoracic dyskinesia, scapula/clavicle/proximal humerus fracture, glenohumeral joint dislocation and AC joint injuries. Patients usually present with dull, aching pain in the superior or posterolateral shoulder (or both) that can radiate down the arm or into the neck. Additional symptoms include a sense of instability if there is associated shoulder pathology, such as a labral or rotator cuff tear. The patient’s pain may be exacerbated by cross-body adduction and internal rotation. Weakness may be noted with either or both shoulder external rotation and abduction.
• Option C. Erb’s palsy injury . Erb’s palsy is caused due to excessive downward stretching of shoulder on same side and head toward opposite side, e.g. Blow or fall on shoulder/delivery. As a result, the arm is adducted and medially rotated and the forearm is extended and pronated. The arm hangs limply by the side. There is loss of shoulder abduction and lateral rotation, elbow flexion, and forearm supination.
• Option D. Musculocutaneous nerve . In its injury, there is loss of flexion of elbow, supination of forearm and sensory loss on lateral aspect of forearm. It is damaged in shoulder dislocation, second common to axillary nerve.
• Educational objective :
• Due to a fracture of surgical neck of humerus, there occurs an injury to the axillary nerve. Injury to the axillary nerve presents with difficulty in shoulder abduction movement. There is presence of a regimental badge area around the deltoid on the arm that represents the loss of sensation in the lower half of the deltoid.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Axillary Nerve Injury is least likely in:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Improper use of crutch

subject: Orthopedics
question: In axillary nerve paralysis, all of the following are true except:
options: Extension of shoulder with arm abducted to 90 degrees is impossible
Small area of numbness is present over the shoulder region

explanation.short: Ans. D) Patient cannot initiate abduction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Deltoid muscle is wasted . True. Axillary nerve injury causes deltoid muscle palsy. This leads to weakness of the shoulder abduction in the range of 15-90⁰.
• Option B. Extension of shoulder with arm abducted to 90 degrees is impossible . True. Since the abduction of shoulder is not possible, there will be no extension of the shoulder also.
• Option C. Small area of numbness is present over the shoulder region . True. A small area of numbness is present over the lower half of the deltoid. This is called regimental badge area.
• Educational objective:
• Due to the axillary nerve paralysis, there is wasting of the deltoid muscle, leading to weakness of the shoulder abduction movement. The patient is unable to initiate shoulder abduction and perform abduction till 90⁰. There is also an area of sensory loss in the lower half of the deltoid, called regimental badge area.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tinel’s sign is:
options: Numbness in the region of the nerve of inflating the pneumatic cuff
Tingling on flexion of the joint

explanation.short: Ans. A) Sensation on tapping the nerve
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Numbness in the region of the nerve of inflating the pneumatic cuff. The described test might be related to assessing nerve compression or ischemia, such as in a Tourniquet test.
• Option C. Tingling on flexion of the joint. Tinel's sign is specifically related to tapping or percussing over a nerve to elicit tingling, not related to joint flexion.
• Option D. Applying direct pressure. Tinel's sign involves tapping or lightly percussing a nerve, not applying direct pressure.
• Educational objective:
• Tinel’s sign is the tingling sensation felt on tapping the nerve in the distal to proximal direction.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following features can be observed after the injury to axillary nerve, except:
options: Loss of rounded contour of shoulder
Loss of sensation along lateral side of upper arm

explanation.short: Ans. C) Loss of overhead abduction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Loss of rounded contour of shoulder . True. Due to axillary nerve injury, there is weakness of the deltoid muscle which leads to flattening of the rounded shoulder contour.
• Option B. Loss of sensation along lateral side of upper arm . True. Axillary nerve injury causes loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid, called as regimental badge area.
• Option D. Atrophy of deltoid muscle . True. Due to the weakness of the deltoid muscle, there is wasting/atrophy of the muscle.
• Educational objective:
• The overhead abduction of the shoulder is performed by the serratus anterior and trapezius muscles which are supplied by the long thoracic and spinal accessory nerves respectively. Since these muscles are not affected by the axillary nerve injury, the overhead abduction of the shoulder is not lost. 15-90⁰ of shoulder abduction is lost in the axillary nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
Image of dorsum of hand is given. Which nerve gives sensory supply to this region?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/09/radial-nerve-supply.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Posterior interosseous nerve . It is the superficial branch of the radial nerve. It gives motor innervation to the common extensors (ECRB, EDC, EDM, and ECU) and deep extensors (Supinator, APL, EPB, EPL, and EIP), and sensory innervation to dorsal wrist capsule.
• Option C. Median nerve . It originates from the medial and lateral cords of the brachial plexus. It gives motor innervation to the superficial volar forearm group (pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus), intermediate group (FDS), deep group (lateral FDP, FPL, pronator quadratus), and hand (1-2 lumbricals, opponens pollicis, APB, flexor pollicis brevis). The cutaneous innervation by the median nerve is given to the hand (lateral aspect of palm, palmar surface, lateral 3 ½ digits) via palmar and digital cutaneous branches.
• Option D. Ulnar nerve . It is the continuation of medial cord of the brachial plexus. It gives motor innervation to the anterior compartment of the forearm, and hand. In the forearm, it supplies the FCU and medial FDP. In the hand, it supplies the hypothenar muscles, medial 2 lumbricals, adductor pollicis, palmar and dorsal interossei of hand. The sensory supply is to the medial half of the palm via palmar cutaneous branch, dorsal surface of medial 1 ½ fingers via dorsal cutaneous branch, and palmar surface of medial 1 ½ fingers via superficial branch.
• Educational objective:
• The given image of the dorsum of hand in the question shows the sensory supply of the radial nerve to the hand.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient presented with loss of sensations in the lateral three and a half fingers. Which of the following will be an additional finding in this patient?
options: Loss of sensation on thenar eminence
Loss of sensation on hypothenar eminence

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Loss of sensation on thenar eminence . The thenar eminence is is innervated primarily by the median nerve. In the scenario described, the involvement of the median nerve is unlikely, as it primarily innervates the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and half of the ring finger. Therefore, loss of sensation on the thenar eminence would not typically be an additional finding in this patient.
• Option B. Loss of sensation on hypothenar eminence . The hypothenar eminence is innervated primarily by the ulnar nerve. Since the scenario describes loss of sensation in the lateral three and a half fingers, which are primarily innervated by the median nerve, there cannot be loss of sensation on the hypothenar eminence.
• Option C. Atrophy of adductor pollicis . The adductor pollicis muscle of the hand is primarily innervated by the ulnar nerve. Atrophy of the adductor pollicis is a characteristic finding in ulnar nerve injury or compression. Therefore, atrophy of the adductor pollicis cannot be an additional finding in this patient.
• Educational objective:
• The presented case scenario describes loss of sensation in the lateral 3 ½ fingers, which is caused due to median nerve injury at the wrist. When the median nerve is injured at the wrist, there is also loss of opposition of the thumb seen due to the paralysis of the opponens pollicis muscle. This muscle paralysis appears as the ape-thumb deformity where the thumb remains in the same plane as the palm.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Compression neuropathy is:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Nerve entrapped in closed space
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Muscle entrapped in closed space . Muscle entrapment in a closed space is commonly referred to as compartment syndrome . This condition occurs when there is increased pressure within a muscle compartment, leading to reduced blood flow, nerve compression, and tissue damage. Compartment syndrome can be acute or chronic and typically requires medical intervention, including surgical decompression in severe cases. Symptoms may include severe pain, swelling, numbness, and weakness in the affected muscle compartment.
• Option C: Vein entrapped in closed space . Vein entrapment in a closed space is often referred to as venous entrapment syndrome or May-Thurner syndrome . This condition occurs when the left iliac vein becomes compressed by the right iliac artery against the lumbar spine, leading to narrowing or obstruction of the vein. As a result, blood flow may be impeded, potentially causing symptoms such as swelling, pain, and discomfort in the affected area. Venous entrapment syndrome typically requires medical evaluation and may be treated with interventions such as anticoagulant therapy or surgery to relieve the compression on the vein.
• Option D: Artery entrapped in closed space . Artery entrapment in a closed space is commonly referred to as arterial entrapment syndrome or arterial compression syndrome . This condition occurs when an artery becomes compressed or trapped within surrounding structures, such as muscles, ligaments, or bones. As a result, blood flow through the artery may be restricted, leading to symptoms such as pain, numbness, or weakness in the affected limb. Arterial entrapment syndrome typically requires medical evaluation and may be managed with interventions such as surgical release of the entrapped artery or vascular reconstruction to restore normal blood flow.
• Educational objective:
• Compression neuropathy refers to entrapment of the nerves in a closed space. This commonly occurs in the peripheral nerves that are compressed by the surrounding tissues.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Brachial plexus injury with Horner’s syndrome, nerve root level involved is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Saturday night palsy is which type of nerve injury?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Axonotmesis. This is seen in closed fractures and dislocations.
• Option C. Neurotmesis. This involves injury from sharp objects like glass, knife or sharp metal.
• Educational objective:
• Saturday night palsy is neuropraxia of the radial nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Foot drop is due to palsy of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Superficial peroneal nerve: Ankle sprains and ankle twisting cause stretching of superficial peroneal nerve. It can compress at the exit in the deep fascia of the leg. This stretching/compression may present as pain and paresthesias over dorsum of foot. Direct damage from fractures of the proximal fibula, perforating injury to lateral leg may present as loss of eversion, and loss of sensation over most of dorsal foot and anterolateral aspect of distal leg.
• Option C. Femoral nerve neuropathy: Is an uncommon peripheral neuropathy, which most commonly occurs when the femoral nerve is compressed as it passes under the inguinal ligament, anterior to the iliopsoas muscle. The patient presents with groin pain, loss of power of hip flexion and knee extension, loss of/reduced knee jerk, decreased sensation over anteromedial thigh and medial aspect of the lower leg, and loss of muscle bulk of quadriceps.
• Option D. Obturator nerve: Obturator neuropathy is a difficult clinical problem to evaluate. One possible cause of pain is due to fascial entrapment of the nerve. Symptoms include medial thigh or groin pain, weakness with leg adduction, and sensory loss in the medial thigh of the affected side.
• Educational objective:
• Foot drop is caused due to injury to the deep peroneal nerve, a branch of the common peroneal nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tendon most commonly used for tendon transfer is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Test for tight iliotibial band is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Thomas test. The Thomas Test measures hip flexor length and distinguishes tightness between one joint and two joint muscles. Hip flexor length directly correlates to the available range of motion at the hip and knee joints.
• Option C: Simmond’s test. Simmonds-Thompson test is a diagnostic tool for Achilles tendon rupture. The absence of foot plantarflexion on calf compression is interpreted as a positive test result and indicative of Achilles tendon rupture.
• Option D: Charnley’s test. Charnley's test, also known as the Charnley compression test , is a clinical maneuver used to assess the stability and integrity of the hip joint, particularly in cases of suspected hip pathology such as hip osteoarthritis.
• Educational objective:
• To test the tightness of the iliotibial band, the Ober’s test is used. The Thomas test is used to test the tightness of the hip flexor muscles. The Simmond’s test is used to evaluate the Achilles tendon rupture. The Charnley’s test is used to evaluate for hip osteoarthritis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient can make his fist but is unable to flex his index finger. Which nerve is affected in him?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Radial nerve. With the hand supinated, the extensors are aided by gravity, and the hand function may appear normal. However, when the hand is pronated, the wrist and hand will drop. This is also referred to as " wrist drop .”
• Option B. Ulnar nerve. The initial presentation include a decrease in normal hand function. The MCP joints is hyperextended, and the IP joints flexed. This is called ulnar claw hand. The second and third digits will not be as involved as the fourth and fifth digits. This is because the median nerve innervates the lumbricals involving the second and third digits, and the ulnar nerve innervates the lumbricals involving the fourth and fifth digits. The patient may also exhibit functional weakness while attempting a grasp, grip, or pinch.
• Option C. Musculocutaneous nerve. Isolated injury to the musculocutaneous nerve is a rare occurrence. Associated signs and symptoms of an isolated musculocutaneous neuropathy may include weakness in elbow flexion or shoulder flexion, atrophy of the biceps brachii, and pain or paresthesia at the lateral forearm.
• Educational objective:
• The inability to flex the index finger and make a fist are suggestive of median nerve injury at the elbow. This occurs due to loss of flexion of IP joint of the middle and index fingers, presenting as a pointing index deformity.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient present with loss of sensation of ring and little fingers with wasting of hypothenar muscles. Where is the lesion?
options: Ulnar nerve before division into deep and superficial

explanation.short: Ans. C) Ulnar nerve before division into deep and superficial
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Deep branch of ulnar nerve . The deep branch of the ulnar nerve innervates the intrinsic muscles of the hand, including the interossei muscles, but it does not provide sensory innervation to the skin. Therefore, a lesion affecting only the deep branch of the ulnar nerve would not lead to loss of sensation in the ring and little fingers.
• Option B. Superficial branch of ulnar nerve . The superficial branch of the ulnar nerve primarily provides sensory innervation to the skin of the ulnar aspect of the hand, including the ring and little fingers. However, it does not innervate the hypothenar muscles. Therefore, a lesion affecting only the superficial branch would not lead to wasting of the hypothenar muscles.
• Option D. Median nerve . The median nerve primarily innervates muscles in the anterior compartment of the forearm and muscles in the thenar eminence of the hand. It also provides sensory innervation to the palmar aspect of the hand, but it does not innervate the ring and little fingers or the hypothenar muscles. Therefore, a lesion of the median nerve would not cause the specific symptoms described in the question.
• Educational objective:
• The loss of sensation of the ring and little fingers and wasting of the hypothenar muscles are descriptive of lesion of the ulnar nerve before its division into the deep and superficial branches at the wrist.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Nail bed of index finger is supplied by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Ulnar nerve . The ulnar nerve provides motor innervation to part of the forearm and majority of the hand. It supplies sensory cutaneous innervation to the medial forearm, medial wrist, and medial one and one-half digits.
• Option C. Palmar branch of median nerve . It pierces the palmar carpal ligament, and divides into lateral and medial branches. The lateral branch supplies the skin over the ball of the thumb. The medial branch supplies the skin of the palm.
• Option D. Palmar branch of ulnar nerve . The palmar branch of ulnar nerve divides into superficial and deep branches. The superficial branch supplies the Palmaris brevis, and the skin on the ulnar side of the hand. The deep branch supplies the three short muscles of the little finger, all the Interossei and the third and fourth Lumbricals: the Adductor pollicis and the medial head of the Flexor pollicis brevis.
• Educational objective:
• The nail bed of the index finger is supplied by the median nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest cause of “Wrist Drop” is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Intramuscular injection . A delayed complication of intramuscular injections is the axillary nerve injury that presents as the weakness of the shoulder abduction from 15-90⁰.
• Option C. Dislocation of elbow . Posterior dislocation of the elbow can injure the ulnar or median nerves. This may present as ulnar claw hand or ape thumb deformities of the hand.
• Option D. Dislocation of shoulder . Whilst all nerves of the brachial plexus are at risk of injury during glenohumeral dislocation, the most commonly injured is the axillary nerve.
• Educational objective :
• The commonest cause of wrist drop deformity of the hand is the fracture of the shaft of the humerus, which causes radial nerve injury and the consequent deformity.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tardy ulnar nerve palsy is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Common peroneal nerve is related to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Shaft of tibia. The shaft of the tibia is not directly related to the common peroneal nerve. The tibia is a separate bone from the fibula, and the common peroneal nerve primarily courses along the fibula.
• Option C. Lower tibiofibular joint. Although the common peroneal nerve is in proximity to the lower tibiofibular joint, it does not have a direct anatomical relationship with this joint. The nerve primarily runs along the fibular neck and head.
• Option D. Shaft of fibula. While the common peroneal nerve courses along the fibula, it typically does not relate directly to the shaft of the fibula. Instead, it primarily wraps around the neck and head of the fibula.
• Educational objective :
• The common peroneal nerve runs along the neck of the fibula. It curves around the neck of the fibula and then branches into superficial and deep branches.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 45-year-old carpenter with a blunt trauma to his arm sustained a fracture following which he developed wrist drop, loss of extension at fingers and loss of sensations on the lateral aspect of the wrist joint. Which of the following is true?
options: Patient has an injury to the median nerve
He should have also lost extension of the forearm
Patient has injured the radial nerve in the spiral groove
There is combined involvement of the radial nerve and median nerve

explanation.short: Ans. C) Patient has injured the radial nerve in the spiral groove
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Patient has an injury to the median nerve. False. In the given scenario, the patient sustained blunt trauma followed by fracture which caused wrist drop, loss of finger extension and sensations on lateral aspect of wrist joint. The median nerve can also be injured from blunt trauma or compression neuropathy. The motor symptoms includes loss of ability to abduct and oppose the thumb (ape thumb deformity), weak forearm pronation, and wrist and finger flexion. Sensory loss appears in the thumb, index finger, middle finger and radial aspect of the ring finger.
• Option B. He should also lost extension of the forearm. False. Loss of extension of the forearm typically occurs when there is involvement of the radial nerve at a higher level in the arm, such as in the axilla or proximal to the elbow. Injuries to the radial nerve at higher levels can affect the triceps muscle, which is responsible for extending the forearm at the elbow joint. Therefore, patients commonly present with weakness or paralysis of forearm extension in addition to wrist drop. However, in the scenario provided, the patient's symptoms are consistent with a radial nerve injury in the spiral groove of the humerus, which primarily affects the wrist extensor muscles and sensation in the dorsal aspect of the hand and lateral aspect of the wrist joint. Injuries at this level typically spare the triceps muscle, preserving forearm extension.
• Option D. There is combined involvement of the radial nerve and median nerve. False. While it is possible to sustain injuries to multiple nerves simultaneously, such as in cases of complex trauma or nerve compression syndromes, the symptoms described in the scenario are more consistent with isolated radial nerve injury. There is no mention of symptoms that would suggest median nerve involvement, such as weakness in thumb opposition or flexion of the wrist and fingers.
• Educational objective :
• In the given scenario, there is history of blunt trauma to the arm causing fracture with wrist drop, loss of finger extension, and loss of sensation on lateral aspect of wrist. These symptoms are suggestive of radial nerve injury at the spiral/radial groove of the humerus due to the fracture of the humerus shaft from the trauma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In a road traffic accident, a patient was lying in right lateral position with bruise on face, elbow and lateral side of knee. Which nerve injury has maximum chances in this position of the victim?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Trigeminal nerve . The trigeminal nerve primarily innervates the face, providing sensory information for the forehead, cheeks, and jaw. However, the bruises on the face may not necessarily indicate trigeminal nerve injury, as these bruises could be due to impact trauma without direct nerve involvement.
• Option B. Ulnar nerve . The ulnar nerve innervates the muscles of the forearm and hand, providing sensation to the little finger and part of the ring finger. Bruising on the elbow may suggest injury in the vicinity of the ulnar nerve, but this position alone doesn't necessarily predispose the ulnar nerve to maximum chances of injury.
• Option D. Tibial nerve . The tibial nerve is also a branch of the sciatic nerve and innervates the muscles in the posterior compartment of the lower leg and foot. It provides sensation to the sole of the foot. While it's possible for the tibial nerve to be injured in a road traffic accident, the described bruising locations are not typically associated with the area innervated by the tibial nerve.
• Educational objective :
• Given the scenario and the positioning of the bruises, Common peroneal nerve injury is the most likely nerve injury. The lateral side of the knee is a common location for injury to this nerve due to its superficial course around the fibular head, making it prone to compression or trauma in accidents involving lateral knee impact.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Carpal tunnel syndrome is seen in all except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Pregnancy. During pregnancy, hormones cause fluid retention, and softens the ligament that forms the roof of the tunnel. When this happens, the nerve running through the tunnel may become squashed which can cause symptoms like pins and needles, numbness and stiff painful hands.
• Option B. Alcoholism. Chronic alcoholism can lead to various neurological conditions. Alcohol drinking is related to the potential risk of development of carpal tunnel syndrome.
• Option C. Colles’ fracture. Colles' fracture is a type of fracture of the distal radius bone in the forearm. While Colles' fracture itself doesn't directly cause carpal tunnel syndrome, the subsequent immobilization or malunion of the fracture may lead to changes in the wrist anatomy or increased pressure on the median nerve, potentially predisposing to carpal tunnel syndrome.
• Educational objective :
• Carpal tunnel syndrome is seen in pregnancy, alcoholism and as a complication of malunited Colles’ fracture. It is not directly related to the Smith’s fracture.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cubital tunnel syndrome involves:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Median nerve. The median nerve is related to the carpal tunnel syndrome. It is caused by the compression of the nerve in the flexor retinaculum at the wrist.
• Option C. Tibial nerve. The tibial nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and innervates muscles in the posterior compartment of the leg and the sole of the foot. It is not associated with cubital tunnel syndrome.
• Option D. Common peroneal nerve. The common peroneal nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and innervates muscles in the anterior compartment of the leg and the dorsum of the foot. It is not involved in cubital tunnel syndrome.
• Educational objective :
• Cubital tunnel syndrome involves the entrapment of the ulnar nerve at the elbow, leading to symptoms like tingling, numbness and weakness in the hand and 4 th -5 th digits.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Investigation of choice for entrapment neuropathy is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. CT scan . CT scans can provide detailed images of bone structures and some soft tissues. They are not typically used as the primary investigation for entrapment neuropathy. CT scans are more useful for assessing bony abnormalities or injuries but may not show nerve entrapment as clearly as other imaging modalities.
• Option B. Clinical examination . Clinical examination is essential for diagnosing any medical condition, including entrapment neuropathy. However, while clinical examination can provide valuable information about symptoms, signs, and physical findings, it may not always pinpoint the exact location or severity of nerve entrapment. Therefore, it is usually supplemented by other diagnostic tests.
• Option C. Ultrasonography . Ultrasonography can be useful in evaluating soft tissue structures, including nerves, tendons, and muscles. It is a non-invasive and relatively inexpensive imaging modality. While it can sometimes identify nerve entrapment, it may not always provide sufficient detail to confirm the diagnosis definitively.
• Educational objective :
• The investigation of choice for the diagnosis of entrapment neuropathy are the EMG and NCV studies as they describe the precise location and severity of the entrapment of the nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient with leprosy presents with clumsiness of hand. His ulnar nerve is affected. Clumsiness is due to palsy of which muscle?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Extensor carpi ulnaris . The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle is located in the forearm and is responsible for extending and adducting the wrist. Although it is innervated by the ulnar nerve, palsy of this muscle typically results in weakness or inability to perform these movements, rather than causing clumsiness of the hand.
• Option B. Abductor pollicis brevis . The abductor pollicis brevis muscle is located in the thenar eminence of the hand and is responsible for abducting the thumb at the carpometacarpal joint. It is innervated by the median nerve, not the ulnar nerve. Therefore, this muscle will not be involved in the scenario.
• Option C. Opponens pollicis . The opponens pollicis muscle is located in the thenar eminence of the hand. Its main function is to oppose the thumb, bringing it towards the other fingers. Although it is innervated by the median nerve, it will not be affected in the given scenario.
• Educational objective :
• The clumsiness of the hand occurs due to loss of fine movements of the hand and fingers. The fine movements are controlled by the interossei of the hand. Since, in the given case scenario, ulnar nerve is affected from leprosy, thus there will be an effect on the interossei, leading to clumsiness of the hand.

subject: Orthopedics
question: For Carpal Tunnel Syndrome, test used is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Finkelstein test: It is done for De-Quervain tenosynovitis.
• Option C. Cozen’s Test : It is done for Tennis Elbow.
• Option D. Thompson Test: It is done to check integrity of Achilles Tendon.
• Educational objective :
• The tests done for carpal tunnel syndrome includes: Tinel’s sign Phalen’s test Reverse Phalen’s test Durkan’s test

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following will not take place in a patient with ulnar nerve injury in arm?
options: Sensory loss over medial aspect of hand

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is used as a substitute for wrist extensors in radial nerve palsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Palmaris longus . The palmaris longus is the most commonly used tendon for transfer/grafts. It is used for the substitution of extensor pollicis longus. But pronator teres is the preferred choice for wrist extensor substitution after radial nerve palsy.
• Option C. Flexor digitorum superficialis . It is substituted for extensor digitorum communis in the middle finger, and extensor pollicis longus and extensor indicis in the ring finger.
• Option D. Flexor digitorum profundus . The flexor digitorum profundus muscle is also located in the forearm and is responsible for flexing the distal phalanges of the fingers. Similar to the flexor digitorum superficialis, it primarily acts as a finger flexor and also assists in flexing the wrist. However, it does not directly extend the wrist and would not serve as a substitute for wrist extensors in radial nerve palsy.
• Educational objective :
• The primary substitute muscle used for wrist extensor in radial nerve palsy is the pronator teres muscle.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Extension of metacarpophalangeal joint of the hand is lost in injury to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Median nerve. Motor function of the median nerve are mainly flexor aspect of forearm, hand, and thumb. It innervates all the superficial muscles on the front of the forearm except the Flexor Carpi Ulnaris.
• Option B. UInar nerve. Weakness may start from clumsiness and loss of hand dexterity. This may then progress to a decrease in handgrip and pinch strength (Froment sign). In more severe cases, there may be atrophy of the hand intrinsic muscles and clawing of the fourth and the fifth digits, classically known as claw hand.
• Option C. Anterior interosseous nerve. Anterior interosseous neuropathy is a form of median nerve injury. The anterior interosseous branch of the median nerve moves into the anterior forearm. It innervates the flexor pollicis longus, pronator quadratus, and the deep flexors of 2 nd and 3 rd digits. There is no cutaneous branch; thus, the neuropathy presents with muscle weakness, and no sensory deficits. On physical examination, this can appear as the inability to approximate the thumb and index finger. Patients are unable to pinch objects or make an "OK" sign with their index finger and thumb.
• Educational objective :
• Extension of the MCP joint of the hand is lost in the injury to the posterior interosseous nerve, a branch of the radial nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following nerve is involved in ‘pronator teres syndrome’?

subject: Orthopedics
question: “Ulnar paradox” is related with:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Ulnar half of FDP
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lumbricals. The lumbrical muscles are small intrinsic muscles of the hand that arise from the tendons of the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) muscle. They flex the metacarpophalangeal joints and extend the interphalangeal joints of the fingers. However, they are not directly associated with the "ulnar paradox."
• Option B. Intrinsic muscle. Intrinsic muscles are responsible for fine motor movements and stability of the fingers. While ulnar nerve dysfunction can affect the intrinsic muscles of the hand, the term "ulnar paradox" specifically refers to a phenomenon related to the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) muscle.
• Option C. Extensor pollicis longus. The extensor pollicis longus muscle is located in the forearm and is responsible for extending the thumb at the interphalangeal joint. It is not directly related to the "ulnar paradox."
• Educational objective :
• The ulnar paradox is related with the ulnar half of the FDP muscle, leading to inability of DIP joint flexion of the ring and little fingers.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
Action of lumbricals at this joint is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/09/marked-area.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Extension . The extensor pollicis brevis, extensor indicis proprius, extensor digitorum communis, and extensor digiti minimi extend the MCP joints.
• Option C. Adduction . Adduction of the thumb is produced by the adductor pollicis, while the adduction of the other four digits is produced by palmar interossei muscles.
• Option D. Abduction . Abduction of the thumb is produced by the abductor pollicis longus and abductor pollicis brevis; abduction of the fifth digit is produced by the abductor digiti minimi; abduction of fingers 2-5 is produced by the dorsal interossei muscles.
• Educational objective:
• The marked joint in the hand is the MCP joint of the index finger. The lumbricals function to perform the flexion at the MCP joint and extension of the PIP and DIP joints.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Damage to median nerve produces:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Claw hand . Claw Hand is a hand deformity with hyperextension of the metacarpophalengeal (MCP) joints and flexion of the interphalengeal (IP) joints due to weakness of the intrinsic muscles of the hand. The most common cause is the ulnar nerve injury. Complete claw hand involves all the digits and results from both Ulnar and Median Nerve Palsy. Incomplete or Partial claw hand involves only ulnar 2 digits and is due to isolated Ulnar Nerve Palsy.
• Option B. Winging of scapula . In a patient with a winged scapula, the medial or lateral aspect of the scapula appears to be protruding from the back. The most common etiology of a winged scapula is usually due to damage or impaired innervation to the serratus anterior muscle. The nerve that innervates this muscle is the long thoracic nerve. Some patients may also complain of an inability to raise the affected arm above their head. This dysfunction results in the medial border of the scapula giving a winged-like appearance.
• Option D. Wrist drop. Wrist drop is a disorder caused by radial nerve palsy. Because of the radial nerve's innervation of the extensor muscles of the wrist and digits, those whose radial nerve function has been compromised cannot actively extend them. As such, the hand hangs flaccidly in a position of flexion when the patient attempts to bring the arm to a horizontal position.
• Educational objective:
• Damage to the median nerve produces ape thumb deformity of the hand where the thumb is unable to abduct and oppose and remains in the same plane as the fingers.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Labourer’s Nerve is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ulnar nerve. It is also known as musician’s nerve.
• Educational objective:
• The median nerve is also known as labourer’s nerve because it performs the coarse movements of the hand that are required by the labourers.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Compression of a nerve within the carpal tunnel produces inability to:
options: Flex the distal phalanx of the thumb

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Abduct the thumb . Due to low median nerve palsy i.e., compression of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel, there is weakness of the thumb abduction and opposition. Abduction of the thumb is controlled by the abductor pollicis brevis and longus muscles which are innervated by both median and radial nerves respectively. If the median nerve is compressed, then the thumb abduction movement can also be performed by the abductor pollicis longus muscle which is supplied by the radial nerve. Thus, due to median nerve compression, thumb abduction may become weak but will not be lost completely.
• Option B. Adduct the thumb . This movement is primarily controlled by the adductor pollicis muscle, which is innervated by the ulnar nerve. Carpal tunnel syndrome typically does not affect the adduction of the thumb directly.
• Option C. Flex the distal phalanx of the thumb . The flexion of the distal phalanx of the thumb is controlled by the flexor pollicis longus muscle which is supplied by the anterior interosseous nerve branch of the median nerve above the carpal tunnel. Thus, this movement is not affected by the median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel.
• Educational objective:
• Due to median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel, the main movement lost is the opposition of the thumb, which characteristically present as the ape thumb deformity of the hand.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Median nerve is injured during: Elbow dislocation Fracture lateral epicondyle of humerus Fracture medial epicondyle of humerus Supracondylar fracture of humerus

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• 2. Fracture lateral epicondyle of humerus: False. The radial nerve (or its deep branch, the posterior interosseous nerve) is more closely related to the lateral epicondyle, not the median nerve. Median nerve runs medially, and is not typically affected by lateral epicondyle injuries.
• 3. Fracture medial epicondyle of humerus: False. Ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle, and is the nerve at risk in such fractures. The median nerve is not near the medial epicondyle, so it is not typically injured here.
• Educational objective:
• The common etiology of the median nerve injury are the elbow dislocation and supracondylar fracture of humerus.

subject: Orthopedics
