question: A patient complaint of flexion of interphalangeal joints and hyperextension of MCP of hands. He is most likely suffering from:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Posterior Interosseous Nerve palsy. PIN palsy involved weakness of metacarpal extension, wrist extension in neutral or ulnar deviation. The wrist will extend with radial deviation due to intact ECRL (radial nerve) and absent ECU (PIN).
• Option B. Dupuytren’s contracture. Dupuytren disease starts as a palpable nodule in the palm, usually at the distal palmar crease. The nodules enlarge into cords and present with palpable cords along the palm. As the cords thicken and shorten, they cause fixed flexion contractures of the fingers at the MCP and PIP joints. At this stage, there is loss of range of motion of the hand and palpable cords in the palm extending into the affected digits. The 4th digit is most often affected, followed by the 5th digit. In most cases, palpation of the nodules does not elicit pain unless the ulnar nerve is compressed.
• Option D. Erb’s palsy. The neonate presents with the arm hanging limply from the shoulder with adduction at the shoulder, internal rotation of the upper arm, pronation of the forearm, and an outward direction of the palm, giving the appearance of a " waiter's tip posture ." The arm cannot be raised from the side, and all power of flexion of the elbow is lost due to the paralysis of the biceps muscle.
• Educational objective:
• The presenting complaint of IP joint flexion and MCP joint hyperextension is suggestive of presence of claw hand caused by ulnar nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Knuckle-Bender splint is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Median nerve injury. In median nerve injury, wrist splint is more commonly used. Knuckle bender can also be used, but not commonly than a wrist splint.
• Option B. Radial nerve injury. For radial nerve injury, a cock-up splint is used.
• Option D. Erb’s palsy. In brachial plexus injury, aeroplane splint is used.
• Educational objective:
• The knuckle bender splint is used commonly for ulnar nerve injury more than median nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Lumbricals palsy causes:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Erb’s palsy. In erb’s palsy, there is paralysis of brachialis, biceps brachii, deltoid, rotator cuff, and supinator muscles. This leads to a characteristic policeman’s tip deformity of the limb where the shoulder is adducted and medially rotated and the forearm is extended and pronated.
• Option C. Mallet finger. Mallet Finger is a finger deformity caused by disruption of the terminal extensor tendon distal to DIP joint. This leads to loss of active DIP extension.
• Option D. Hammer toe. Hammer Toe is a lesser toe deformity characterized by PIP flexion, DIP extension and MTP slight extension. It is caused due to plantar plate injury/over pull of EDL/intrinsic imbalance/rheumatoid arthritis.
• Educational objective:
• Lumbricals palsy is caused by the ulnar nerve palsy, which leads to claw hand, more pronounced in the 4 th and 5 th digits.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Following an incised wound in the front of wrist, the subject is unable to oppose the tips of the little finger and the thumb. The nerve(s) involved is/are

explanation.short: Ans. C) Median and ulnar nerves
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ulnar nerve alone. The ulnar nerve innervates the intrinsic muscles of the hand, including those responsible for fine motor movements and grip strength in the ulnar aspect of the hand. While the ulnar nerve does not directly control opposition of the thumb, it does play a role in stabilizing the hand during this movement. Additionally, it controls movements of the little finger. So, while it is involved in the scenario described, it alone does not fully account for the inability to oppose the thumb and little finger simultaneously.
• Option B. Median nerve alone. The median nerve innervates several muscles in the forearm and hand, including the muscles of the thumb. It is primarily responsible for opposition of the thumb. However, the median nerve alone does not directly control opposition of the little finger. Thus, median nerve alone cannot account for the inability of the opposition of both the thumb and the little finger simultaneously.
• Option D. Radial and ulnar nerves. The radial nerve primarily innervates the extensor muscles in the forearm and hand, controlling movements such as wrist extension and finger extension. While the ulnar nerve controls movements of the little finger, the radial nerve does not directly contribute to the opposition of either the thumb or little finger.
• Educational objective :
• The median nerve is responsible for the inability to oppose the thumb, while the ulnar nerve is likely contributing to the inability to oppose the little finger. Thus, in the given scenario, there is injury to both the median and ulnar nerves leading to inability of the opposition of little finger and thumb.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 24-year-old male presents with a humerus fracture as shown in the figure below. Which is the nerve likely to be involved by the fracture?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/09/spiral-fracture-humerus.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ulnar nerve. It is injured in the fracture of the medial condyle of the humerus.
• Option C. Median nerve. The median nerve can be injured in the supracondylar humerus fracture.
• Option D. Musculocutaneous nerve. This nerve is injured more commonly in shoulder dislocation, after axillary nerve.
• Educational objective :
• The given x-ray image shows the humerus shaft fracture. It is associated with radial nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient can’t extend his wrist after he has met with an accident. He has no sensory loss. Level at which the affected nerve is injured is:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Spiral groove of humerus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Head of radius . The radial nerve passes near the head of the radius. An injury here is more likely to cause pain and affect forearm supination rather than a complete wrist drop without sensory loss.
• Option C. Near medial epicondyle . The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Injury around this area may lead to ulnar claw hand deformity.
• Option D. Surgical neck of humerus . At this level, axillary nerve is affected. Axillary nerve paralysis may cause weakness of shoulder abduction and loss of sensation in the lower half of the deltoid area.
• Educational Objective :
• In the given case, the level affected is the spiral groove of the humerus, from where the radial nerve passes. Thus, there will be symptoms of wrist drop in the patient without any sensory loss.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Extensor Carpi Radialis Longus is
options: Extensor and ulnar deviator of the wrist
Extensor and radial deviator of the wrist
Injured in posterior interosseous nerve injury

explanation.short: Ans. B) Extensor and radial deviator of the wrist
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Extensor and ulnar deviator of the wrist . The extensor carpi radialis longus is the prime extensor of the wrist, but it is not the ulnar deviator. The ulnar deviation is performed by the extensor carpi ulnaris muscle, while the extensor carpi radialis longus muscle performs the radial deviation of the wrist.
• Option C. Injured in posterior interosseous nerve injury . Due to PIN injury, the ulnar wrist extensors and the MCP joint extensors are compromised. The most commonly affected muscle is extensor carpi ulnaris.
• Option D. Weak extensor of wrist . The extensor carpi ulnaris is generally considered as the weak extensor of the wrist.
• Educational objective :
• Extensor carpi radialis longus is the prime extensor and radial deviator of the wrist.

subject: Orthopedics
question: High stepping gait is seen in:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Common peroneal nerve palsy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. CTEV. CTEV (Congenital Talipes Equinovarus), commonly known as clubfoot , is a congenital condition where the foot is twisted inward and downward. This condition typically presents with a rigid foot deformity, making it difficult for the individual to walk normally. However, it typically manifest as a "in-toeing gait."
• Option C. Polio. Polio is a viral infectious disease that affects the nervous system, particularly motor neurons. It can lead to muscle weakness and paralysis, often asymmetrically. However, polio-induced gait abnormalities usually involve weakness or paralysis of specific muscle groups. It manifests as a "hand-to-knee gait." The gait is a sum of quadriceps weakness and fixed knee flexion deformity.
• Option D. Cerebral palsy. There are 4 gait patterns seen in spastic hemiplegic CP. The type 1 presents with a foot drop due to weak dorsiflexors. Type 2 presents with true foot equinus due to spasticity/contracture of gastrosoleus. Type 3 presents with stiff knee gait due to hamstring/quadriceps co-contraction.
• Educational objective :
• High stepping gait is seen in common peroneal nerve injury. This occurs as a result of foot drop from weakness of the foot dorsiflexors.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Injury to the common peroneal nerve at the lateral aspect of head of fibula results in all of the following except:
options: Sensory impairment on lateral aspect of leg extending to the dorsum of foot

explanation.short: Ans. C) Loss of ankle reflex

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 25-year-old lady sustained a lacerated wound on the back of right thigh by a horn of a bull. The wound was sutured. Two months later she developed foot drop and an ulcer on the dorsum of the foot. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: Chronic ischemia to limbs due to popliteal artery injury

explanation.short: Ans. B) Partial injury to sciatic nerve
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chronic ischemia to limbs due to popliteal artery injury . Popliteal artery entrapment syndrome (PAES) is a very rare pathology that can cause lower extremity ischemia in healthy young people. Chronic critical limb ischemia is manifested by pain at rest, non-healing wounds and gangrene. Ischemic rest pain is typically described as a burning pain in the arch or distal foot that occurs while the patient is recumbent but is relieved when the patient returns to a position in which the feet are dependent. Gangrene is usually found on the toes.
• Option C. Complete injury of sciatic nerve . Sciatic nerve injury occurs due to trauma (pressure, stretching or cutting) to the nerve and can cause symptoms such as paresthesias, loss of muscle power and pain. Trauma like hip dislocation and acetabular fracture; Iatrogenic causes like Direct surgical trauma, Faulty positioning during anaesthesia, Injection of neurotoxic substances, Tourniquets, Dressings, casts or faulty fitting orthotics, and Radiation. The common symptoms are pain and abnormal gait pattern, External rotation and abduction contracture of the hip, Equinovarus or Equinus deformity, Muscular weakness/atrophy, and Motor and sensory deficit such as paraesthesia and numbness.
• Option D. Injury to hamstring muscles . Hamstring injuries most commonly occur at the myotendinous junction in running athletes as a result of sudden hip flexion and knee extension. The patient may feel a sudden pain in the posterior thigh during running, kicking or jumping activity. Occasionally, a "pop" felt. Common symptoms includes posterior thigh pain, hamstring tightness and pain with sitting. There may be a palpable mass in middle 1/3 of posterior thigh. The patient walks with a "stiff-legged" gait (avoiding knee and hip flexion). The patient may also have peroneal nerve weakness.
• Educational objective :
• The given case scenario describes the presentation of foot drop and ulcers on the dorsum of the foot. These are suggestive of partial injury to the sciatic nerve with some injury to the common peroneal nerve because the presentation is that of foot drop and ulcer on foot dorsum. The common peroneal nerve injury leads to foot drop due to motor loss to foot dorsiflexors, sensory loss at the lateral side of the leg and dorsum of the foot. Thus, in the given case, the patient had partial injury to the sciatic nerve (or injury to the common peroneal nerve).

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common cause of carpal tunnel syndrome is:
options: Occupational – excessive use of vibration instruments

subject: Orthopedics
question: Meralgia Paresthetica is due to involvement of:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Ilioinguinal nerve. Ilioinguinal neuralgia is a frequent cause of pain in the lower abdomen and the upper thigh and is commonly caused by entrapment or injury of the nerve after lower abdominal surgeries. The ilioinguinal nerve is responsible for motor innervation of the transverse abdominis and internal oblique muscles. It provides sensory innervation to a region of the upper anteromedial thigh. In males, it also supplies sensory innervation to the anterior surface of the scrotum and root of the penis and in females, supplies sensory innervation to the labia majora and mons pubis. Male or female patients with ilioinguinal neuralgia may complain of pain, paresthesia and abnormal sensation in the area supplied by the nerve.
• Option C. Genitofemoral nerve . Genitofemoral neuralgia is a cause of neuropathic pain that is characterized by chronic neuropathic groin pain that is localized along the distribution of the genitofemoral nerve. The symptoms include groin pain, paresthesias, and burning sensation spreading from the lower abdomen to the medial aspect of the thigh. It may present with scrotal pain in male, while females experience symptoms radiating to the labia majora and mons pubis. Genitofemoral neuropathy has been attributed to iatrogenic nerve injury during inguinal and femoral herniorrhaphy.
• Option D. Saphenous nerve . Compression of the saphenous nerve typically occurs at the adductor canal or at the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve as it courses near the medial femoral condyle. Patients typically complain of vague dull or achy pain located in the area of the anteromedial knee, with burning or other neuropathic qualities. The pain is often reported as worse with stairs, prolonged walking, and sitting.
• Educational objective :
• Meralgia paresthetica is caused by the compression of the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh. It is characterized by paresthesia in the upper lateral side of the thigh. The other entrapment syndromes caused by compression of nerves are described below:

subject: Orthopedics
question:
In a patient with history of trauma of forearm and X-ray showing fracture of proximal part of medial bone with dislocation of proximal part of lateral bone, the muscles which may be paralyzed are

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/09/montaggia-fracture.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Flexor carpi ulnaris . This muscle is innervated by the ulnar nerve. It flexes and adducts the wrist. This muscle’s tendon is susceptible to involvement in the Volkmann’s ischemic contracture.
• Option B. Adductor pollicis . This muscle is also innervated by the ulnar nerve (deep branch). It functions in the adduction of the thumb, helpful in pinching and gripping movements. A ganglion, fracture of the hook of the hamate, or hypertrophic scars caused by repeated microtrauma are the most common causes of compression of the deep motor branch of the ulnar nerve, leading to adductor pollicis paralysis.
• Option D. Opponens pollicis . It is innervated by the median nerve. It performs the opposition movement of the thumb i.e., it allows the tip of the thumb to touch the tip of the fingers.
• Educational objective :
• The x-ray description of the patient in the question is of Monteggia fracture-dislocation. Due to monteggia fracture, the nerve most commonly affected is the posterior interosseous nerve. This nerve supplied the extensor pollicis longus muscle which controls the extension of the little finger. So, in this case, the muscle paralysed is the extensor pollicis longus.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient came with complaints of difficulty in adducting his fingers and flexion of extended metacarpophalangeal joint. Muscles that are paralyzed are:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Lumbricals. The paralysis of the lumbricals of the hand causes the weakness of the MCP joint flexion and PIP and DIP joint extension.
• Option C. Flexor digitorum profundus. Paralysis of this muscle weakens the flexion of the terminal phalanx and the wrist.
• Option D. Flexor carpi radialis. Its paralysis can cause the weakness of wrist flexion and abduction.
• Educational objective:
• Difficulty in adducting the fingers is noticed when the interossei are paralysed. This can be tested by positive card test (patient unable to hold the card firmly between the fingers).
• Since there is weakness in adduction, the fingers will appear abducted. The abducted position of the little finger appears as Wartenberg’s sign .

subject: Orthopedics
question: In fracture of distal half of humerus, the nerve injured is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Axillary nerve . Axillary nerve is injured in the fracture of the surgical neck of humerus, anterior and inferior shoulder dislocations, and from intramuscular injections in the deltoid region.
• Option B. Median nerve . Median nerve is injured in elbow dislocation, wrist dislocation, supracondylar humerus fracture, and in carpal tunnel syndrome.
• Option D. Ulnar nerve . The fracture of medial epicondyle of the humerus is the most common cause of ulnar nerve injury as the nerve passes just behind the medial epicondyle.
• Educational objective :
• In the fracture of the distal half of the humerus, involving the spiral groove, the most commonly injured nerve is the radial nerve as the nerve crosses the bone at the distal half of the shaft.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Dorsum of 1st web space of hand is an autonomous zone for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Anterior interosseous nerve. This nerve provides sensory supply to the carpal bones of the wrist.
• Option C. Posterior interosseous nerve. This nerve provides sensory fibers to the dorsal aspect of the wrist capsule. It does not provide cutaneous branches.
• Option D. Median nerve. The autonomous zone for the median nerve are the tip of the index finger and tip of the thumb.
• Educational objective :
• The radial nerve’s autonomous zone in the hand is the 1 st dorsal web space of the hand i.e., the anatomical snuff box.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tinel’s sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Avascular necrosis of scaphoid. Avascular necrosis of the scaphoid is a condition where the blood supply to the scaphoid bone in the wrist is compromised, leading to bone tissue death. While avascular necrosis of the scaphoid can cause wrist pain and dysfunction, Tinel's sign is not typically associated with this condition.
• Option B. Kienbock’s disease. Kienbock's disease is a condition where the blood supply to the lunate bone in the wrist is disrupted, leading to lunate bone necrosis. Similar to avascular necrosis of the scaphoid, Tinel's sign is not typically associated with Kienbock's disease.
• Option C. 1 st carpometacarpal joint arthritis. Arthritis affecting the first carpometacarpal joint can cause pain, swelling, and decreased range of motion in the thumb. While arthritis can lead to joint compression and nerve irritation, Tinel's sign is not commonly elicited in cases of first carpometacarpal joint arthritis.
• Educational objective :
• Tinel’s sign is a provocative test to evaluate the sensations in a nerve. Out of the given choices, the only nerve-related condition is the carpal tunnel syndrome, rest are the vascular and arthritic conditions. Thus, Tinel’s sign is positive in carpal tunnel syndrome, as in, when the median nerve is tapped or percussed along its course, it produces tingling sensation in the wrist and hand.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
Identify the nerve supply of the marked muscle.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/09/marked-muscle.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Radial nerve . It innervates the triceps, anconeus, extensor carpi radialis longus (ECRL), extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB), brachioradialis.
• Option C. Ulnar nerve . It gives motor innervation to the anterior compartment of the forearm, and hand. In the forearm, it supplies the FCU and medial FDP. In the hand, it supplies the hypothenar muscles, medial 2 lumbricals, adductor pollicis, palmar and dorsal interossei of hand.
• Option D. Anterior interosseous nerve . The anterior interosseous nerve supplies motor innervation to the deep compartment of the anterior forearm i.e., Flexor pollicis longus, Lateral half of flexor digitorum profundus (index and middle fingers), and Pronator quadratus.
• Educational objective:
• The marked muscle in the image is the 1 st lumbrical which is supplied by the median nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Adductor pollicis is supplied by:
options: Anterior Interosseous branch of median nerve

explanation.short: Ans. A) Deep branch of ulnar nerve
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Median nerve . The median nerve gives motor innervation to the superficial volar forearm group (pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus), intermediate group (FDS), deep group (lateral FDP, FPL, pronator quadratus), and hand (1-2 lumbricals, opponens pollicis, APB, flexor pollicis brevis).
• Option C. Superficial branch of ulnar nerve . The superficial branch of the ulnar nerve provides branches that innervate the hypothenar muscles (abductor digiti minimi, flexor digiti minimi brevis, and opponens digiti muscles), palmaris brevis muscle, and skin of palmar aspect of medial one and a half digits.
• Option D. Anterior interosseous branch of median nerve . The anterior interosseous nerve supplies motor innervation to the deep compartment of the anterior forearm i.e., Flexor pollicis longus, Lateral half of flexor digitorum profundus (index and middle fingers), and Pronator quadratus.
• Educational objective:
• The adductor pollicis muscle of the hand is supplied by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Ulnar paradox is seen in:
options: Combined median and ulnar nerve palsy
Guyon’s Canal entrapment of ulnar nerve

explanation.short: Ans. A) High ulnar nerve palsy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Low ulnar nerve palsy. Clawing is present with low ulnar nerve injury. There is clawing of ulnar 2 digits, finger cannot be completely extended. There is hyperextension of MCP joint, because of unopposed action of long extensors. There is no extension of IP joints, they remain flexed due to unopposed action of long flexors. Thus clawing occurs, with hyperextension of MCP joints & flexion of IP joints.
• Option C. Combined median and ulnar nerve palsy . When Low Median and Low Ulnar Nerve Lesions occur, there is loss of sensation in palm, loss of Intrinsic muscle function, loss of thumb opposition and adduction. When there is High Median and High Ulnar Nerve Lesions, there is hand anesthesia, and loss of flexors and intrinsics.
• Option D. Guyon’s canal entrapment of ulnar nerve. There is weakness/paralysis of the intrinsic muscles of the hand innervated by the ulnar nerve, which may present as a weakening of the handgrip and clawing of the fourth and fifth digits. Hypothenar atrophy may be present in more advanced cases.
• Educational objective:
• Ulnar paradox phenomenon is seen due to high ulnar nerve palsy i.e., when the ulnar nerve is injured at the level of elbow. This appears as clawing of the fingers, but the clawing is less severe than that of the low ulnar nerve palsy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Froment’s Sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Median nerve palsy. Benediction Hand Sign can be seen due to median nerve palsy when the patient is asked to oppose the tip of the thumb and the tip of the little finger. In the next step, the patient is asked to make a fist. Paralysis of the opponens pollicis makes it impossible to bring the tips of the thumb and little finger into opposition. Because of this opposition weakness, the patient cannot make a fist. This produces the deformity where only the ring and little fingers are flexed while the other digits remain extended. Paralysis of the opponens, abductor pollicis brevis, and flexor pollicis brevis muscles coupled with the antagonistic pull of the adductor pollicis causes the thumb to lie in the plane of the fingers, creating a deformity resembling an apes hand, and the patient is unable to oppose the thumb. This is called ape thumb deformity .
• Educational objective:
• Froment’s sign is seen in ulnar nerve palsy due to the paralysis of the adductor pollicis muscle.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Card test is done for testing the function of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lumbricals. Ask the patient to fully flex the fingers and at end range, ask the patient to tuck the fingertips tighter into flexion. There is characterized paradoxical extension of the IP joints while attempting to flex the fingers. This is known as Lumbrical Plus Finger.
• Option C. Dorsal interossei. Positive Egawa’s test is due to weakness of dorsal interossei, where side-to-side movements of middle finger becomes weak.
• Option D. Adductor pollicis. In case of ulnar nerve palsy , adductor pollicis supplied by ulnar nerve is paralyzed . So, patient holds the book between thumb and palm by using flexor pollicis longus (supplied by AIN nerve). A normal individual will be able to maintain a hold on the object without difficulty. With ulnar nerve palsy, the patient will experience difficulty maintaining a hold and this produces flexion at interphalangeal joint of the thumb , while holding book to maintain grip pressure causing a pinching effect . This is known as a positive froment’s sign .
• Educational objective:
• Card test is done to evaluate the palmar interossei muscle paralysis or weakness due to ulnar nerve palsy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tendon used for repair of flexor tendon injury in hand requiring reconstruction from forearm to fingertip is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Palmaris. The palmaris tendon is used for repair of flexor tendon injury from hand to the fingertip .
• Option C. Extensor digitorum communis. This muscle extends the forearm and the wrist. Thus, it is not used in flexor tendon injury repair.
• Option D. Extensor digiti minimi. This muscle is responsible for 5 th digit MCP joint extension. Thus, it is not used in flexor tendon injury repair.
• Educational objective :
• The plantaris tendon is used for flexor tendon injury repair from the forearm to the fingertip.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Crutch palsy is injury to which nerve?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Ulnar nerve . The ulnar nerve injury is related to the presence of claw hand deformity where there is flexion of the IP joint, hyperextension of the MCP joint due to paralysis of the lumbricals and the interossei.
• Option C. Median nerve . If the median nerve is injured at the elbow, then there are pointing index and benediction hand deformities. This involves flexion of the IP joint of index and middle fingers. If the nerve is injured at the wrist, this may appear as the ape thumb deformity wherein the thumb is adducted and laterally rotated and lies in the same plane as other fingers, due to unopposed action of the extensor pollicis longus and adductor pollicis.
• Option D. Musculocutaneous nerve . Isolated injury to the musculocutaneous nerve cause weakness in elbow/shoulder flexion, atrophy of the biceps brachii, and paraesthesia at the lateral forearm.
• Educational objective :
• Crutch palsy is caused by the injury to the radial nerve due to pressure from the axillary pad of the crutch on the axilla.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Wrist Drop is a feature of:

explanation.short: Ans. C) High radial nerve palsy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Superficial radial nerve. Injury to superficial radial nerve most commonly presents as loss of sensation in the dorsolateral aspect of the hand including the back of the hand, the thumb and the index finger. There may be numbness or reduced sensitivity to touch, pain and temperature.
• Option B. Posterior interosseous nerve palsy . Injury to posterior interosseous nerve most commonly leads to weakness or paralysis of the finger extensors leading to finger drop, leaving sensory functions intact .
• Option D. Avulsion of triceps tendon . Avulsion of triceps tendon classically presents as pain and swelling at the back of the elbow or upper forearm.
• Educational objective :
• Wrist drop is a feature of high radial nerve palsy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following nerve injury is most commonly associated with a humerus shaft fracture?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option B. Median nerve. The median nerve can be injured in the supracondylar humerus fracture.
• Option C. Axillary nerve. An Axillary nerve is injured in the surgical neck of humerus fracture.
• Option D. Ulnar nerve. It is injured in the fracture of the medial condyle of the humerus.
• Educational objective :
• With humerus shaft fracture, there is an association of the radial nerve injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Saturday night palsy involves which nerve?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Median nerve . Common injuries to the median nerves include anterior shoulder dislocation, elbow dislocation, humerus fracture, midshaft radius fractures, stab wounds, and prolonged placement of a tourniquet.
• Option C. Axillary nerve. Anterior shoulder dislocation is the most common cause of axillary nerve injury that can either compress the nerve or cause traction to it.
• Option D. Musculocutaneous nerve . Musculocutaneous nerve is usually injured by brachial plexus damage, compression injury involving repeated elbow flexion and forearm supination like weightlifting, shoulder dislocation, or entrapment of the nerve at the elbow. It is not involved in Saturday night palsy.
• Educational objective :
• Saturday night palsy involves compression neuropathy of the radial nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cock up splint is used in treatment of:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following statements about low Radial nerve palsy is not true?
options: Loss of nerve supply to brachioradialis
Loss of nerve supply to extensor carpi radialis brevis
Loss of nerve supply to extensor pollicis brevis
Loss of sensation over first dorsal web space

explanation.short: Ans. A) Loss of nerve supply to brachioradialis

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 30-year-old male underwent excision of the right radial head. Following surgery, the patient developed an inability to extend the fingers and thumb of the right hand. He did not have any sensory deficit. Which one of the following is the most likely cause?
options: Iatrogenic injury to common extensor origin

explanation.short: Ans. A) Injury to posterior interosseous nerve
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Iatrogenic injury to common extensor origin. The common extensor origin is a tendon attachment site for several muscles that extend the wrist and fingers. Injury to this area could lead to weakness or dysfunction in wrist and finger extension. However, in the scenario described in the question stem, the patient's inability to extend the fingers and thumb is more indicative of a specific nerve injury rather than a tendon injury.
• Option C. Injury to anterior interosseous nerve. The anterior interosseous nerve primarily innervates the deep muscles of the forearm, such as the flexor pollicis longus, flexor digitorum profundus to the index finger, and pronator quadratus. Damage to this nerve typically results in weakness or paralysis of these specific muscles, leading to difficulty with pinch grip and loss of flexion at the distal interphalangeal joints of the thumb and index finger. The presentation described in the question, with inability to extend the fingers and thumb, does not align with the distribution of the anterior interosseous nerve.
• Option D. High radial nerve palsy. High radial nerve palsy refers to a nerve injury occurring proximally along the radial nerve, usually at or above the level of the spiral groove of the humerus. This can result in weakness or paralysis of the triceps brachii, extensor muscles of the forearm, and wrist, finger, and thumb extension. However, the specific presentation described in the question stem (inability to extend the fingers and thumb) suggests a more localized nerve injury rather than a high radial nerve palsy, which would typically affect a broader distribution of muscles supplied by the radial nerve.
• Educational objective :
• The posterior interosseous nerve is injured commonly due to the radial head/neck fracture.
• Since the presenting symptoms of the patient describes an inability to extend the fingers and the thumb without sensory deficit, it is suggestive of posterior interosseous nerve injury as this nerve is pure motor and do not have any sensory component.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Injury to radial nerve in lower part of spiral groove:
options: Spares nerve supply to extensor carpi radialis longus
Results in paralysis of anconeus muscle
Leaves extension at elbow joint intact

explanation.short: Ans. C) Leaves extension at elbow joint intact
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Spares nerve supply to extensor carpi radialis longus. False. The extensor carpi radialis longus muscle is responsible for extending and abducting the wrist. However, in the radial nerve injury, there is loss of nerve supply to the extensor carpi radialis longus, leading to inability of wrist extension.
• Option B. Results in paralysis of anconeus muscle. False. The anconeus muscle assists in extension of the elbow joint. Paralysis of this muscle would indeed affect the extension at the elbow joint. In the radial nerve injury at the spiral groove, anconeus muscle is spared. This, thus does not affect the extension of the elbow, along with the triceps muscle.
• Option D. Weakens pronation movement. False. The radial nerve does not primarily control pronation; it primarily controls extension of the elbow, wrist, and fingers, and forearm supination. Injury to the radial nerve typically affects extension and supination movements rather than pronation.
• Educational objective :
• If the extension at the elbow joint remains intact despite the injury, it implies that the radial nerve's function in supplying the triceps brachii responsible for elbow extension is preserved.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following nerve injury leads to Foot Drop

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Tibial nerve . Injury to the tibial nerve can cause motor loss, altered sensation and pain in the popliteal fossa region if it is injured due to a space-occupying lesion, laceration, posterior knee dislocation, or fracture of tibia and fibula. At the medial malleolus level, the nerve can get compressed in the tarsal tunnel, leading to tarsal tunnel syndrome, presenting as pain and paresthesia in sole of the foot.
• Option C. Femoral nerve . Femoral nerve damage can occur from an injury or prolonged compression. Typically, damage and dysfunction of the femoral nerve are associated with the leg weakness and sensation changes. Injury of the femoral is uncommon.
• Option D. Obturator nerve . Obturator neuropathy may present as pain & paresthesia extending from hip to knee along the medial aspect of the thigh. Extension or lateral leg movement can increase pain. The patient m ay have trouble walking or experience leg weakness due to problems adducting the ipsilateral hip. There is weakness in the hip adductors on affected side, wasting of medial thigh, circumductory wide-based gait , sensory loss in the medial thigh, and ipsilateral loss of the hip adductor tendon reflex.
• Educational objective :
• The foot drop deformity is caused by the injury to the common peroneal nerve.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Foot drop result because of injury to: Superficial peroneal nerve Deep peroneal nerve Posterior tibial nerve Anterior tibial nerve

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• 1. Superficial peroneal nerve. This nerve innervates the muscles that evert the foot and also provides sensation to the top of the foot and toes. Injury to this nerve would not typically result in foot drop.
• 3. Posterior tibial nerve . This nerve innervates the muscles that invert the foot and provide sensation to the sole of the foot. Injury to this nerve would not typically result in foot drop.
• Educational objective :
• Both the deep peroneal nerve and the anterior tibial nerve gives motor supply to the muscles that dorsiflexes the foot. Thus, any injury to these nerves leads to a classical foot drop deformity.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are true regarding carpal tunnel syndrome except:
options: Pregnancy is the most common cause

explanation.short: Ans. A) Pregnancy is the most common cause

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not a cause for the development of carpal tunnel syndrome?

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is wrongly matched?
options: Secondaries of spine – Winking owl sign

explanation.short: Ans. B) Chondroblastoma – Soap bubble appearance
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteosarcoma – Sunray appearance.
• Option C. Ewing’s sarcoma – Onion peel appearance.
• Option D. Secondaries of spine – Winking owl sign.
• Educational objective:
• Chondroblastoma shows chicken wire calcification on biopsy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Radio-resistant bone tumor among the following are: Ewing’s Sarcoma Osteosarcoma Multiple myeloma Chondrosarcoma Lymphoma

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is/are true about Ewing sarcoma? Vascular origin Ewing’s sarcoma is second most common primary malignant bone tumor in children and adolescent after Osteosarcoma Metaphysis of long bone is most common site Fever and weight loss may be present Surgery is very useful in management

subject: Orthopedics
question:
X-ray proximal femur in a patient with pain in hip is given below. The deformity shown is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/12/picture22.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Blade of grass deformity. The blade of grass sign refers to the lucent leading edge in a long bone seen during the lytic phase of Paget disease of bone.
• Option C. Chicken wire appearance. Chicken wire calcification is classically seen in the chondroblastoma.
• Option D. Corduroy appearance. This is seen in the radiographic image of the spine in hemangioma of the bone, as thickened, vertically oriented trabeculae, giving a jailhouse appearance.
• Educational objective:
• The given image in the question shows the s hepherd crook deformity of the proximal femur , seen in case of fibrous dysplasia of the bone.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 30-year-old male presented with hip pain for last 6 months. Hip X-ray is as shown below. What is the likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/picture17.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Simple bone cyst. Unicameral bone cysts are most common in the proximal humerus and femur. The lesions are most active during skeletal growth and usually heal spontaneously at maturity. Unicameral bone cysts are often asymptomatic , unless a pathological fracture has occurred. Plain radiographs reveal a centrally located, purely lytic lesion with a well-marginated outline. Occasionally (20%), a thinned cortical fragment fractures and falls into the base of the lesion confirming its empty cystic nature. This “fallen fragment” sign is pathognomonic of a unicameral bone cyst, bone fragment, may also hinge around and move with fluid called as trap door sign.
• Fallen fragment (leaf) sign
• Option C. Adamantinoma. Adamantinomas are rare, low-grade malignant bone tumors that predominately affect the diaphyses and metaphyses of the tibia . The tumor is called adamantinoma due to its histological similarity to ameloblastoma, often found in the mandible. Histopathologically, adamantinoma displays biphasic patterns consisting of epithelial cells and osteofibrous components. On x-ray, it appears as a well-circumscribed, cortical tumor with a multilobulated, osteolytic pattern. Intralesional opacities, septation, and peripheral sclerosis may also be seen. These multifocal radiolucencies, surrounded by ring-shaped densities, produce the characteristic ''soap-bubble'' appearance . The lesion is typically confined within the intracortical region and extends longitudinally along the bone. However, in some cases, it can result in cortical destruction and invade the medullary cavity or adjacent periosteum and soft tissues. In such cases, a lamellar or solid periosteal reaction is typically associated with the lesion.
• Option D. Ewing’s sarcoma. Ewing sarcoma is the second most common (after osteosarcoma) in patients younger than 30 years of age. Classically, Ewing sarcoma appears radiographically as a destructive lesion in the diaphysis of a long bone with an “ onion skin” periosteal reaction . In reality, Ewing sarcoma more often originates in the metaphysis of a long bone, but frequently extends for a considerable distance into the diaphysis.
• Educational objective:
• The given x-ray in the question shows eccentric giant cell tumor of the proximal femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which among the following is/are metaphyseal tumor? Chondrosarcoma Enchondroma Giant cell tumor Ameloblastoma Osteosarcoma

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not a type of geographic lesion of bone?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fibrous dysplasia . It is a type 1A geographic lesion.
• Option B. Brodie’s abscess . It is also a geographic lytic lesion.
• Option C. Giant cell tumor . It is a type 1B geographic lesion.
• Educational objective :
• Ewing’s sarcoma is a type of a permeative lytic lesion which is poorly demarcated and numerous.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following childhood tumors most frequently metastasizes to the bone?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Ganglioneuroma . Ganglioneuroma is an uncommon neurogenic tumor arising from sympathetic ganglia. Most of the patients are older than 10 years . The posterior mediastinum is the main localization followed by the retroperitoneum and cervical region. This is a benign tumor. Benign tumors do not metastasize.
• Option C. Wilm’s tumor . Wilms tumor, or nephroblastoma, is the most common renal cancer in the pediatric age group. Wilms tumor is typically found in children younger than five years of age. The most common site of metastatic spread of Wilms tumor is the lungs (most frequent) and the liver. Bone metastases from Wilms' tumor are relatively rare.
• Option D. Ewing’s sarcoma . This is a primary bone tumor (or sometimes soft tissue tumor that invades bone). This means it originates in the bone itself. Therefore, it doesn't "metastasize to the bone" from a distant primary site in the same way. Ewing’s sarcoma mostly happens in children and young adults , although it can happen at any age. It most often begins in the leg bones and pelvis, but it can happen in any bone. It can involve the muscle and the soft tissues around the tumor as well. The cells can also metastasize to other areas of the body, including the bone marrow, lungs, kidneys, heart, adrenal glands and other soft tissues.
• Educational Objective:
• Neuroblastoma is the most common tumor in children that metastasize to the bone frequently. It is the most frequently-occurring extracranial childhood tumor, classified as an embryonal neuroendocrine tumor, originating from neural crest progenitor cells. Hence, it can occur anywhere along the sympathetic nervous system , including the superior cervical, paraspinal, and celiac ganglia; the majority arise in the adrenal glands. Neuroblastoma may metastasize to lymph nodes, bone marrow, liver, skin and bones . B one to bone metastasis is seen in O steosarcoma, N euroblastoma, E wing's Sarcoma [ B O N E ] (Maximum incidence of a particular tumor type).

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is true regarding osteoid osteoma?
options: The femur and tibia are the commonest bones involved
Radiologically appears as a radiolucent lesion surrounded by dense bone
It is a benign bone tumor, presents with severe pain that is typically relieved by aspirin

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above

subject: Orthopedics
question: Development of Chondrosarcomas is related with:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Both A and C
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Felty syndrome . Felty syndrome is an uncommon extra-articular manifestation of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) characterized by RA, neutropenia (ANC<1500 mm 3 ), and splenomegaly . HLA-DR4 has been noted to be present in >90% of Felty syndrome cases.
• Educational Objective:
• Both Maffucci syndrome and Ollier's disease are well-established conditions that predispose individuals to the development of chondrosarcomas due to the presence of multiple enchondromas.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
X-ray of Tibia of an adolescent boy is shown. Probable diagnosis is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/picture16.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteosarcoma. Radiographically, the p eriosteal reaction may be seen in the form of “ Codman’s triangle ”, or it may have a “ sunburst ” or “ hair on end ” appearance . The codman’s triangle is formed by the periosteal lifting. There is also new bone formation in the soft tissues along with permeative pattern of bone destruction.
• Option C. Bone cyst with fracture. Benign bone cysts generally do not cause pain, but if they are large enough, they can weaken the bone and cause it to fracture. This type of fracture is called a pathologic fracture . A classic radiographic finding is the fallen fragment sign which occurs when a piece of cortex breaks off after a fracture, and sinks to the gravity-dependent portion of the lesion.
• Fallen fragment (leaf) sign
• Option D. Fibrosis cystica. Cystic fibrosis ( CF ), also called mucoviscidosis, is an autosomal recessive genetic disease that affects the exocrine function of the lungs, liver, pancreas, small bowel, sweat glands, and the male genital system. The musculoskeletal manifestations of cystic fibrosis are uncommon. Musculoskeletal manifestations include low bone mineral density , short stature, kyphosis , cystic fibrosis arthropathy (~5%), hypertrophic osteoarthropathy (~5%), finger clubbing .
• Educational objective:
• The given image in the question shows a chondromyxoid fibroma of the tibia with lobulated or oval eccentric lytic lesion with well-defined sclerotic margin.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
What is the most likely diagnosis of the X-ray depicted below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/08/gct.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chondrosarcoma. It peaks between 40 and 60 years for primary chondrosarcoma and between 25 and 45 years for secondary chondrosarcoma. Chondrosarcoma can occur in any location; however, most are located in a proximal location such as the pelvis (most common site) , proximal femur, and proximal humerus. Although chondrosarcomas rarely occur in the hand, they are the most common malignant tumor of bone in this location. The radiographic appearance of chondrosarcoma lesion arising in the medullary cavity with irregular matrix calcification. The pattern of calcification has been described as “ punctate ”, “ popcorn ”, or “ comma-shaped ”. Chondrosarcoma has a more aggressive appearance with bone destruction, cortical erosions, periosteal reaction, and rarely a soft-tissue mass.
• Option C. Osteogenic sarcoma. Osteosarcoma is a tumor characterized by the production of osteoid matrix by malignant cells. It is the second most common primary malignancy of bone behind multiple myeloma. Onset can occur at any age; however, primary osteosarcoma occurs most commonly in the second decade of life. All skeletal locations can be affected; however, most primary osteosarcomas occur at the sites of the most rapid bone growth, including the distal femur, the proximal tibia, and the proximal humerus. Typical radiographic appearances include medullary and cortical bone destruction, permeative or moth-eaten appearance, aggressive periosteal reaction, sunburst appearance, Codman triangle.
• Option D. Osteoid sarcoma . Osteoid osteoma accounts for 10% of all benign bone tumors. It is neither locally aggressive nor does it have the potential for malignant transformation. This bone tumor frequently affects the long bones of the femur and tibia . It presents with a nidus of vascular osteoid with surrounding sclerotic bone. Osteoid osteomas in long bones are predominantly intracortical. Plain radiograph may be normal or may show a s olid periosteal reaction with cortical thickening. The nidus is sometimes visible as a well-circumscribed lucent region, occasionally with a central sclerotic dot. However, dense sclerosis may sometimes obscure the nidus.
• Educational objective:
• The shown x-ray image is characteristic of osteoclastoma or GCT of the distal end of the radius.

subject: Orthopedics
question: GCT malignant component is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Giant cells . The multinucleated giant cells in the GCT are benign in nature.
• Educational objective :
• The mononuclear stromal cells around the multinucleated giant cells in the GCT are the malignant components of the tumor.

subject: Orthopedics
question: McCune-Albright syndrome features are: High calcium Presents around puberty Cysts in long bones Precocious puberty High ACTH levels

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about Ameloblastoma is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rapidly growing . This is false. Ameloblastoma are slow growing tumors.
• Option C. Malignant disease . This is false. Ameloblastoma are benign tumors.
• Option D. MC site is tibia . This is false. The most common site is the posterior mandible in the area of the molar teeth. Among the long bones, tibial diaphysis is a common site. In the long bones, this tumor is known as adamantinoma.
• Educational objective:
• Ameloblastoma is a cystic benign tumor that causes cystic degeneration of the bone.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 12-year-old boy presents with chronic knee pain for 2 years. Knee X-ray is shown below. Probable diagnosis is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/07/08/codman-triangle.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chronic osteomyelitis. Homogeneous osteosclerosis and/or sequestrum formation is characteristic of chronic osteomyelitis on radiograph.
• Option C. Rickets. There is growth plate widening and abnormal configuration of the metaphysis, seen as fraying (indistinct margins of the metaphysis); splaying (widening of metaphyseal ends); cupping (concavity of metaphysis) and pseudofracture on the compression side of bone known as Looser's zone .
• Option D. Hyperparathyroidism. Radiological Features of Hyperparathyroidism are s ubperiosteal resorption of terminal tufts of phalanges, lateral end of clavicle and symphysis pubis. There is loss of lamina dura of tooth socket surrounding teeth, generalized osteopenia, thinning of cortices, and indistinct bony trabeculae. There is also the presence of brown tumors and salt pepper appearance of the skull.
• Educational objective:
• The given x-ray image shows the formation of codman’s triangle which is seen in the cases of osteosarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Peak incidence of osteosarcoma occurs at:

subject: Orthopedics
question: 22 year old male present with bony mass in the metaphyseal region of right. knee with typical sun ray appearance and CT Scan of the chest reveals osteoblastic metastases. What is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Chondrosarcoma. The radiographic appearance of chondrosarcoma shows a lesion arising in the medullary cavity with irregular matrix calcification. The pattern of calcification is described as “ punctate ”, “ popcorn ”, or “ comma-shaped ”. There is bone destruction, cortical erosions, and periosteal reaction. Chondrosarcoma is a highly malignant tumor with the potent capacity to cause local invasion and distant metastasis, preferentially in the lungs.
• Option C. Ewing’s sarcoma. Ewing sarcoma appears radiographically as a destructive lesion in the diaphysis of a long bone with an “ onion skin ” periosteal reaction. Lung is the most common site of metastases followed by the bone.
• Option D. Osteochondroma. An osteochondroma can be either sessile or pedunculated and is seen in the metaphyseal region typically projecting away from the epiphysis. There is associated broadening of the metaphysis from which it arises. The cartilage cap can be thin and difficult to identify, or thick with rings and arcs calcification and irregular subchondral bone. The incidence of malignant degeneration is approximately 1% for patients with a solitary osteochondroma and 6% for patients with multiple hereditary exostoses .
• Educational objective:
• The given characteristics of bony mass in metaphysis of knee with sunray appearance and osteoblastic metastases in chest are seen in the osteosarcoma of the bone.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following malignant tumors is radio-resistant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ewing’s sarcoma . It is highly radio-sensitive tumor.
• Option B. Retinoblastoma . It is also a radio-sensitive tumor.
• Option D. Neuroblastoma . It is also a radio-sensitive tumor.
• Educational objective :
• Among the given options, osteosarcoma is a highly radio-resistant tumor . Thus, it is ideally treated with chemotherapy followed by limb salvage surgery followed by chemotherapy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: An 8-year-male child presents with progressive swelling of upper end tibia which is irregular with local temperature raised, variable consistency and ill-defined margins. What is the probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Giant cell tumor . This is commonly seen in the epiphyseal region of the long bones. The lesion is solitary . The swelling is associated with more extensive lesions and a soft tissue mass or bump is occasional.
• Option B. Ewing’s sarcoma . Ewing’s sarcoma presents with local rise of temperature , progressive swelling with variable consistency and ill-defined margins but it is seen in the diaphyseal region of the long bones.
• Option D. Secondary metastasis . Spine and pelvis are the most common sites of secondary metastasis.
• Educational objective :
• The given clinical features are seen in both osteosarcoma and Ewing’s sarcoma. The only differentiating point is the involvement of the upper end of the tibia i.e., the metaphysis, which is involved in the osteosarcoma and not ewing’s sarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following investigations are needed for the diagnosis of osteosarcoma, except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. MRI of femur . MRI is the best test to measure the extent of the tumor within the bone and in the soft tissue and to determine the relationship of the tumor to nearby anatomical structures.
• Option C. Bone scan . A bone scan should be obtained to look for skeletal metastases .
• Option D. CT chest. CT scans of the chest should be done to search for pulmonary metastases .
• Educational objective:
• All the given investigations are done in a case of osteosarcoma except for the bone marrow biopsy. Instead, biopsy of the lesion if done before starting the treatment.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following bone tumor present with secondaries in lung with pneumothorax:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Ewing’s sarcoma . This tumor also metastasize to lung but osteosarcoma metastasis is more common.
• Option C. Osteoclastoma . It rarely metastasize to lung.
• Option D. Chondroblastoma . This tumor also metastasize to lung but osteosarcoma metastasis is more common.
• Educational objective :
• Osteosarcoma is the most common tumor that metastasize to the lungs with increased incidence of pneumothorax.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Characteristic translocation seen in Ewing’s sarcoma is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. t(2;8). This is seen in Burkitt’s lymphoma .
• Option C. t(x;18). This is seen in synovial sarcoma .
• Option D. t(14;18). This is seen in follicular lymphoma .
• Educational objective:
• t(11;22) translocation is characteristic of Ewing’s sarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In 1st decade of life, the most common bone tumor is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteosarcoma. This is commonly seen in 2 nd decade of life.
• Option C. Multiple myeloma. ts incidence peaks in 5 th -7 th decade of life.
• Option D. Metastasis. It occurs in patients >40 years of age.
• Educational objective:
• Ewing’s sarcoma is common in 1 st decade of life, in children younger than 10 years of age.

subject: Orthopedics
question: PAS positive cells are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteosarcoma . About 27% of osteosarcomas have PAS positive cells.
• Option C. Chondrosarcoma . About 92% of chondrosarcomas have PAS positive cells.
• Option D. Multiple myeloma . Multiple myelomas have PAS negative cells.
• Educational objective :
• All ewing sarcomas have PAS positive cells among the given options.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Mic 2 positive cells are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Osteosarcoma, Option C. Chondrosarcoma, and Option D. Multiple myeloma are negative for MIC 2 cells.
• Educational objective:
• MIC2 is a specific marker for Ewing’s sarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: CD 99 is marker of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans. CD 34 is the most consistent marker for DFSP.
• Option C. Osteosarcoma. RBM 15 and YTHDC1 are the specific markers for osteosarcoma.
• Option D. Metastasis. Bone metastases tumor biomarkers include bone specific alkaline phosphatase ( BALP ), tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase ( TRACP ), tumor necrosis factor ( TNF ), carbohydrate antigen 15-3 ( CA15-3 ).
• Educational objective:
• CD 99 is the specific marker for Ewing’s sarcoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 7-year-old boy with h/o trauma 2 months back now presents with fever and acute pain over thigh. On X-ray, femoral shaft shows lesions with multiple laminated periosteal reaction. Next line of management is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CRP measurement . In Ewing's sarcoma cases, CRP pre-treatment value >0.5 mg/dL is considered as a sensitive prognostic risk factor indication for distant metastasis and poor prognosis.
• Option B. Core biopsy . Core biopsy methods are preferable to provide a histological diagnosis of the tumor cell. In some cases, repeated core biopsy attempts may fail to provide a definite result.
• Option C. Tc99 MDP scan . When Ewing Sarcoma is lytic, MDP bone scan does not add to staging performed by FDG PET/CT; thus, MDP bone scanning may be omitted. However, when Ewing Sarcoma is sclerotic, MDP bone scan may detect osseous metastases not detected by FDG PET/CT.
• Educational objective :
• MRI is the investigation of choice in ewing’s sarcoma before biopsy is performed.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest site of occurrence of chordoma is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Femur . Metaphysis of the femur is most commonly involved in osteosarcoma .
• Option C. Ribs . Enchondroma is the most comon benign rib lesion after fibrous dysplasia.
• Option D. Proximal tibia . It is also involved in osteosarcoma .
• Educational objective:
• Sacrum is most commonly involved in the chordoma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following tumor is associated with hyperglycemia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• There is no association of the tumor with hyperglycemia seen in cases of ewing’s sarcoma (option A), osteosarcoma (option B) and multiple myeloma (option D).
• Educational objective:
• Chondrosarcoma can cause hyperglycemia in around 85% cases.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Moth eaten bone is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Osteoid osteoma. Plain radiograph may be normal or may show a solid periosteal reaction with cortical thickening. The nidus can be sometimes visible as a well-circumscribed lucent region, occasionally with a central sclerotic dot. However, dense sclerosis may sometimes obscure the nidus.
• Option C. Eosinophilic granuloma . The radiographic features vary considerably, depending on the site of the lesion. In the skull, a round to oval osteolytic lesion is seen. The lesion have sharp borders, with a punched-out appearance . In the spine, the thoracic vertebrae are involved most often (54%), followed by lumbar (35%) and cervical (1 1%) vertebrae. The body of the vertebra is affected; the posterior elements are usually spared . Fractures of the vertebral bodies may cause marked compression, resulting in the characteristic vertebra plana or silver-coin appearance . The diaphysis is the most common region involved in the long bones.
• Option D. Chodromyxoid fibroma. Plain radiograph may show lobulated or oval eccentric lytic lesion, well-defined sclerotic margin, often expansile, with long axis parallel to long axis of long bone, no periosteal reaction (unless a complicating fracture present), geographic bone destruction , and presence of septations (pseudotrabeculation).
• Educational objective:
• Moth-eaten bone is seen in radiographic image of multiple myeloma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 70-year-old male complains of multiple bone pains. On evaluation, he has high ESR, high Calcium values, lytic lesion in multiple bones, and >20% plasma cells in peripheral smear. Most likely diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Multiple myeloma . Diagnosis of MM is made if plasmacytosis (>10%) is present with either  lytic bone lesion, serum or urine M component, progressive increase in M component over time or extramedullary mass lesion development.
• Option B. Hairy cell leukemia . The hairy cell has characteristic-appearing mononuclear cells which are typically large with circumferential hair-like cytoplasmic projections and a round, well-defined nucleus. The flow cytometry markers positive in hairy cell leukemia include CD11c, CD25, CD103, and CD123, along with typical B-cell markers such as CD19, CD20, or CD22. The cyclin-d1 expression is usually present, but it is weak or focal. HCL-V is negative for CD25 and CD123. BRAF -mutation V600E is seen in nearly all cases of classic hairy cell leukemia. 80% patients have cytopenias on presentation. Splenomegaly is a predominant feature. Infectious complications are common, due to both the underlying immunosuppression from cytopenias and myelosuppressive therapy.
• Educational objective :
• The given history of multiple bone pain, high ESR and calcium levels, with >20% plasma cells and lytic lesions is characteristic of plasma cell leukemia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient presented with pain in back. Lab investigation shows elevated ESR. X-ray skull shows multiple punched out lytic lesions. Most important Investigation to be done is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Serum acid phosphatase . Serum phosphatase levels are lower or normal in multiple myeloma. So, it cannot be used as a sole diagnostic marker for multiple myeloma.
• Option B. CT head with contrast . The role of radiological imaging in multiple myeloma is essential in the initial staging of the disease, in the detection and characterisation of complications, and in the evaluation of patient's response to treatment.
• Option C. Whole body scan . Whole-body MRI is the most sensitive technique for detecting bone involvement and assessing painful complications.
• Educational objective :
• The investigation of choice for the patient with pain in back, elevated ESR, and multiple punched out lytic lesions on skull x-ray is serum electrophoresis, which shows monoclonal gammopathy, confirming the diagnosis as multiple myeloma.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which metabolic condition has phosphaturia and osteomalacia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Tumor-induced osteomalacia and phosphaturia are not seen in Osteosarcoma (Option B), Undifferentiated sarcoma (Option C), and Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (Option D).
• Educational objective:
• Tumour-induced osteomalacia (TIO), is a rare paraneoplasatic syndrome found in >95% of benign tumours that secrete fibroblast growth factor 23 – a phosphaturic circulating hormone.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Synovial sarcoma, gene affected is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. MIC 2 . MIC 2 gene is a specific marker for Ewing’s sarcoma.
• Option C. RAS . This gene is affected in pancreatic, lung and colorectal cancers.
• Option D. P53 . This gene is affected in breast, colorectal, liver, lung and ovarian cancers.
• Educational objective :
• In synovial cell carcinoma, SYT-SSX gene is affected.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about Bone metastasis are: 5% bone metastasis are symptomatic Higher serum levels of alkaline phosphatase Most common secondary in female is breast Prostate produce osteosclerotic lesion Commonly involves hand and feet bones

subject: Orthopedics
question: Metastases is least common in:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Small bones of the hand

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not true about Perthes disease?
options: It is also called coxa plana
It is caused by interruption in the blood supply of the capital femoral epiphyses
Abduction, internal rotation and flexion of hip are restricted

explanation.short: Ans. B) Occurs more commonly in females
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is also called Coxa plana: This is true because coxa plana is the other name of Perthes disease which means flattening of the femoral head epiphysis, that occurs in this disease.
• Option C. It is caused by interruption in the blood supply of the capital femoral epiphyses : This is also true. Perthes disease involves osteonecrosis of the proximal femoral epiphysis due to ischemia of the femoral head. Between 4-8 years of age, femoral head depends on the lateral epiphyseal vessels for its blood supply and venous drainage whose situation in retinacula makes them susceptible to stretching and to pressure from an effusion.
• Option D. Abduction, internal rotation and flexion of hip are restricted: This statement is also true. With the progression of the disease, abduction, flexion & internal rotation movements gets limited.
• Educational objective:
• Perthes disease is more common in males than in females.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is false regarding Salter Harris classification?
options: Type II is the most common type of epiphyseal injury
Type IV classifies injury through epiphysis, physis, metaphysis
Type V is the perichondrial ring injury

explanation.short: Ans. D) Type V is the perichondrial ring injury

subject: Orthopedics
question: Irregular thigh folds are seen in:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Developmental dysplasia of hip
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Perthes disease: Physical examination in Perthes disease usually reveals a limping gait. The mobility of the hip is limited mainly in internal rotation and abduction. In addition, the legs may be of different length because of an adduction contracture or a collapsed epiphysis. The thigh folds appear normal.
• Option C. Slipped capital femoral epiphysis: A child presenting with a chronic slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) will generally walk with an antalgic gait, out-toeing and some shortening of the affected limb. The child may complain of vague pain in the groin, thigh or knee.
• Option D. Congenital coxa vara: The child presents with a painless limp resulting from Trendelenburg gait and minor limb length inequality. Easy fatigability or aching pain around the gluteal muscles may be a complaint.
• Educational objective:
• Irregular thigh folds are seen in developmental dysplasia of hip (DDH) and is not associated with other pediatric hip conditions like Perthes, SCFE or coxa vara.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Risk factor(s) for Legg-calve-Perthe’s disease is/are: Accelerated skeletal growth Growth hormone abnormalities Positive family history Female sex Passive smoking

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Accelerated skeletal growth. Skeletal immaturity is the aetiology in Perthes disease, which retards the bone age. Growth hormone abnormalities. Growth hormone deficiency is the another risk factor for Perthes disease.
• Educational objective:
• The risk factors associated with Legg-Calve-Perthe’s disease are a positive family history & presence of passive smoking.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is X-ray feature of Perthes disease?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following movements is restricted in Perthes disease?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Abduction and internal rotation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, B, C. During the initial phase of the disease, only extreme of all movements are diminished & painful. Later on, most movements are full except for abduction (with flexed hip) & internal rotation.
• Educational objective:
• Of all the movements of the hip, only abduction & internal rotation movements are restricted.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The commonest cause of limp in a child of seven years is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. TB hip. Early presentations are pain around hip and limp. But pain is the most common complaint in TB hip patients.
• Option B. CDH. Congenital Dysplasia of Hip occurs in children in the age group of 1-3 years and involves presentation of a painless limp. The given question mentions about a 7-year-old child, so CDH is ruled out here.
• Option D. Slipped upper femoral epiphysis. A stable SCFE causes stiffness or pain in the knee or groin, & possibly a limp, causing the child to walk with the foot outward. The pain & limp may come & go, & may worsen with activity & get better with rest.
• Educational objective:
• The commonest cause of limp in a 7-year-old child will be Perthes disease due to disrupted blood supply to the head of femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Perthes disease is treated by:
options: High dose of calcium with steroids
Supervised containment of femoral head in acetabulum

explanation.short: Ans. D) Supervised containment of femoral head in acetabulum
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. High dose of calcium with steroids. Calcium dose protects the infarcted femoral head from deformity but lacks bone anabolic effects. Steroids use reduces the oxygen in the blood which is required for the joint.
• Option B. Total hip replacement. Treatment with hip replacement at a younger age puts the children at a higher risk of failure of surgery.
• Option D. Relieving weight bearing. Weight-bearing restriction is associated with poor mobility in active stage of the disease.
• Educational objective:
• The main treatment of Perthes disease is use of braces that keep the hip in abduction, flexion & some internal rotation, to contain the femoral head in the acetabulum.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are true about Slipped capital femoral epiphysis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A. Avascular necrosis may occur. True. Avascular necrosis is a possible complication of SCFE.
• Option B. Usually occur after 10 years of age. True. SCFE is seen in adolescent children of age more than 10 years (males = 13-15 years, females = 11-13 years).
• Option C. Obesity is a risk factor. True. Most of the patients of SCFE are fat or overweight.
• Educational objective:
• SCFE is more commonly seen in adolescent males than females of age 13-15 years, who are fat or overweight. Avascular necrosis is the possible complication of SCFE.

subject: Orthopedics
question: An 11-year-old 70 kg child presents with limitation of abduction and internal rotation. There is tenderness in Scarpa’s triangle. On flexing the hip, the limb is externally rotated. The diagnosis is:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Slipped capital femoral epiphyses
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Perthes disease. Perthes disease is seen in children of 4-8 years of age, and it presents with limitation of internal rotation and abduction movements of the hip.
• Option C. Observation hip. “Observation Hip” is a provisional diagnosis in children, characterized by limp, with or without pain in the knee, thigh and groin, and limited motion of the hip joint, which may or may not be associated with systemic reactions. Its clinical manifestations are due to synovitis of the hip and occasionally due to inflammation of the psoas muscle. The aetiology is initially unknown but may be clarified by diagnostic tests and clinical observation.
• Option D. Tuberculosis hip. In the stage of synovitis, there is joint effusion and the affected limb is flexed, abducted, and externally rotated with an apparent lengthening. There is a restriction of terminal range of movements.
• Educational objective:
• In SCFE, there is limitation of abduction & internal rotation, tenderness in Scarpa’s triangle, & external rotation of the hip in flexion. It is seen in children more than 10 years of age.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trethowan’s sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Perthes disease. In Perthes disease, when the hip is flexed, it may go into obligatory external rotation and the knee points towards axilla (normally, it goes towards midclavicular region). This is called as Catterall’s sign .
• Option B. CDH. In unilateral cases, one leg is short as demonstrated by Galeazzi/Allis test. In bilateral cases, Klisic test & Trendeleburg test are positive.
• Option D. Fracture neck femur. In impacted and stress fractures, there is no obvious clinical deformity seen. There is minor discomfort with active or passive hip range of motion, muscle spasms at extremes of motion, pain with percussion over greater trochanter. In displaced fractures, leg is in external rotation and abduction, with shortening of the limb.
• Educational objective:
• The Trethowan sign is used to diagnose slipped capital femoral epiphysis. In this sign, the line of Klein passes above the femoral head. It is best seen on both AP and lateral views of the hip X-ray. On the AP view of a normal hip, the Klein line intersects a small portion of the lateral capital epiphysis. In posterior displacement of the capital epiphysis, the line intersects a smaller portion of the epiphysis or does not intersect it at all. This is called as positive Trethowan sign.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is true about DDH?
options: Limbs are in adduction, internal rotation and flexion in old children

explanation.short: Ans. B) Limbs are in adduction, internal rotation and flexion in old children
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Male gender is risk factor. Incorrect. About 80% of the cases of DDH are seen in girls (usually first born).
• Option C. Femur neck is retroverted. Incorrect. There is superior & lateral displacement of the femoral head with sloping, shallow acetabulum. There is femoral anteversion in DDH.
• Option D. Ligamentum teres is atrophied. Incorrect. In DDH, ligamentum teres is thickened and elongated.
• Educational objective:
• DDH is commonly seen in female patients. The limbs are in adduction, internal rotation & flexed position in older children. Femoral neck is anteverted. Ligamentum teres is thickened & elongated.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Following is not a part of Ortolani test:
options: Examiners thumb rests on the medial thigh
Examiners finger lie on the greater trochanter
Hips are flexed to 90 degrees and gently abducted
Jerk is given to the knee to dislocate the femur head jerk of exit

explanation.short: Ans. D) Jerk is given to the knee to dislocate the femur head jerk of exit
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A, B, and C describes the correct steps of the Ortolani test.
• Educational objective:
• In Ortolani’s Test, the hips and knees are flexed to 90-degree, then an anterior pressure is applied over the greater trochanter and the examiner gently abduct the leg with thumbs. The examiner’s fingers lies over the greater trochanter & thumb rests over the medial thigh. If the hip is dislocated, a distinctive clunk will be heard as the hip relocates, which makes the test positive.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following are true regarding congenital dislocation of hip except:
options: Asymmetric Thigh folds may be seen
Galleazi sign and Ortolani test may be positive
Polyhydramnios is a known risk factor

explanation.short: Ans. D) Polyhydramnios is known risk factor
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A, B, and C are correct statements regarding congenital dislocation of the hip.
• Educational objective:
• In congenital dislocation of hip, asymmetric thigh folds are seen; Galleazi sign & Ortolani test may be positive. CDH/DDH can be bilateral. Oligohydramnios is a known risk factor.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 4-month-old child diagnosed with DDH. Cast used after closed reduction is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hip spica. After the age of 6 months, if the Pavlik harness and closed reduction fails, then following the open reduction, hip spica cast is applied. This is valid in the age group of 6-18 months.
• Option C. Minerva cast. The Minerva cast is used to protect an injured spine or as part of the treatment for a spinal deformity like scoliosis. It includes the trunk (sometimes extending down till the rib cage) as well as the patient's head, with openings provided for the patient's face, ears, and usually the top of the head and hair.
• Option D. Riser’s cast. The Riser cast is similar to the Minerva cast, extending from the patient's hips to the neck and sometimes including part of the head.
• Educational Objective:
• In children under the age of 6 months, with DDH, after the closed reduction, Pavlik harness is applied to keep the reduction in place

subject: Orthopedics
question: Ortolani test is positive when the examiner hears the:
options: Clunk of entry on abduction and flexion of hip
Clunk of entry on extension and adduction of hip
Click of exit on abduction and flexion of hip
Click of exit on extension and adduction of hip

explanation.short: Ans. A) Clunk of entry on abduction and flexion of hip
