explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B, C, and D are incorrect because the clunk is heard only when the hip is flexed and then abducted, and not in any other hip movement/position.
• Educational objective:
• Ortolani’s test is used to diagnose DDH and it is done by abducting the hip in flexion and then reducing the posterior hip dislocation. When the hip is relocated at its position, a ‘clunk’ is heard, which indicates the positive sign.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest presentation of congenital dislocation of knee is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Varus. T he newborns have a physiological genu varum which is highest around the age of 1 year and later as the child grows the lower limbs become straight. It may be seen in conditions like vitamin D deficiency rickets, blount’s disease, physeal injury due to trauma/infection.
• Option B. Valgus. It is most commonly seen in children with vitamin D deficiency rickets, dysplasias, achondroplasia, physeal injuries, and tumors like osteochondroma, fibrous dysplasia.
• Option C. Flexion. This deformity is generally seen in the knees of children with cerebral palsy and spina bifida, or limb length discrepancy.
• Educational objective:
• Congenital dislocation of knee is commonly presented with hyperextension deformity of the knee i.e., genu recurvatum.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Characteristic of blount’s disease:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Genu valgum. It is most commonly seen in children with vitamin D deficiency rickets, dysplasias, achondroplasia, physeal injuries, and tumors like osteochondroma, fibrous dysplasia.
• Option C. Coxa vara. The most common cause of coxa vara is either congenital or developmental. Other common causes include metabolic bone diseases like Paget's disease, post-Perthes deformity, osteomyelitis, and post traumatic.
• Option D. Coxa valgus. It is seen more commonly in children with cerebral palsy, and hip dislocation/subluxation.
• Educational objective:
• Blount’s disease/infantile tibia vara is seen in children less than 3 years of age which involves varus of tibia, hyperextension of the knee, & internal rotation of the tibia.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Blount’s diseases in a bowleg deformity occur due to:

question: Varus is:
options: Distal part away from the midline

explanation.short: Ans. A) Distal part towards midline
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Distal part away from midline. A valgus deformity is a condition in which the bone segment distal to a joint is angled outward, that is, angled laterally, away from the body's midline. It is the opposite of varus deformity.
• Educational objective:
• Varus deformity occurs when the distal part moves towards the midline.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Charlie Chaplin gait is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CDH. In congenital dysplasia of hip, the child has a Trendelenburg gait due to abductor insufficiency. The child also walks on toes in an attempt to compensate for relative shortening of the affected side.
• Option B. Congenital coxa vara. In congenital coxa vara, the child presents with abnormal but painless gait pattern. A Trendelenburg limp is sometimes associated with unilateral coxa vara and a waddling gait is often seen when bilateral coxa vara is present.
• Option C. Genu valgum. The children with genu valgum presents with abnormal circumduction gait.
• Educational objective:
• Charlie Chaplin gait or in-toeing gait is often seen in cases of external tibial torsion.

Orthosis is recommended during day time
Orthosis is recommended during night time

• Orthosis is a must only during weight bearing — was considered correct in earlier exams like JIPMER 95/PGI 94, emphasizing bracing during weight-bearing stress. Today, Orthosis is recommended full time would be more accurate based on current orthopedic protocols.

question: True regarding CTEV shoes is:
options: It is the same as normal shoe
It has heel with extra length

explanation.short: Ans. B) It has straight medial border
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is the same as normal shoe. Incorrect. CTEV shoes are different from normal shoes as they do not have a heel, instead they have a flat sole.
• Option C. It has medial shoe raise. Incorrect. Instead of a medial raise, CTEV shoes have an outer raise to prevent foot inversion and a straight medial border to prevent forefoot adduction.
• Option D. It has heel with extra length. Incorrect. CTEV shoes do not contain any heel. This is so to prevent foot equinus.
• Educational objective:
• CTEV shoes do not have any heel and has flat sole to prevent foot equinus. They have straight medial border to prevent forefoot adduction and an outer raise to prevent foot inversion.

question: Which of the following is the most common congenital anomaly in India?

• Option C. Genu Valgum. It is also commonly seen in India between the age of 2-5 years. It may not be seen at birth in many children like CTEV.

• Educational objective:
• CTEV is the most commonly seen congenital anomaly in India.

question: Most important pathology in club foot is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Tightening of tendoachilles. In clubfoot, the calf muscles and Achilles tendon are very tight, which limits the amount of flexibility in the ankle and pulls the heel up in the back into a non-functional position. But it is the secondary pathology that occur due to congenital foot position.
• Option C. Calcaneal fracture. In a clubfoot, calcaneus is not fractured but it is in varus and medially rotated around the talus.
• Educational objective:
• Congenital dislocation of the talonavicular is the most important pathology in club foot.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Common fractures in children are all except:

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are incomplete fracture in children except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Greenstick Fractures:
• The cortex in tension fracture is completely broken while the cortex in compression remain intact but frequently undergo plastic deformation.
• Greenstick fracture and plastic deformation
• So, it is an incomplete fracture and it is necessary to complete the fracture on the intact compression side for reduction and POP application.
• Option C. Buckle (Torus) Fracture:
• It is so called, because of its resemblance to the base of an architectural column. Most commonly occurs at the transition between metaphysis and diaphysis where buckling of cortex takes place.
• Option D. Plastic Deformation:
• Immature bone is weaker in bending strength but absorbs more energy prior to fracture. This may result in permanent deformation of bone (without fracture) known as plastic deformation . It is most common in forearm particularly ulna. Reduction (correction of deformity) is recommended, if there is: >20 degrees of angulation, If a child is >4 years old, and Has either a clinically evident deformity or limitation of pronation and supination
• Educational objective:
• Incremental fracture is a complete, horizontal pathological fracture seen in deformed bones in Paget’s disease.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In Child abuse, all the following are X-ray feature except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Metaphyseal corner fracture and Option B. Metaphyseal bucket handle fracture. Metaphyseal corner fractures are also known as classical metaphyseal lesions (CML) or bucket handle fractures . They are observed in young children, less than 2 years old. It is suggestive of non-accidental injury. It is also common among abused children, occurring in 39-59%. There are multiple microfractures across the metaphysis in immature mineralized bone with an orientation perpendicular to the long axis of the bone. To-and-fro manipulation (shaking) is the force applied.
• Educational objective:
• The X-ray in case of child abuse will not show subepiphyseal microfractures.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
A 6-year-child is brought to emergency with complete of severe pain in aim after a fall, immediate X-ray done shows fracture distal radius and ulna. Which of the following term best describes the fracture?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/29/1_Eyi8yaj.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Torus. Buckle/Torus fracture most commonly occurs at the transition between metaphysis and diaphysis where buckling of cortex takes place.
• Option C. Barton’s. It is a fracture of the distal radius which extends through the dorsal aspect of the articular surface with associated dislocation of the radiocarpal joint. There is no disruption of the radiocarpal ligaments, and the articular surface of the fractured distal radius remains in contact with the proximal carpal row.
• Option D. Colles. The Colles fracture is defined as a distal radius fracture with dorsal comminution, dorsal angulation, dorsal displacement, radial shortening, and an associated ulnar styloid fracture.
• Educational objective:
• A greenstick fracture is a partial thickness fracture where only the cortex and periosteum are interrupted on one side of the bone while they remain uninterrupted on the other side.

Break the other side of cortex and immobilization by POP

subject: Orthopedics
question: In children, best remodeling is seen in fracture with:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Angulation in diaphysis. Roughly 20% of angular remodeling occurs in the diaphysis. Remodeling in the diaphysis follows Wolf's law i.e., increased pressure (compression) on the concave side stimulates new bone formation. Tension on the convex side leads to resorption of bone.
• Option C. Rotation in diaphysis and Option D. Rotation in metaphysis. For practical purposes, no rotational remodeling occurs.
• Educational objective:
• In children, the best remodeling is seen in fractures with angulation of the metaphysis.

question: Type VI Rang’s injury includes:
options: Transverse fracture of metaphysis with longitudinal extension into physis
Open injury with loss of physis

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Transverse fracture of metaphysis with longitudinal extension into physis. Peterson type I fracture is a transverse fracture of metaphysis with longitudinal extension into the physis. It is commonly seen in distal radius.
• Option B. Open injury with loss of physis. Paterson type VI injury is an open injury associated with loss of physis.
• Option C. Thurston Holland’s sign. Type II of Salter-Harris classification for epiphyseal injury represent Thurston Holland’s sign in which the fracture extends from the physis into the metaphysis.
• Educational objective:
• Type VI Rang’s injury includes perichondrial ring injury which extends towards metaphysis and becomes continuous with its periosteum.

question: An 8-year-old boy with a history of fall from 10 feet height complains of pain in the right ankle. X-ray taken at that time are normal without any fracture line. But after 2 years, he developed a calcaneovalgus deformity. The diagnosis is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Epiphyseal dysgenesis is a feature of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hyperparathyroidism. Primary hyperparathyroidism is not commonly seen in children and adolescents.
• Option B. Hypoparathyroidism. Hypoparathyroidism is a rare disorder that may be acquired or inherited. Bone disease occurs in hypoparathyroidism due to markedly reduced bone remodelling due to the absence or low levels of parathyroid hormone.
• Option D. Hyperthyroidism. Thyrotoxicosis in children is relatively rare and causes accelerated intramembranous and endochondral ossification and an increase in linear growth rate. Paradoxically, this may result in premature fusion of the growth plates and early cessation of growth leading to persistent short stature. In severe thyrotoxicosis in young children, early closure of the cranial sutures can result in craniosynostosis with neurological sequelae. Untreated hyperthyroidism during pregnancy is also associated with craniosynostosis and may be a causative risk factor.
• Educational objective:
• Epipyseal dysgenesis/Fragmented/punctate epiphysis is due to Hypothyroidism.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following are features of Pyle disease except:

question: Sprengel’s deformity is associated with all except:

• Educational objective:
• Sprengel’s deformity is not associated with dextrocardia, instead it is associated with congenital scoliosis, diastematomyelia and Klippel-Feil syndrome.

question: Idiopathic scoliosis can be treated by:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Minerva cast. A minerva cast is a special type of cast that can be used to treat certain types of scoliosis and cervical spine fractures. It e xtends along the side and in back of the head and neck, chest to hip area, and incorporates a plaster-of-Paris headband.
• Option C. Crutchfield traction. It is a traction device whose pins are inserted into the skull to distract and/or immobilize the neck. Crutchfield tongs are used to stabilize fractures of the cervical spine.
• Option D. PTB cast. The PTB or patellar tendon bearing cast is used to treat fractures of the Tibia, usually the mid to distal third fractures.
• Educational objective:
• Idiopathic scoliosis is best treated with the use of a Milwaukee brace to prevent progression of the curve during skeletal growth.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cobb’s angle is used to measure the degree of:
options: Extent of depression in calcaneal fracture

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Angular deformity of the knee. The angulation in the knees are measured using the radiographic scannogram of the lower extremity.
• Option C. Extent of depression in calcaneal fracture. It is measured by Bohler's Angle , that may be depressed on plain radiographs. It is defined as the angle between two lines drawn on plain film. The first line is between the highest point on the tuberosity and the highest point of posterior facet and the second is the highest point on the anterior process and the highest point on the posterior facet. The normal angle is between 20-40 degrees.
• Option D. Extent of spondylolisthesis. The Meyerding classification grade is used to measure the degree of slip using standing, neutral lateral radiographs of the lumbar spine. The classification system divides slip into five grades (shown in table below). The grade percent is determined by drawing a line through the posterior wall of the superior and inferior vertebral bodies and measuring the translation of the superior vertebral body as a percentage of the distance between the two lines. Grades I and II are generally considered low-grade slip, whereas Grades III, IV, and V are considered high-grade slip. Grade V is also called spondyloptosis.
• Educational objective:
• The Cobb angle is utilized to assess for severity of kyphoscoliosis, risk of progression, and means of intervention.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Progression of congenital scoliosis is least likely in which of the following vertebra anomalies?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fully segmented hemivertebra is the second vertebral anomaly that most likely has the chance of progression of congenital scoliosis.
• Option B. Wedge vertebra. The characteristic radiographic deformity of congenital scoliosis is represented by lateral bending in the coronal plane, along with different degrees of vertebral wedge change. Even a slight scoliosis deformity is marked by this kind of change, which worsens with curve progression.
• Option D. Unilateral unsegmented bar with hemivertebra . It has the greatest potential for rapid progression (5 to 10 degrees/year).
• Educational objective:
• Out of all the vertebral anomalies, block vertebra has the least chance of progression (< 2 degrees/year).

subject: Orthopedics
question:
The following deformity is seen in a child. What is the likely cause?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/16/picture29_lMemF1e.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) Congenital pseudarthrosis of Tibia
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tibial hemimelia. Tibial hemimelia (TH) manifests as a shortened leg with knee and ankle deformities. Most often seen deformities are varus in the ankle and a knee flexion contracture in the knee.
• Option B. Fibula hemimelia. Children who have fibular hemimelia are born with a short or missing fibula. Other bones in the leg, ankle, and foot can be affected too. Most children with fibular hemimelia have it in one leg, but some have it in both.
• Option D. Congenital posteromedial angulation of tibia. Posteromedial bowing of tibia is a congenital condition often associated with calcaneovalgus deformity of ipsilateral foot. This condition undergoes spontaneous resolution to a major extent, often leaving only leg length inequality. The latter may result in abnormal gait and backache.
• Educational objective:
• Congenital pseudarthrosis of the tibia is a rare dysplastic pathological condition of the lower limbs, associated with neurofibromatosis type 1. It may be seen at birth or manifest at the walking age with pathological fractures leading to deformity and limb length discrepancy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cause of congenital pseudarthrosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Neurofibromatosis. Neurofibromatosis is an association; it is not a cause.
• Option C. Fibrous dysplasia. Pseudarthrosis is associated with fibrous dysplasia and not caused by it.
• Educational objective:
• The most common cause of congenital pseudarthrosis is idiopathic (unknown).

subject: Orthopedics
question:
The child with the following clinical presentation will most probably have the underlying diagnosis:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/16/picture33_KiUVJvA.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Neuropathy . The main symptoms can include :
• Numbness and tingling in the feet Burning, stabbing or shooting pain in affected areas Loss of balance and co-ordination Muscle weakness, especially in the feet
• Option C. Metaphyseal dysplasia. It is characterized by progressive short stature that develops by age of two years. The clinical and radiographic features are usually not present at birth, but manifest in early childhood with short limbs, genu varum, and waddling gait.
• Option D. Neurofibromatosis (NF). The skeletal abnormalities seen in neurofibromatosis includes scoliosis, congenital pseudoarthrosis of tibia, and hemihypertrophy.
• Educational objective:
• Muscular dystrophy presents with pseudohypertrophy of the calf muscles, which is the main differentiating sign seen in such patients.

question: Mutation seen in Marfan syndrome is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. FGFR-3 gene. Activating mutations in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) gene are responsible for several autosomal dominant craniosynostosis syndromes and chondrodysplasias i.e. hypochondroplasia, achondroplasia, and thanatophoric dysplasia – a neonatal lethal dwarfism syndrome.
• Option C. COL1A-1 gene. Mutations of COL1A1 gene is associated with osteogenesis imperfecta, some types of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Caffey diseases, and osteogenesis imperfect/Ehlers- Danlos syndrome overlapping diseases.
• Option D. Fibrillin-2 gene. Mutations in the gene for fibrillin-2 cause Congenital Contractural Arachnodactyly.
• Educational objective:
• Mutations in the gene for fibrillin-1 cause the Marfan syndrome.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following is true about dashboard injury except:
options: It is associated with posterior dislocation of the hip
Sciatic nerve may be involved leading to foot drop
Avascular necrosis of the hip could be a late complication
The point of impact is on the greater trochanter

explanation.short: Ans. D) The point of impact is on the greater trochanter
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: It is associated with posterior dislocation of the hip. True. Posterior hip dislocation is classically associated with dashboard injury. The direction of impact is along the long axis of femur, forcing the femoral head out of the acetabulum and moves backwards.
• Option B: Sciatic nerve may be involved leading to foot drop. True. In posterior dislocation of hip, the sciatic nerve gets commonly injured. It has two terminal branches – Tibial nerve and Common Peroneal Nerve. It is the common peroneal nerve which controls the muscles for dorsiflexion of foot. Injury to these fibers lead to foot drop.
• Option C: Avascular necrosis of the hip could be a late complication . True. Due to the damage to the network of reticular vessels in the femoral head, there may be ischemia leading to Avascular necrosis of hip.
• Educational objective:
• Dashboard injury is associated with posterior dislocation of hip. Sciatic nerve is commonly involved that leads to foot drop. Avascular necrosis is the common late complication of the injury. The point of impact in this injury is the femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient presents with hip dislocation with limitation of Abduction at hip, and flexion and internal rotation deformity at hip and shortening. Diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Central dislocation. In central fracture dislocation of hip, femoral head is forced medially through the floor of acetabulum and can be palpated on per rectal examination. There is shortening of limb. This is an extremely rare variety of dislocation.
• Option B: Anterior dislocation . Anterior dislocations occur with the hip externally rotated and abducted. The degree of flexion at the time of injury determines the eventual position of the femoral head. They are associated with lengthening.
• Educational objective:
• In posterior dislocation of the hip, the hip is in the FADIR deformity i.e., flexion, adduction and internal rotation, along with shortening of the limb.

question: A patient is diagnosed with disc prolapse. Examination reveals paralysis of Extensor Hallucis Longus. Which nerve root is affected?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. L3. When L3 nerve root is affected, quadriceps is affected, which causes weak knee extension and hip adduction.
• Option B. L4. When L4 nerve root is affected, there is weakness of tibialis anterior and quadriceps muscles, leading to weak ankle dorsiflexion, hip adduction and knee extension.
• Option D. S1. When S1 nerve root is affected, gastrocnemius and soleus or flexor hallucis longus; abductor hallucis and gluteus maximus are affected. This leads to weakening of ankle plantarflexion and hip extension .
• Educational objective:

• Option C. Surgical treatment is necessary . Treatment options of hangman’s fracture include conservative management, cervical orthosis, halo-vest orthosis, and surgical procedures. Surgical fixation may be considered in case of severe angulation of C2 on C3 and disruption of the C2 to C3 disc space.
• Educational objective:
• The hangman’s fracture successfully heals with non-operative treatment in almost 95% cases.

question: Spur sign is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Supracondylar fracture of humerus. If no fracture line can be identified on the radiological images, indirect signs like anterior or posterior fat pad signs can be seen. Since supracondylar fracture is a transverse or oblique fracture, thus is does not involve spur sign on radiography.
• Option B: Radial head fracture. A fracture of the radial head can sometimes result in a spur sign, especially if there is an associated avulsion fracture of the annular ligament or the adjacent soft tissues. The avulsed fragment may appear as a spur on imaging studies. However, not all radial head fractures present with a spur sign.
• Option C: Fibula fracture. A fibula fracture, particularly at the level of the ankle or distal leg, can sometimes result in a spur sign. This is especially true if the fracture involves the attachment site of the lateral collateral ligament or the biceps femoris tendon, leading to an avulsion-type injury and the formation of a bone spur.
• Educational objective:
• An acetabular fracture involves the hip socket can occur due to high-energy trauma such as motor vehicle accidents or falls from a height. These fractures can be complex and involve displacement of bone fragments. The avulsion of the bone appears as a spur-like fragment on the radiograph, thus called spur sign.

question: An 80-year-old female after fall developed inability to walk with external rotation deformity. On examination, SLR is not possible and broadening of trochanter is present. The possible diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Fracture neck of femur . In femoral neck fracture, trochanters are normal. The limb presents with shortening, abduction and partial external rotation deformity.
• Option C: Fracture subtrochanteric femur . This fracture is a risk factor in patients on denosumab or bisphosphonate use since a long time. Such patients will have a history of thigh pain before the trauma. The patients with subtrochanteric fracture presents with hip and thigh pain, inability to bear weight on the affected extremity, pain on movement, limb shortening, varus deformity, and flexion of proximal fragment that may threaten the overlying skin.
• Option D: Fracture greater trochanter . In the isolated greater trochanter fracture, the patient presents with pain in the abduction and extension of the hip. The pain exacerbates on palpation of the greater trochanter.
• Educational objective:
• Broadening of trochanters is seen in both intertrochantric as well as Greater Trochanter fracture. But inability to do SLR, and an external rotation deformity points towards intertrochantric fracture.

question: Non-union is common in fracture:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Scapula. Scapula fractures most commonly undergoes post-traumatic glenohumeral arthritis, malunion, recurrent glenohumeral instability, and neurovascular injuries.
• Option B: Supracondylar humerus. The main complications of supracondylar humerus fractures are malunion, Volkmann ischemic contracture, and damage to ulnar/median/radial nerves. Osteonecrosis is a rare complication.
• Educational objective:
• Non-union is most commonly seen in fracture neck of femur among the given options. Scapular and supracondylar humerus fractures can lead to malunion as a complication.

subject: Orthopedics
question: An 80-year-old male sustained fracture neck of femur 1 week back. The treatment of choice is:
options: Closed reduction and fixation with three cancellous screws

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Excision arthroplasty. It is not typically the treatment of choice for a fractured neck of femur, as it does not address the issue of restoring function to the hip joint.
• Option C: Closed reduction and fixation with three cancellous screws. Closed reduction is preferred in cases of patients less than 65 years of age with the fracture sustained < 3 weeks ago. It is not preferred in older patients due to concerns about bone quality and healing capacity.
• Option D: Longitudinal skin traction for 6 weeks. While traction can be used as a temporary measure to relieve pain and align the fracture, it is not a definitive treatment for a fractured neck of femur, especially in an elderly patient. Long-term traction can lead to complications such as pressure ulcers and muscle atrophy.
• Educational objective:
• The treatment of choice for fracture of femoral neck in an 80-year-old patient is hemiarthroplasty.

question: All of the following are true about fracture of the atlas vertebra, except:
options: Jefferson’s fracture is the most common of atlas
Atlanto-occipital fusion may sometimes be needed
CT scans should be done for diagnosis

explanation.short: Ans. B) Quadriplegia is seen in 80% cases
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Jefferson’s fracture is the most common of atlas . True. Atlas Fractures caused by high-energy trauma with axial loading in young patients is called Jefferson Fracture.
• Option C. Atlanto-occipital fusion may sometimes be needed. True. Stable injuries can be treated with immobilization in a cervical collar. Unstable injuries require either halo-vest immobilization or surgical stabilization with a fusion of the atlanto-occipital joint.
• Option D. CT scans should be done for diagnosis. True. Diagnosis is often missed with plain radiographs so a CT scan may be required to make the diagnosis.
• Educational objective:
• Fracture of the atlas is not associated with neurological deficits unless it is associated with C2 fractures. The patient may present with neck pain and associated neck muscle spasm.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Treatment of choice in fracture neck of femur in a 40-year-old male presenting after 2 days is:
options: Closed reduction and Internal fixation by cancellous screws
Closed reduction and Internal fixation by Austin Moore pins

explanation.short: Ans. B) Closed reduction and Internal fixation by cancellous screws
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hemiarthroplasty. This is preferred in patients > 65 years of age with no pre-existing arthritis.
• Option C: Closed reduction and internal fixation by Austin Moore pins. Austin Moore pins are commonly used for internal fixation of hip fractures, especially in older adults. They are long, metallic pins inserted into the femoral neck to stabilize the fracture. However, in a 40-year-old male with a fracture neck of femur presenting after only 2 days, internal fixation with Austin Moore pins might not be the treatment of choice. This option is more commonly considered in older patients or in certain fracture patterns where hemiarthroplasty or other methods are not feasible.
• Option D: Plaster and rest . Plaster immobilization is not typically indicated for a fracture neck of femur, especially in a 40-year-old male. Fractures in this region usually require surgical intervention for proper alignment and stabilization to prevent complications like avascular necrosis of the femoral head or nonunion. Plaster immobilization alone does not provide adequate stabilization and may lead to poor outcomes.
• Educational objective:
• In patients < 65 years of age with fracture < 3 weeks old, the preferred treatment option is closed reduction and internal fixation with multiple cancellous screws.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 40-year-old male after RTA, attains spinal injury. His lower limb power is greater than that of upper limb and sacral sensations are present. Type of spinal cord lesion is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C. Posterior cord syndrome . Posterior cord syndrome is a condition caused by lesion of the posterior portion of the spinal cord, responsible for proprioceptive sensibility. Main signs and symptoms are loss of proprioception and vibration sensation, ataxic gait, positive Romberg sign, hypotonia, and abolition of deep tendon reflexes.
• Option D. Complete spinal cord injury . These injuries typically demonstrate complete bilateral loss of motor function, pain sensation, temperature sensation, proprioception, vibratory sensation, and tactile sensation below the level of injury. Lumbosacral injuries will present with paralysis and loss of sensation in the lower extremities. These injuries may also result in loss of bowel control, loss of bladder control, and sexual dysfunction. Thoracic injuries lead to the same deficits as lumbosacral injuries and, in addition, may result in loss of function of the muscles of the torso, leading to difficulty maintaining posture. Cervical injuries lead to the same deficits as thoracic injuries and, also, may result in loss of function of the upper extremities leading to tetraplegia. Injuries above C5 may also cause respiratory compromise due to loss of innervation of the diaphragm.
• Difference between various spinal cord injury syndromes:
• Educational objective:
• Lower limb muscle power > upper limb muscle power, and sparing of sacral sensation are the main characteristic features of central cord syndrome.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about fractures of vertebrae is/are: Wedge compression causes flexion injury Anterior longitudinal ligament runs along the posterior surface of the vertebral bodies Vertical compression force does not extend to cervical spine Chance fracture occurs due to flexion distraction injury Fracture dislocation is common in flexion rotation injury

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1. Wedge compression fracture of the vertebra is caused by flexion injury of the spine, due to axial/compressive loading.
• 2. Anterior longitudinal ligament runs along the anterior surface of body and posterior longitudinal ligament over the posterior surface. Interspinous ligament runs along between spinous process.
• 3. Vertical compression forces extends down the cervical spine leading to compressive injuries.
• 4. Chance fracture is a horizontal fracture of the thoracolumbar junction, extending posterior to anterior through the spinous process, pedicle, and vertebral body. It is a flexion-distraction injury of the spine.
• 5. Fracture-dislocation is common in flexion-rotation injury due to high energy trauma.
• Educational objective:
• Compressive wedge fracture of the vertebrae are caused by flexion injury to the spine. Chance fracture or the horizontal splitting of the vertebra is caused by flexion-distraction forces. Flexion-rotation injury of the spine commonly causes fracture-dislocation of the vertebra.

subject: Orthopedics
question: ‘Whiplash’ injury is caused due to:
options: A blow on top to head

explanation.short: Ans. B) Acute hyperextension of the spine
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A fall from a height . High falls were more likely to be work-related and result in thoracic and complete injury, while low falls were more commonly associated with cervical and motor functionally incomplete injury.
• Option D. Acute hyperflexion of the spine . Hyperflexion sprain or anterior subluxation of the cervical spine is characterized by a localized ligamentous disruption of the cervical spine, cau- sed by a distractive flexion force. In addition, the cranial vertebra may be displaced. The lesion is sometimes associated with a minor wedge-like compression of one or two vertebral bodies. The hyperflexion sprain is a relatively rare injury.
• Educational objective:
• Whiplash injury is the acute hyperextension injury of the spine caused by sudden hyperextension of the neck followed by immediate hyperflexion.

question: Choose the correct statement about lordosis: Lordosis means excessive inward curvature of the spine Can be seen in Osteoporotic females Mostly seen in lumbar region and manifests as persistent back pain Poor posture is a common cause of lordosis Obesity protects against developing lordosis

subject: Orthopedics
question: ​​​ Bryant’s triangle is useful in diagnosis of all of the following except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Measures Supra-trochanteric area The patient lies supine and tips of trochanter and ASIS are marked on both sides. A perpendicular is dropped from each ASIS on to the bed. From tip of greater trochanter , another perpendicular is dropped on to the first one (base of the triangle). Now, join the tips of greater trochanter to ASIS on respective side. Each side of this right-angled triangle is compared with its counterpart on the normal side. Any shortening of the base (i.e., femoral axis continuation), which may be because of shortening in the neck, head, joint or dislocation of joint can be measured.
• B ryant’s triangle is on no value in : (Mnemonic: ABC ) A SIS absent B ilateral disease C hopped hip (hip disarticulated) Reversed Bryant’s triangle: When the proximal migration of the tip of greater trochanter is beyond the ASIS , then reversed Bryant’s triangle is drawn . Total supratrochanteric shortening will be the summation of base of triangle on normal side and the base of triangle in abnormal side .
• Educational objective:
• Bry ant’s triangle is mainly used to measure supratrochanteric shortening in anterior and posterior hip dislocation cases.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient came with complaint of difficulty in climbing upstairs. When he is made to stand on his right leg, left side of pelvis fell to a lower level. When he stands on left leg, then right side of pelvis can be drawn up. Which of the following nerve of him has got affected?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Normally when the body weight is supported on one limb, the glutei (medius and minimus) of the supported side contract and raise the opposite and unsupported side of pelvis, if the abductor mechanism is defective the unsupported side of pelvis drops and this is known as positive Trendelenburg's test . Trendelenburg's test is done to assess the integrity of abductor mechanism. It is positive in the conditions in which any of the three—fulcrum (Femoral Head), lever arm (neck length) or power (muscles/nerve) is affected.
• The hip abductors become weak due to damage to the superior gluteal nerve. On standing on the affected leg, the pelvis drops towards the unaffected side. This indicates that the superior gluteal nerve of the affected side has been damaged.
• Educational objective:
• In the given question, the pelvic drop to the left side while standing on the right leg is indicative of the right superior gluteal nerve damage.

• Option C: Intertrochanteric fracture. It is seen in patients > 80 years of age. The limb appears shortened and externally rotated with the lateral border of foot touching the couch.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The commonest hip injury in the elderly patients is:
options: Subcapital capsular fracture neck of femur

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Stress fracture . Stress fractures typically result from repetitive stress or overuse rather than acute trauma, and they are more commonly seen in athletes or individuals engaged in high-impact activities.
• Option C: Impacted fracture neck of femur . This type of fracture, also known as a subcapital fracture , occurs within the neck of the femur but is impacted, i.e., the fractured segments are driven into each other. While impacted fractures are relatively common in elderly patients, they are generally less frequent than extracapsular fractures.
• Option D: Subcapital capsular fracture neck of femur . This option refers to fractures that occur just below the head of the femur and involve the capsule of the hip joint. These fractures are also relatively common in the elderly population but are less frequent than extracapsular fractures.
• Educational objective:
• The most commonly seen hip fracture in the elderly population is the extracapsular fracture which involves intertrochanteric fractures. This is followed by the fracture of the neck of the femur.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 60-year-old man fell in bathroom and was unable to stand. On right buttock region, ecchymosis was present with external rotation of the leg in a way that the lateral border of foot is touching the bed. The most probable diagnosis is:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Extracapsular fracture neck of femur
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Anterior dislocation of hip. Superior anterior dislocations classically present with the hip extended and externally rotated while inferior anterior dislocations generally present with the hip abducted and externally rotated.
• Option C: Intracapsular fracture neck of femur. This presents as shortened limb with abduction and partial external rotation.
• Option D: Posterior dislocation of hip. Patients with a posterior dislocation present with a limb adducted, flexed, internally rotated, and shortened.
• Educational objective:
• The presence of ecchymosis on the hip region after a fall in an elderly patient is seen in the extracapsular fracture of the hip. The extracapsular fracture presents with limb shortening and extreme external rotation deformity of the hip where the lateral border of the foot touches the couch.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trochanteric fracture of femur is best treated by:
options: Plaster in abduction and internal rotation

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Inlay plates. Inlay plates are not used in the treatment of trochanteric fractures of the femur. These fractures usually involve the proximal portion of the femur, such as the greater or lesser trochanter, and are commonly treated with devices specifically designed for stabilization, such as dynamic hip screws or intramedullary nails.
• Option C: Plaster in abduction. Plaster immobilization in abduction is not the standard treatment for trochanteric fractures. Trochanteric fractures often require surgical intervention for proper alignment and stabilization, as immobilization in plaster alone may not provide adequate support and could lead to malunion or nonunion.
• Option D: Plaster in abduction and internal rotation. While immobilizing the limb in abduction might theoretically reduce tension on the fracture site, adding internal rotation is not typically recommended for trochanteric fractures. Internal rotation could potentially worsen the displacement of the fracture fragments or lead to malalignment during the healing process.
• Educational objective:
• Trochanteric fractures of the femur are best treated by the dynamic hip screw or proximal femoral nailing as it provides greater stability to the fracture fragments.

question: Symmetrical areflexic bladder, bowel and lower limb occur in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cauda equina syndrome. This condition involves asymmetrical areflexic involvement of the bladder & bowel, and the lower limbs. Bladder and bowel involvement occurs late in this condition.
• Option C. Nerve root damage . Compression of nerve root results in radiculopathy. This results in sharp pain which worsens with activity, radiation to specific dermatome, and may result in neurological weakness.
• Option D. Brown sequard syndrome. It is a hemisection lesion of the spinal cord. This causes loss of vibratory, light touch and motor functions on the same side while pain and temperature is lost on the other side.
• Educational objective:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trendelenburg test is positive in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Normally, when the body weight is supported on one limb, the glutei (medius and minimus) of the supported side contract and raise the opposite and unsupported side of pelvis, if the abductor mechanism is defective the unsupported side of pelvis drops and this is known as positive Trendelenburg's test . Trendelenburg's test is done to assess the integrity of abductor mechanism . It is positive in the conditions in which any of the three—fulcrum (Femoral Head), lever arm (neck length) or power (muscles/nerve) is affected.
• Causes of Positive Trendelenburg Test: Power-Paralysis of abductor muscles: Abductors of hip are Gluteus medius and minimus, Tensor fascia lata and sartorius (accessory).
• Note : Disc prolapse at L4–L5 compresses L5 nerve root which supplies G.medius and minimus.
• Superior gluteal nerve palsy (supply gluteus medius and minimus) Polio Iliotibial tract palsy Decreased lever arm: Fracture neck femur Absence of stable fulcrum about which the abductor muscles can act. Dislocation of hip Destruction of femoral head in Perthes disease, AVN, late stages of TB hip (stage 4 and 5) and septic arthritis.
• Educational objective:
• Trendelenburg test is positive in hip dislocation, fracture neck of femur and coxa vara

subject: Orthopedics
question: In spinal shock:
options: Knee jerk is the first reflex to return
High thoracic lesions are commonly associated with more severe neurological deficits
Failure of return of cord activity within 48 hours is a very poor prognostic sign

explanation.short: Ans. D) Both B and C
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Knee jerk is the first reflex to return. False. The first reflex to return is bulbocavernosus reflex (S2, S3, S4) – contraction of the anal sphincter in response to the stimulation of glans or clitoris. Its reappearance is the sign of recovery from the spinal shock.
• Option B. High thoracic lesions are commonly associated with more severe neurological deficits. True. Higher the level of lesion, greater the number of spinal segments involved and more extensive the neurological deficits. Higher thoracic lesions may cause paraplegia, quadriplegia and may also cause autonomic dysfunction such as impaired BP regulation etc. due to involvement of sympathetic nerves.
• Option C. Failure of return of cord activity within 48 hours is a very poor prognostic sign . True.  The absence of any signs of neurological improvement during first 48 hours is suggestive of severe spinal cord injury and implies a lower chances of significant recovery.
• Spinal injury in first acute phase include neurogenic shock and spinal shock. Neurogenic shock: Hypotension and relative bradycardia Spinal shock: Temporary loss of spinal cord function and reflex activity below the level of spinal cord injury characterized by : Flaccid areflexic paralysis Bradycardia & hypotension (due to loss of sympathetic tone) Absent bulbocavernosus reflex Important to know because one cannot evaluate neurologic deficit until spinal shock phase has resolved. End of spinal shock is indicated by return of the bulbocavernous reflex. Conus medullaris or cauda equina injuries may also lead to permanent loss of the bulbocavernous reflex.

options: Type I – immobilization in rigid collar
Type IV – open reduction internal fixation

subject: Orthopedics
question: Motorcyclist’s fracture is:
options: Stellate fracture across base of skull
Transverse fracture across base of skull
Spinous process fracture of C7 vertebra

explanation.short: Ans. B) Transverse fracture across base of skull
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C. Lamina fracture of C7 vertebra. C7 laminar fracture can occur after C7 dome-like laminectomy when a substantial amount of lamina is resected. Although C7 fractures may not cause deleterious clinical outcomes, they can lead to an unharmonized cervical curvature.
• Option D. Spinous process fracture of C7 vertebra. This type of injury occurs after sudden flexion of the head. It can also occur after repeated stress caused by the pulling of the trapezius and rhomboid muscles on the spinous processes. This injury is known as a “clay shoveler’s fracture”. There is an acute avulsion fracture. The lower cervical or upper thoracic spinous processes are general affected as they have longer and more vulnerable spinous processes. It is usually a very stable fracture and does not produce any neurologic deficits. It typically heals with 6-8 weeks of relative rest with no further treatment being needed in most cases.
• Educational objective:
• Motorcyclist’s fracture is the transverse fracture across the base of the skull.

options: Spinal cord injury with radiological abnormality
Spinal cord injury without radiological abnormality
Spinal cord involvement with radiological abnormality
Spinal cord involvement without radiological abnormality

subject: Orthopedics
question: Block vertebrae are seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Paget’s disease. In paget’s, the vertebral body increases in anteroposterior and lateral dimensions but the height remains unchanged. Early radiographic appearance is thickening and hypertrophy of trabecular bone parallel to the end plate. The combination of trabecular bone hypertrophy and thickening at the end-plates with absorption on the periosteal surfaces at the anterior and posterior vertebral borders leads to the “picture frame” sign.
• Option B. Leukemia . The radiographic changes noted in the vertebrae included diffuse demineralization of the bodies, the neural arches, and the transverse processes. The loss of bone density appeared is more pronounced in the thoracic and the upper lumbar spine. In some cases, demineralization is greater in the centers of the vertebrae than at peripheries. There can be compression of the vertebral bodies, the most common structural change being anterior wedging.
• Option C. TB . In spinal tuberculosis, characteristically, there is destruction of the intervertebral disk space and the adjacent vertebral bodies, collapse of the spinal elements, and anterior wedging leading to the characteristic angulation and gibbus (palpable deformity because of involvement of multiple vertebrae) formation.
• Educational objective:
• Block vertebrae is seen commonly in Klippel-Feil syndrome in children.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Ring fracture of C1 is called as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hangman’ fracture . Hangman’s fracture, also known as Traumatic Spondylolisthesis of Axis, is defined as bilateral fracture traversing the pars interarticularis of C2 with an associated traumatic subluxation of C2 on C3.
• Option C. Clay Shoveller’s fracture . Clay-shoveler Fractures are an avulsion-type spinous process fracture in the lower cervical or upper thoracic spine.
• Option D. Chance fracture . A Chance fracture is an unstable, flexion-distraction injury of the spine, typically at the thoracolumbar junction, with a horizontal fracture through the spinous process, pedicles, and vertebral body.
• Educational objective:
• Ring fracture of C1 is called as Jefferson’s fracture.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Seat belt injury is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tear drop fracture. Tear drop fracture is a fracture pattern occurring in severe axial/flexion injury of the cervical spine. They indicate extensive underlying ligamentous injury and spinal instability. Associated spinal cord injury is common, especially anterior cervical cord syndrome and quadriplegia.
• Option B. Wedge fracture. Spinal wedge (compression) fractures are hyperflexion injuries to the vertebral body resulting from axial loading. Most commonly affecting the anterior aspect of the vertebral body, wedge fractures are considered a stable fracture. Most common type (~50%) of thoracolumbar spine fractures. Typically, these fractures are insufficiency fractures secondary to osteoporosis, although some are pathological secondary to a focal bone lesion.
• Option D. Whiplash injury. These are the acceleration-deceleration injuries occurring to the cervical spine. It is caused by neck bending forcibly forward and then backward, or vice versa. The injury, which is poorly understood, usually involves the muscles, discs, nerves, and tendons in neck.
• Educational objective:
• Seat belt injury is chance fracture of the thoracolumbar junction.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tear drop fracture of lower cervical spine implies:
options: Flexion-rotation injury with failure of anterior body

explanation.short: Ans. D) Flexion compression failure of body
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Wedge compression fracture. Spinal wedge compression fractures are hyperflexion injuries to the vertebral body resulting from axial loading. Most commonly affecting the anterior aspect of the vertebral body, wedge fractures are considered a stable fracture. Most common type (~50%) of thoracolumbar spine fractures. Typically, these fractures are insufficiency fractures secondary to osteoporosis, although some are pathological secondary to a focal bone lesion.
• Option B. Axial compression fractures. Compression wedge fractures are caused by axial compression alone or flexion forces, when the spine is bent forward or in side flexion at the moment of trauma. It is a stable fracture and patients rarely accompany neurological deficits. The most common causes in younger patients are falls and motor vehicle accidents.
• Educational objective:
• A tear drop fracture of the lower cervical spine is the flexion-compression fracture of the vertebral body.

subject: Orthopedics
question: ​​​ Line joining anterior superior iliac spine to ischial tuberosity and passing above greater trochanter is called:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Shoemaker’s line . The line from the tip of Greater Trochanter to the anterior superior iliac spine is prolonged anteriorly to meet in the central line at or above the umbilicus of the patient. If the GT is elevated/proximally migrated, the line will cross the mid line below the umbilicus.
• Option C: Chiene’s line . 2 lines joining the GT on both sides and ASIS on both sides must be parallel. Supratrochanteric shortening at 1 side leads to convergence of line. This forms a parallelogram called as Chiene’s parallelogram . It is used to detect the femoral head fracture based on the changes in the bony landmark relation. Normally both the lines should be parallel. If GT of one side migrates proximally, as in femoral fracture, the line converges. This test is ineffective in bilateral hip affections.
• Option D: Perkin’s line . Perkin line is a line drawn perpendicular to Hilgenreiner line, intersecting the lateral most aspect of the acetabular roof. The upper femoral epiphysis should be seen in the inferomedial quadrant. It should lie below Hilgenreiner line, and medial to Perkin line. If the nucleus of the femoral head is not visible because it is not ossified yet, the femoral metaphysis should be used. Lateral displacement of the femoral head occurs in developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH).
• Educational objective:
• The line joining the ASIS to ischial tuberosity and passes above the greater trochanter is called the Nelaton’s line.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trendelenburg’s sign is negative in an Inter-Trochanteric fracture because of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Gluteus medius . The gluteus medius is one of the primary abductor muscles of the hip. If it is weak or dysfunctional, it can lead to a positive Trendelenburg sign.
• Option B: Gluteus maximus . The gluteus maximus is primarily involved in hip extension and external rotation. While it is an important muscle around the hip joint, weakness or dysfunction in the gluteus maximus is not typically associated with Trendelenburg's sign.
• Option C: Gluteus minimus . The gluteus minimus, along with the gluteus medius, is also involved in hip abduction. Dysfunction or weakness in the gluteus minimus can contribute to a positive Trendelenburg sign, not a negative one.
• Educational objective:
• The tensor fascia lata is a muscle that assists in hip flexion and stabilization of the pelvis during walking. Dysfunction in the tensor fascia lata can lead to a negative Trendelenburg sign. This is because if the tensor fascia lata is unable to stabilize the pelvis on the unsupported side during single-leg stance, the pelvis may tilt upward rather than dropping, resulting in a negative Trendelenburg sign.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trendelenburg’s test is positive in all except:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Tuberculosis of hip joint

question: Telescopic test is useful to diagnosis:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Intracapsular fracture neck of femur
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In Perthe’s disease (option A), ankylosis of hip joint (Option D) and malunited trochanteric fracture (Option C) , the hip joint is stable and will not lead to positive telescopy test.
• Educational objective:
• The telescopy test is positive in intracapsular neck of femur fractures while in the other conditions, it is negative because of the stability of the hip joint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: “Judet” view of X-ray is for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Calcaneum. To rule out calcaneal pathologies, lateral and axial (Harris) views are used. To rule out further foot and ankle pathologies in calcaneal injuries, Broden’s view and Canale/Kelly views are used.
• A) Broden’s view; B) Canale view
• Option C: Scaphoid. For scaphoid, the posteroanterior ulnar deviation scaphoid view is used. It is performed with the wrist in ulnar deviation to free the scaphoid from bony superimposition.
• Option D: Shoulder. Standard projections used are AP view and lateral/scapular Y view. Modified trauma projections include modified trauma axial view, Garth projection for Hill-Sachs defects, supine lateral view, modified transthoracic supine lateral view, and supine axial view etc.
• Educational objective:
• The Judet’s view is used for the evaluation of the pelvis radiographically.

subject: Orthopedics

options: Anterior segment is not visualized adequately
Superior exposure is very well exposed
Sciatic nerve injury in 10% in cases

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not true about intracapsular fracture of femur neck?
options: Displacement is less common than extracapsular fractures
Contour of greater trochanter is normal
Tenderness is mainly present over the anterior joint line

explanation.short: Ans. B) Malunion is common complication
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Displacement is less common than extracapsular fractures: This occurs due to the intact capsule in intracapsular fractures. The capsular displacement is more apparent in extracapsular fracture than intracapsular fractures.
• Option C: Contour of greater trochanter is normal: Intertrochantric line is maintained, hence the contour of the trochanter is normal.
• Option D: Tenderness is mainly present over the anterior joint line: Upon clinical examination, tenderness of anterior joint line is positive. The Scarpa’s triangle appears to be tender.
• Educational objective:
• The most common complication of fracture neck of femur is the non-union of the fracture. There is absence of cambium layer of periostium in the femoral neck within the joint capsule. Thus, the fracture neck of femur heals without external callus. Blood supply is precarious in this region which is further jeopardized by displacement of fragments and a tamponade effect adds to the insult by occluding the veins at lower pressure and arteries at higher pressure. Being intracapsular, the fracture is bathed by synovial fluid which interferes with the healing process.
• NOTE : Difference between intracapsular and extracapsular femoral neck fractures are :

subject: Orthopedics
question: The most common type of hip fracture in childhood is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Garden type I fractures are also known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Complete fracture without displacement . This is Garden’s type II fracture.
• Option B: Complete fracture with minimal (partial) displacement . This is Garden’s type III fracture.
• Option C: Complete fracture with full displacement . This is Garden’s type IV fracture.
• Educational objective:
• Valgus impaction fractures are the type I fracture of the femoral neck according to Garden’s classification. This type of fracture is incomplete with the femoral head tilted in posterolateral direction i.e., into valgus.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Increase in Pauwel’s angle indicate:

explanation.short: Ans. C) More chances of displacement
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Good prognosis. This can be seen in cases of type I fracture with less angle of inclination as the chances of non-union are very low.
• Option B: Impaction. Impaction is seen in type I fracture due to compressive forces but the angle of inclination is still less in this type.
• Option D: Trabecular alignment disrupted. While an increase in Pauwel's angle indicates a more vertically oriented fracture line, it doesn't directly imply disruption of trabecular alignment.
• Educational objective:
• An increase in Pauwel's angle indicates a more vertically oriented fracture line, which is associated with increased shear forces acting on the fracture site, leading to a higher likelihood of displacement.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Pauwel’s angle is:
options: The difference between neck-shaft angle between two femurs of a patient
Formed by joining a line extended from fracture line of femur neck to an arbitrary line depicting the horizontal plane

explanation.short: Ans. C) Formed by joining a line extended from fracture line of femur neck to an arbitrary line depicting the horizontal plane
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Neck-shaft angle of femur . The angle of the femoral neck relative to the shaft is known as neck-shaft angle. Normal value of neck-shaft angle in an adult is 120-135⁰. Angle < 120⁰ is called coxa vara, angle > 135⁰ is called coxa valga.
• Educational objective:
• The Pauwel’s angle is defined as the angle formed by the line joining the fracture line of neck of femur to an arbitrary horizontal line.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 40-year-old female with a history of fall, complaints of pain in right hip and inability to walk. On examination, tenderness is present in Scarpa’s triangle. The X-ray is normal. Next investigation is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Aspiration. Aspiration involves the removal of fluid from a joint or tissue for diagnostic purposes. While it may be indicated in suspected joint effusion or infection, it's not the most appropriate next step in this case, where the primary concern seems to be related to the hip and surrounding structures.
• Option B: CT scan. CT scan provides detailed images of bones and can reveal fractures or bone abnormalities that may not be apparent on X-ray. However, in this case, where soft tissue involvement is suspected (tenderness in Scarpa's triangle), CT scan might not be the most informative initial investigation for assessing soft tissue pathology.
• Option D: Bone scan. Bone scan is primarily used to detect abnormalities in bone metabolism and is helpful in diagnosing fractures, infections, or tumors that may not be visible on conventional X-rays. However, given the clinical presentation with tenderness in Scarpa's triangle and normal X-ray, the concern seems to be more focused on soft tissue involvement rather than purely bone pathology, making MRI a more appropriate choice for further investigation.
• Educational objective:
• In the given case scenario, the injury is around the hip involving soft tissues and bone. The presenting complaints and examination suggests the presence of occult fracture around the hip. The occult fractures are not visible on x-rays and CT scans. They can be only viewed on MRI. Thus, in this case, the next investigation will be an MRI.

Identify the deformity.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture49_DQ1LOjE.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:

There is also reciprocal flexion of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint. This is a result of an imbalance of the extensor mechanism of the digit.
• Option B. Boutonniere deformity. Boutonniere deformity is described as finger flexion at the proximal interphalangeal joint (PIP) and hyperextension at the distal interphalangeal joint (DIP). This is usually a result of an acute trauma or rupture of the PIP central slip.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Patient comes with crush injury to upper limb, doctor is concerned about gangrene and sepsis. What can help decide between amputation and limb salvage?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. GCS score. The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is used to objectively describe the extent of impaired consciousness in all acute medical and trauma patients. The scale assesses patients according to three aspects of responsiveness : eye-opening, motor, and verbal responses. Reporting each of these separately provides a clear, communicable picture of a patient’s state. The score expression is the sum of the scores as well as the individual elements. For example, a score of 10 might be expressed as GCS10 = E3V4M3. The Glasgow Coma Scale divides into three parameters : best eye response (E), best verbal response (V) and best motor response (M). The levels of response are ‘scored’ from 1, for no response , up to normal values of 4 (Eye-opening response), 5 (Verbal response) and 6 (Motor response) . The total Score has values between 3 and 15 ; 3 being the worst and 15 being the highest.
• Option C. Gustilo-Anderson classification. The purpose of the Gustilo-Anderson classification is to provide a prognostic framework that guides treatment for open (or compound) fractures , with higher grade injuries associated with higher risk of complications.
• Option D. ASIA guidelines. The American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) Impairment Scale is a standardized neurological examination used by the rehabilitation team to assess the sensory and motor levels affected by the spinal cord injury . The scale has five classification levels, ranging from complete loss of neural function in the affected area to completely normal. The results to set functional goals based on the neurological level of injury.
• Educational objective:
• To decide between amputation and limb salvage, MESS scoring system is used. Higher the score, higher the chances of amputation.

• Explanation

• Option C. Arcuate ligaments. The arcuate ligament is part of the posterolateral ligamentous complex of the knee. It is a Y-shaped thickening of the posterolateral capsule, which arises from the fibular styloid and divides into two limbs:
• Medial limb : Curves over the popliteus muscle to join with the oblique popliteal ligament Lateral limb : Ascends to blend with the capsule near the lateral gastrocnemius muscle

subject: Orthopedics
question: An athlete sustained an injury around the knee joint suspecting cartilage damage. Which of the following is an investigation of choice?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Plain x-ray. If the injury is restricted to the cartilage, it will not show up in a plain X-ray; it may be noted on an MRI.
• Option B. Clinical examination . The clinical examination of a knee is addressed to evaluate three aspects: 1) patello-femoral joint/extensor mechanism; 2) articular (meniscal and chondral) lesions; and 3) knee instability. The diagnosis cannot be made only on the basis of the clinical examination.
• Option D. Arthrotomy . An arthrotomy is a surgical exploration of a joint, which should include inspection of the cartilage, intra-articular structures, joint capsule, and ligaments. Although arthrotomy alone can be performed to evaluate and treat the contaminated joint space, arthroscopy allows more complete evaluation of the cartilage and the joint.
• Educational objective:
• Arthroscopy is the gold standard investigation and treatment choice in suspected cases of cartilage injury of the knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The primary pathology in Athletic Pubalgia is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rectus femoris strain. This is caused by sudden, forceful over-stretching and eccentric contraction of the muscle. It is seen in soccer and football players. It is associated with avulsion of AIIS. The patient presents with pain and tenderness on the anterior aspect of the hip and mid-thigh at muscle belly.
• Option C. Gluteus medius strain. Gluteus medius strain is uncommon but can occur in sports like running or football. It occurs due to repetitive force on the contracting muscle. The patient experience sudden sharp pain in the buttocks that may reduce over time.
• Option D. Hamstring strain. Hamstring strains are caused by a rapid extensive contraction or a violent stretch of the hamstring muscle which causes high mechanical stress. Hamstring strains are common in sports with a dynamic character like sprinting, jumping, contact sports where quick eccentric contractions are regular. In soccer, it is the most frequent injury. It results in a sudden, severe pain in the posterior thigh. Also, a "popping" or tearing sensation is described. Sometimes swelling and ecchymosis are possible.
• Educational objective:
• The primary pathology of athletic pubalgia is abdominal muscle strain, mainly of the rectus abdominis muscle.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Mallet finger is:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Avulsion of extensor tendon
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fracture of proximal phalanx. Proximal phalanx fractures can be epiphyseal or shaft fractures and can be intra-articular or extra-articular. They are most often the result of forced rotation, hyperextension or direct trauma.
• Option C. Rupture of flexor tendon. Flexor tendon injuries commonly result from volar/palmar lacerations. This can occur with an accompanying neurovascular injury. Another mechanism is a rupture from its insertion caused by a forced extension during active flexion like in ball sports where a finger is forced into extension while it is being flexed or where a player grabs an opponent's shirt (jersey finger) or in activities such as rock climbing.
• Educational objective:
• Mallet finger is the avulsion of the extensor tendon of the finger caused by forceful flexion of an extended finger.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Jersey finger is caused by rupture of:

• Option B. Extensor digiti minimi. Rupture of only one slip of the FDS is extremely rare. Rupture of only one slip will result in retained flexion of both superficialis and profundus tendons. The ruptured one slip of FDS, like a partial tendon laceration, can cause triggering.
• Educational objective:
• Jersey finger is caused by the rupture or avulsion of the flexor digitorum superficialis tendon from the distal phalanx.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 55-year-old male presents with severe backache for 10 days and urinary incontinence with a H/o Intervertebral lumbar disc prolapse. There is no H/o fever or weight loss. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pott’s spine . Pott’s spine or tuberculosis of the spine is caused by mycobacterium tuberculosis. It is usually secondary to hematogenous spread from a primary site, commonly lungs. The common site of involvement in the spine is the paradiscal region because the subchondral bone around the disc space is supplied by the paradiscal vessels. This infection leads to progressive vertebral destruction and kyphotic deformity of the spine. The patient typically presents with back pain. Rest pain is rare. Radicular pain can be the presenting symptom. Constitutional symptoms of weight loss and fever are less commonly associated (more common with pulmonary involvement). There can be a cold abscess in the affected region. Nerve compression can be secondary to vertebral body collapse, leading to exaggerated DTRs or sensory loss.
• Option B. Multiple myeloma . Multiple myeloma is a neoplastic proliferation of plasma cells affecting multiple organ systems. The patient may present with anemia, fatigue, bone pain that worsens on movement and in the night. The affected vertebra is collapsed or have a compression fracture. Symptoms of weight loss, nausea, muscle weakness and mental confusion may be present along with kidney failure, hypercalcemia.
• Option D. Bone metastasis . In bone metastasis, there is sparing of the intervertebral space. The symptoms of spinal canal invasion and spinal cord compression may be present. Pain is the most common presenting initial symptom in patients. The pain is aching and deep, often occurs at night. Also, if nerve involvement has occurred, the pain is sharp and shooting in a specific dermatomal distribution. When the tumor has extended inside the spinal canal, motor, and sensory weakness may become permanent.
• Educational objective:
• The provided information about the patient’s symptoms of severe back ache with urinary incontinence with history of intervertebral disc prolapse, without fever or weight loss is suggestive of cauda equina syndrome. Remember, bladder and bowel involvement is typical of cauda equina syndrome, and it is not associated with constitutional symptoms, rather it presents with disc prolapse and cord compression.

question: Compound palmar ganglion is:
options: Ulnar bursitis due to compound injury

explanation.short: Ans. A) Tuberculosis affection of ulnar bursa
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Pyogenic affection of ulnar bursa. Pyogenic or suppurative flexor tenosynovitis is a severe bacterial infection within the closed space of the digital flexor tendon sheaths. It can cause necrosis of the tendons and devitalization of fingers. It alters the gliding mechanism and creates adhesions within the flexor tendon sheath, resulting in marked loss of finger movements. It can result from bloodstream infection but is generally caused by penetrating finger injuries involving the flexor tendon sheath. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common bacteria causing this infection. The detection of the 4 Kanavel signs on physical assessment has high sensitivity. A painful passive extension is the earliest sign while flexor sheath tenderness is the last sign to be detected. The presence of erythema, swelling, and tenderness over the thenar eminence in the palm raises the suspicion of the proximal spread of infection to the radial bursa. Patients with an infected ulnar bursa present with pain, swelling, and erythema over the hypothenar eminence.
• Educational objective:
• Compound palmar ganglion is the chronic, tuberculous infection of the ulnar bursa.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient gives a history of twisting strain and locking of the knee joint. The most likely diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Avulsion of tibial tubercle. Tibial tubercle fractures are most frequently seen in sports that involve jumping activities. Injury may be caused by quadriceps contraction during knee extension while initiating a jump. Damage can also take place during landing when the quadriceps contracts and the knee flexes to absorb the impact of landing. The patellar ligament inserts on the secondary ossification center, which places the tibial tubercle at risk for an avulsion injury.
• Option C. Tearing of lateral collateral ligament . The most common mechanism of injury is seen via a high-energy blow to the anteromedial knee, combining hyperextension and extreme varus force.
• Option D. Tear of anterior cruciate ligament . A common and frequent injury mechanism is non-contact combined valgus and internal-rotation trauma. Therefore, ACL injuries are often associated with other ligamentous injuries, such as a (partial) rupture of the medial collateral ligament (MCL) or the menisci.
• Educational objective:
• The given history of twisting strain and locking of the knee joint is the most common mechanism of injury of meniscal tear.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Most frequently results from an incidence of acute trauma. False. Cervical spondylosis is an age-related degeneration of the intervertebral disc and cervical spinal elements. Degenerative changes in surrounding structures, including the facets joints, posterior longitudinal ligament (PLL), and ligamentum flavum all combine to cause narrowing of the spinal canal and intervertebral foramina. Consequently, the spinal cord, spinal vasculature, and nerve roots can be compressed, resulting in axial neck pain, cervical myelopathy, and cervical radiculopathy.
• Option B. Causes compression of nerve roots to produce an upper motor neuron lesion in the lower limbs. False. Cervical myelopathy due to spondylotic changes leads to spinal cord compression and consequently upper motor neuron symptoms in the upper limb like spasticity, and hyperreflexia.
• Option D. Most frequently affects the upper cervical vertebrae. False. Cervical spondylosis involves most commonly the lower cervical vertebrae i.e., C4-C5, C5-C6, C6-C7.
• Educational objective:
• Cervical spondylosis produces pain and paresthesia over the lateral aspect of the forearm and thumb when the 6 th cranial nerve is affected.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The following are True regarding Rotator cuff tendinitis except:
options: Injection of local anesthetic helps in diagnosis
Painful arc of movement causes secondary weakness
Acromial beak of bone appears with age

explanation.short: Ans. B) Steroid injections are contraindicated

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient sustained injury to his knee with a twisting force. On examination, pain is felt more on medial femoral side of knee than tibial side. Injured structure might be:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anterior cruciate ligament . The most common mechanism of ACL injury is a sudden pivoting or cutting maneuver during sports activity, which is commonly seen in football, basketball, and soccer. The ligament can also tear due to automobile accidents. At the time of the injury, a “pop” or “snap” can sometimes be felt or heard. The amount of pain experienced at the time of the injury is can be severe. Immediate swelling of the knee develops at the time of injury within the first few hours, but the extent of swelling can be limited if the knee is immediately iced or splinted. There is an inability to bear weight on leg along with an instability of the knee.
• Option B. Posterior cruciate ligament . The PCL is usually injured by hyperxtension of the knee while landing after jumping. The PCL can also be injured from a direct blow to the flexed knee, such as falling hard on a bent knee. Most PCL injuries occur with other ligament injuries and severe knee trauma. Often, the knee is dislocated and the nerves and blood vessels are injured. The symptoms of the injury include knee swelling, tenderness in the popliteal fossa, joint instability and pain.
• Option D. Lateral collateral ligament . A lateral collateral ligament (LCL) injury is usually caused by pressure or an injury that pushes the knee joint from the inside, which results in stress on the outside part of the joint. The symptoms of a tear in the lateral collateral ligament can include knee swelling, locking or catching of knee with movement, pain or tenderness along the outside of knee, and a feeling of giving way.
• Educational objective:
• History of a twisting injury along with pain on the medial side of the knee is suggestive of injury to the medial collateral ligament.
