subject: Orthopedics
question: Sudden inversion of foot leads to rupture of which tendon?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Deltoid ligament . Deltoid Ligament Injury causes pain around medial malleolus. The x-rays appear normal. The ligament has tibial attachment and the other expansions are into talus, navicular, calcaneum, spring ligament. The most common mechanism of injury is supination and external rotation or pronation and external rotation.
• Option C. Posterior talofibular ligament . Posterior talofibular ligament (PTFL) injuries are less common than other lateral collateral ligament injuries of the ankle and almost always occur with other injuries. One potential reason for relatively lower PTFL injury rates it is not a major stabilizer of the lateral ankle joint. PTFL injuries have been associated with talar dome ostechondral defect, calcaneofibular ligament (CFL) injury, ATFL injury, and ankle dislocation.
• Option D. Spring ligament . Spring ligament injuries or calcaneonavicular ligament injuries refer to stretching sprains, tears, or ruptures of the plantar calcaneonavicular ligament complex and can affect one or more of the three portions. Spring ligament complex injuries are most commonly associated with posterior tibial tendon dysfunction in adult acquired flat foot and are rare in isolation. Middle-aged women are most commonly affected.
• Educational objective:
• Sudden inversion of the foot leads to rupture of the anterior talofibular ligament, with or without foot plantarflexion.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 23-year-old professional footballer suffered a twisting injury to his right ankle. On examination, there is a lot of swelling around the medial malleolus but X-ray doesn’t show any fracture. The structure injured could be:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Anterior talofibular ligament. ATFL injuries typically occur with an inversion injury to the ankle, either with or without plantar flexion. The x-ray in ATFL injury may show an avulsion fracture at the fibular end.
• Option C. Spring ligament . A spring ligament tear can occur because of failure of the tibialis posterior tendon in adult-acquired flatfoot deformity or as an isolated injury with a normal tibialis posterior tendon. Plain radiographs may show flat foot deformity or evidence of hindfoot valgus.
• Option D. Tendo Achilles . Causes of Achilles tendon rupture include sudden forced plantar flexion of the foot, direct trauma, and long-standing tendinopathy or intratendinous degenerative conditions. Plain radiographs may show soft tissue swelling and obliteration of pre-Achilles fat pad (Kager's triangle).
• Educational objective:
• Twisting injury of the ankle with swelling around the medial malleolus and normal x-ray are suggestive deltoid ligament injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest ligament injured in ankle injury:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Calcaneofibular ligament. This is the 2 nd most commonly injured ligament after ATFL.
• Option C. Posterior talofibular ligament. Injury to this ligament have lower rates, around 12%.
• Option D. Spring ligament. Isolated tears of the spring ligament without associated Posterior Tendon tear are extremely rare and can present as an acquired flatfoot deformity.
• Educational objective:
• The most commonly injured ligament in the ankle injury is the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL).

subject: Orthopedics
question: When the foot is in plantar flexed position, if it is suddenly inverted, which of the following ligament will be injured?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Posterior tibiofibular ligament. The PTFL is the strongest of the lateral ligaments, and extreme inversion with plantar flexion is required to place the PTFL under stress; as a result, the PTFL is less commonly injured.
• Option C. Calcaneocuboid ligament. The mechanism of injury can be inversion +/- plantarflexion, or eversion.
• Option D. Calcaneofibular ligament. Calcaneofibular ligament injuries typically occur along with an anterior talofibular ligament injury. The typical mechanism is excessive supination of the rearfoot on an externally rotated lower leg, leading to an inversion-internal-rotation type injury.
• Educational objective:
• With the foot in plantarflexion and sudden inversion, the ligament most commonly injured is the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL).

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 30-year-old man involved in a fight, injured his middle finger and noticed slight flexion of DIP joint. X-rays were normal. The most appropriate management at this stage is:
options: Surgical repair of the flexor tendon

explanation.short: Ans. B) Splint the finger in hyperextension

• Explanation:
• Option C. Surgical repair of the flexor tendon. Surgery is not advised in this condition due to the related high complication rate and poor outcome.
• Educational objective:
• The ideal treatment for mallet finger is splinting the finger in hyperextension for 6-8 weeks using a stack or mallet hyperextension splint.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A previously healthy 45-year-old labourer suddenly develops acute lower back pain with right leg pain and weakness of dorsiflexion of the right great toe. Which of the following is true?
options: Immediate treatment should include analgesics, muscle relaxants and back strengthening exercises
The appearance of the foot drop indicates early surgical intervention
If the neurological sign resolve within 2-3 weeks but low back pain persists, the proper treatment would include fusion of affected lumbar vertebra
If the neurological signs fail to resolve within 1 week, lumbar laminectomy and excision of any herniated nucleus pulposus should be done

explanation.short: Ans. B) The appearance of the foot drop indicates early surgical intervention
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Immediate treatment should include analgesics, muscle relaxants and back strengthening exercises . Immediate treatment does includes analgesics and muscle relaxants to reduce pain but exercises are contraindicated in acute pain.
• Option C. If the neurological sign resolve within 2–3 weeks but low back pain persists, the proper treatment would include
• fusion of affected lumbar vertebra . Before carrying out any surgical procedure for an acute low back pain, at least 6 weeks of trial of conservative treatment methods like analgesics, rest and physiotherapy is recommended.
• Option D. If the neurological signs fail to resolve within 1 week, lumbar laminectomy and excision of any herniated nucleus pulposus should be done. Bowel bladder involvement, increasing neurological deficit or failure of conservative treatment is an indication for surgery.
• Educational objective:
• In an acute low back pain presenting with weak dorsiflexion of great toe and foot drop symptoms, surgical intervention is the emergent treatment of choice.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A patient has decreased sensation on tip of middle finger and decreased triceps reflex. This presentation can be linked to disc prolapse at:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. C5-C6. This would compress the C6 nerve root, so the pain, tingling, and/or numbness may be felt on the thumb side of the hand. There may be weakness in the biceps and wrist extensor muscles in the forearm.
• Option C. C8-T1. In this case, C8 nerve will be affected, so the patient will experience neck and shoulder pain. There may be paresthesia of the medial forearm, medial hand, and medial two digits along with weakness during finger flexion, in handgrip, and thumb extension.
• Option D. T1-T2. Since this is an extremely rare condition to occur, most of the thoracic disc herniations are asymptomatic. Pain is the most common presentation and is usually located at the thoracic spine level with radiation. Other physical findings are unilateral or bilateral sensory loss, weakness of muscles, and bladder or bowel dysfunctions. T1 radiculopathy may be accompanied by C8 radiculopathy symptoms. Numbness of the 4 th -5 th fingers or weakness of intrinsic muscles in the hand.
• Educational objective:
• C6 nerve root supplies the thumb and the index finger while C7 supplies the middle finger. So, disc prolapse at C6-C7 regions will cause sensory impairment at the tip of the middle finger, along with decreased triceps reflex.

subject: Orthopedics
question:
The following X-ray was taken of a 50-year-old female with chronic backache. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture1_mq8DCjo.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Spondylolysis. In this condition, the x-ray image shows Scotty dog sign on oblique radiographs which is a break in the pars interarticularis appearing as a collar around the dog's neck. Inverted Napoleon hat sign can be also seen.
• Option B. Spondylosis. Spondylosis involves degeneration of the spine, presence of bony spurs or osteophytes.
• Option D. Prolapsed intervertebral disc. PIVD presents with decreased intervertebral space.
• Educational objective:
• The given x-ray image shows spondylolisthesis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Partial anterior dislocation of one segment of the spine over another is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Spondylosis. It is the progressive degenerative changes that affects the cervical spine. It is a natural process of aging and presents in people after the fifth decade of life.
• Option C. Kyphosis. Kyphosis is defined as an increase in the forward curvature of the spine, seen along the sagittal plane.
• Option D. Scoliosis . Scoliosis is a condition in which the spine has an abnormal S-shaped curve seen over three dimensions.
• Educational objective:
• The partial anterior dislocation of one segement of the spine (vertebra) over the another is called spondylolisthesis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Spondylolisthesis is most common at level:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. L4-L5. This is the second most common site of spondylolisthesis after L5-S1.
• Option C. C5-C6. It is less commonly involved in spondylolisthesis than C3-C4 level.
• Option D. C6-C7. Spondylolisthesis is a rare entity at this region of the spine.
• Educational objective:
• Spondylolisthesis is most commonly seen at L5-S1 level.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In spondylolisthesis, there is fracture of vertebra in:

explanation.short: Ans. B) Neural arch pars interarticularis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Spinous process. They can be caused in case of trauma like falls, accidents, severe & sudden twisting/bending, or blow to the spine. Its fracture is not involved in spondylolisthesis.
• Option C. Transverse process. Fractures of the transverse processes may result from direct blunt trauma (as in motor vehicle accidents), violent lateral flexion-extension forces (as in sports particularly football), avulsion of the psoas muscle or Malgaigne fractures of the pelvis. It is not associated with spondylolisthesis .
• Option D. Body. Compression fractures of the vertebral body generally occur from too much pressure on them. This usually results from a combination of bending forward and downward pressure on the spine. For example, falling from a chair in a sitting position on the floor usually causes head to go forward and at the same time, buttocks hit the floor. This causes the spine to bend forward concentrating the pressure on the front part of the spine - the vertebral bodies. The fracture occurs when the bone collapses and the anterior part of the vertebral body forms a wedge shape. The cancellous bone on the inside of the vertebral body is crushed, or compressed.
• Educational objective:
• Spondylolisthesis involves the fracture of pars interarticularis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Cozen’s test is used for the diagnosis of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Golfer’s elbow. In the passive provocative test for golfer’s elbow, the examiner palpates the medial epicondyle of the patient and passively supinates the patient’ forearm while fully extending the elbow, wrist and fingers with the other hand. If the patient experiences pain or discomfort, the test is considered positive.
• Option D. Carpal tunnel syndrome. Carpal Compression Test or Median Compression Test or Durkan’s test is a diagnostic test for carpal tunnel syndrome . With the patient in sitting or standing and forearm supinated, the examiner applies direct pressure over the carpal tunnel between the thenar and hypothenar eminence for 30 seconds. A positive test indicates numbness, pain or paresthesia in the nerve’s distribution.
• Educational objective:
• Cozen’s test is a provocative test used for the diagnosis of tennis elbow.

subject: Orthopedics
question: de Quervain’s tenosynovitis involves:

explanation.short: Ans. C) Both of the above

subject: Orthopedics
question: Finkelstein’s test is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Golfer’s elbow. In the passive provocative test for golfer’s elbow, the examiner palpates the medial epicondyle of the patient and passively supinates the patient’ forearm while fully extending the elbow, wrist and fingers with the other hand. If the patient experiences pain or discomfort, the test is considered positive.
• Option C. Trigger finger. The patient is asked to actively open and close the fingers of the hand as in making and opening a fist. This will cause locking of the finger at a point.
• Option D. Tennis elbow. The most commonly used test for tennis elbow is cozen’s test where the patient is asked to move the wrist in dorsiflexion with the elbow in extension and pronation, wrist in radial abduction, and hand in a fist, while the examiner applies resistance to this movement. This produces pain on the lateral epicondyle.
• Educational objective:
• Finkelstein’s test is used to provocate the pain symptoms of de Quervain’s tenosynovitis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The best treatment for Dupuytren’s contracture is:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Subtotal fasciectomy + Skin transplantation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fasciotomy. Needle fasciotomy is an outpatient procedure in which one or more fibrous contractures are sectioned using a blade or the bevel of a needle. Sectioning is achieved by moving the needle in a sawing motion against the fibrous band repeatedly until the band breaks, or until partial sectioning has been achieved and the finger can be extended causing the band to snap. Depending on the severity of the condition some individuals may require more than one session, particularly if there is contracture of the PIP joint. Based on the evidence, the main benefit offered by this procedure is a short-term reduction in the degree of contracture. Recurrence rate is approximately 50% at 3-5 years. Common complications reported in the studies include skin breaks, localized pain and nerve injuries.
• Educational objective:
• The best surgical treatment for dupuytren’s contracture is subtotal fasciectomy with skin transplantation.

question: All are true regarding pes planus except:
options: There is collapse of medial longitudinal arch
The heel becomes valgus and foot pronates at the subtalar mid complex joint
Jack’s test differentiates between flexible and rigid deformity
Tarsal coalition is inherited as X-linked recessive condition

explanation.short: Ans. D) Tarsal coalition is inherited as X-linked recessive condition
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. There is collapse of medial longitudinal arch; Option B. The heel becomes valgus and foot pronates at the subtalar mid complex joint; Option C. Jack’s test differentiates between flexible and rigid deformity are all true about pes planus .
• Educational objective:
• Pes planus involves collapse of medial longitudinal arch, which causes the heel to go into valgus and foot into pronation at the subtalar mid complex joint.
• Tarsal coalition in pes planus is autosomal dominant and not recessive.
• One of the most common type of tarsal coalition seen in pes planus is calcaneonavicular type.
• Jack’s test is used to differentiate between the flexible and rigid flat foot. It is performed with the patient in weight bearing, with flat foot on the ground, and the examiner dorsiflexes the hallux to watch for an increased concavity of the foot arches.

subject: Orthopedics
question: True about Ganglion cyst: Most common in young male Contain synovial fluid Arise from extensor retinaculum It usually arises from the lunotriquetral joint Surgical treatment is excision of cyst

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Most common in young male. False. Ganglion cyst is most common in females .
• Option C. Arise from extensor retinaculum. False . It arises from the leakage of synovial fluid from joint or tendon sheath. The most common locations are dorsal wrist, digital flexor sheath, and distal interphalangeal joint.
• Option D. It usually arise from the lunotriquetral joint. False. Ganglion cyst arises from the scapholunate joint or palmar wrist radioscaphoid joint or scaphotrapezial joint.
• Educational objective:
• Ganglion cyst occurs commonly in females. It contains synovial fluid. It arises from flexor tendon sheath of digits. It occurs on the scapholunate joint or palmar wrist radioscaphoid joint or scaphotrapezial joint. Surgical treatment is excision of the cyst.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Olecranon bursitis is also called:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tennis elbow. Also known as lateral epicondylitis, tennis elbow is an overuse injury secondary to eccentric overload of the common extensor origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon.
• Option B. Golfer’s elbow. Golfer’s elbow or medial epicondylitis or pitcher’s elbow is the tendinopathy due to overuse of the medial common flexor tendon of the elbow.
• Option D. Lesser leaguer’s elbow. Little Leaguer’s Elbow (Medial Apophysitis) affects young athletes, specifically baseball pitchers, whose bones are still growing. This condition is caused by overusing the elbow. Improper techniques can put stress on the arm’s growth plate, causing it to become inflamed and irritated. In severe cases, the growth plate may break away from the arm.
• Educational objective:
• Olecranon bursitis is also called as student’s elbow.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Site of TB bursitis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Prepatellar bursa. Tuberculous infection of the prepatellar bursa is a rare occurrence.
• Option B. Subacromial bursa. There are a considerable number of cases of septic subacromial bursitis caused by non-specific organisms and rare cases caused by a tuberculous process. The reported cases of tuberculous bursitis in this location were mainly secondarily extended from the shoulder joint. Primary tuberculosis of the subacromial bursitis is quite rare.
• Option C. Subdeltoid bursa . Involvement of the subdeltoid bursa is extremely rare.
• Educational objective:
• The most common site of tuberculous bursitis is that of the trochanteric/gluteal bursa.

• Option B. Clergyman’s knee . Infraptellar bursitis is located just below the patella.
• Option D. Morrant Baker’s cyst . Baker’s cyst is present on the posterior aspect of the knee.
• Educational objective:
• Location of bursitis of knee:
• Prepatellar : superificially between the skin and the patella Infraptellar : inferior to the patella Pes anserine : medial to the knee Baker’s cyst : posteriorly in the popliteal foss

subject: Orthopedics
question:
Identify the abnormality shown in the following picture.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/picture131.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Hallux varus. It is characterized by the medial deviation of the great toe at MTP joint. More frequently, this deformity develops after a surgical procedure for hallux valgus because of overcorrection, excessive lateral release, over-resection of medial eminence, over-plication of the medial capsule, zero-degree or negative intermetatarsal angle, or immobilization of the toe in excessive varus after surgery. Other causes include trauma and certain systemic inflammatory diseases such as psoriasis and rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option C. Rheumatoid nodule . Rheumatoid nodules develop over pressure points in rheumatoid arthritis. They develop along or within tendons in response to pressure and friction. These nodular growths are soft tissue enlargements that can vary in size. They are commonly located in the forefoot areas and often involve the same areas of both feet. Rheumatoid nodules are usually not painful but can cause problems like difficulty in wearing shoes, gait and balance abnormalities, and cosmetic issues.
• Educational objective:
• The given image in the question shows the hallux valgus deformity which is the lateral or outward deviation of the great toe.

• Option B. Patella. The osteochondiritis of the lower pole of patella is known as Johanson-Larsen’s disease.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Microfracture technique is carried out for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Non-union. Current treatment of non-union bone fractures involves surgery to stabilize the area and the insertion of a bone graft at the site to stimulate vasculogenesis and osteogenesis.
• Option C. Tumors . Treatments for tumors include surgery to remove the tumor; chemotherapy to shrink the tumor before surgery or destroy lingering abnormal cells after surgery; immunotherapy; radiation therapy; and targeted therapy to slow or stop the growth of cancer cells.
• Option D. Osteopetrosis . Osteopetrosis treatment can include medications to delay the disease progress and improve blood cell levels; physical and occupational therapy to develop motor and other skills; bone care with casts, splints; surgery to correct bone shape; and bone marrow transplant in certain cases.
• Educational objective:
• To treat osteochondral defects, microfracture technique is used which involves substitution of hyaline cartilage by fibrocartilage.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Meyer pedicle graft is used for:
options: Fracture through neck of the talus
Fracture through the waist of the scaphoid

explanation.short: Ans. B) Non-union fracture neck of femur
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fracture through neck of the talus. Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) with Iliac crest cancellous bone graft is a reliable method for neglected non-union fracture of neck talus.
• Option C. Fracture through the waist of the scaphoid. Free vascular graft can be used for scaphoid union.
• Option D. Inter-trochanteric fracture femur. Valgus intertrochanteric osteotomy with fibular grafting is an effective procedure to achieve union in neglected femoral neck fractures in patients less than 60 years of age.
• Educational objective:
• Meyer’s pedicle graft of quadriceps femoris is used in the treatment of non-union of femoral neck fracture.

question: A 50-year-old man sustained posterior dislocation of left hip in an accident. Dislocation was reduced after 3 days. He started complaining of pain in left hip after 6 months. X-ray of the pelvis was normal. The most relevant investigation at this stage will be:

subject: Orthopedics
question: A woman of 45 years of age, a known cause of pemphigus vulgaris on a regular treatment with controlled primary disease presented with pain in the right hip and knee. Examination revealed no limb length discrepancy but the patient has tenderness in the Scarpa’s triangle and limitation of abduction and internal rotation of the right hip joint as compared to the other side. The most probable diagnosis is:
options: Stress fracture of neck of femur

explanation.short: Ans. B) Avascular necrosis of femoral head
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Stress fracture of neck of femur . In Stress fracture, there is no limitation of abduction and internal rotation.
• Option C. Perthes disease and Option D. Transient synovitis of hip . Perthes (4-8 years) and transient synovitis (6-12 years) will not be considered in this age group.
• Educational objective:
• The given clinical presentation of hip pain without leg length discrepancy, scarpa’s triangle tenderness, and limited abduction and internal rotation of the hip; and history of treatment of pemphigus vulgaris with steroids, points towards the diagnosis of avascular necrosis of the hip.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Jumper’s knee is:
options: Apophysitis of patellar tendon as it inserts in patella
Apophysitis of patellar tendon as it inserts in tibia
Apophysitis of quadriceps tendon as it inserts in patella
Apophysitis of hamstring tendon as it inserts in tibia

explanation.short: Ans. A) Apophysitis of patellar tendon as it inserts in patella
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Apophysitis of patellar tendon as it inserts in tibia . This is known as Osgood-Schlatter Disease . It is a condition where the patellar tendon insertion on the tibial tuberosity is inflamed. It occurs in late childhood, characterized by pain and bony prominence over the tibial tuberosity. The pain is usually exacerbated by physical activities like running, jumping, and climbing stairs. In the acute stage, the margins of the patellar tendon become blurred in radiographs due to the soft tissue swelling. After three to four months, bone fragmentation at the tibial tuberosity is viewed. In the sub-acute stage, soft tissue swelling resolves, but the bony ossicle remains. In the chronic stage, the bone fragment may fuse with the tibial tuberosity which can appear normal. The primary goal in the treatment is the reduction of pain and swelling over the tibial tuberosity. The patient should limit physical activities until the symptoms are resolved.
• Educational objective:
• Apophysitis of patellar tendon as it inserts in patella is known as Jumper’s knee or patellar tendonitis.

question: In cervical spondylosis, which part of vertebral body is involved?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above

subject: Orthopedics
question: Positive pivot shift test in knee is because of injury to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Posterior cruciate ligament. In PCL injury, posterior drawer test is positive.
• Option C. Medial meniscus. Apley grind test is positive in the medial meniscus injury.
• Option D. Lateral meniscus. McMurray test is positive in a lateral meniscus tear.
• Educational objective:
• The pivot shift is a dynamic test of knee stability, carried out by the examiner passively. The movement is a combination of axial load and valgus force, applied by the examiner, during knee flexion from an extended position. When the test is positive, it indicates an injury of the anterior cruciate ligament.

To see the anterior portion of lateral meniscus

Movement of tibial tuberosity towards lateral border of patella

question: It is wise to keep and repair the meniscus rather than removing it when the injury is to which of the following?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Peripheral part of meniscus

subject: Orthopedics
question: An 18-year-old boy was playing football when he suddenly twisted his knee on the ankle and fell down. He got up after 10 minutes and again started playing, but next day his knee was swollen and he could not move it. The most probable cause is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Anterior cruciate ligament tear. ACL injury also occurs following a twist or internal rotation trauma. The swelling in this case appears immediately due to hemarthrosis.
• Option C. Medial collateral ligament injury. MCL is also injured due to twisting force with knee in valgus, flexion and internal rotation of the femur on tibia while the foot is planted on ground. The swelling in this case appears immediately towards the medial side of the knee.
• Option D. Posterior cruciate ligament. It is injured when the knee is flexed with posterior dislocation of tibia. Swelling in this case occurs 2-3 hours after the injury.
• Educational objective:
• The given history of sudden twisting of knee on ankle and presence of swelling on the next day with inability to move the knee are suggestive of medial meniscus injury.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Commonest dangerous complication of posterior dislocation of knee is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Sciatic nerve injury. Sciatic nerve is injured in cases of traumatic hip fracture-dislocations.
• Option C. Ischemia of lower leg compartment. This can be caused following a traumatic crush injury or fracture or muscle rupture.
• Option D. Femoral artery injury. Femoral artery is injured in cases of trauma by bicycle (handlebar injury).
• Educational objective:
• The most dangerous complication of posterior knee dislocation is popliteal artery rupture as it can lead to limb amputation.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which is the investigation of choice for a sport injury of the knee?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ultrasonography . Ultrasonography is a valuable imaging method to evaluate for sports injuries. In the lower extremity, ankle sprains, muscle injury and groin pain are the most common problems benefiting from imaging. Tendinopathy, tendon injury and ligament injury are well visualised in the hip, knee, ankle and foot by diagnostic ultrasound.
• Option B. Plain radiography . If the injury is restricted to the cartilage, it will not show up in a plain X-ray; it may be noted on an MRI.
• Option C. Arthrography . At present, conventional MR imaging of the knee has completely replaced conventional arthrography for the evaluation of internal derangement of the knee. CT or MR arthrography is used for meniscus injury evaluation in cases where the conventional MRI or CT are contraindicated.
• Educational objective:
• Arthroscopy is the investigation of choice in cases of sports injury of the knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bucket handle tear at knee joint is due to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Injury to medial collateral ligament. This can present as swelling on the medial side of the knee and opening of the medial joint space on plain radiograph.
• Option B.  Injury to lateral collateral ligament. This can present as pain and swelling on the lateral side of the knee.
• Option C. Injury to ligamentum patellae. This may present as loss of soft tissue margin of patellar tendon, increased density of infrapatellar fat pad, angulation and wavy soft tissue margin of patellar tendon, and patella alta.
• Educational objective:
• Bucket handle tear is seen in the medial meniscus injury of the knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common cause of non-insertional tendonitis of tendoachilles is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Overuse. Overuse of the tendoachilles leads to insertional tendonitis of the tendon.
• Option B. Improper shoe wear. Improper footwear most commonly causes plantar fasciitis.
• Option D. Steroid injections. Significant side effects have been reported with the use of corticosteroid injections as they can lead to tendon degeneration and rupture. It can inhibit tendon repair, delay tendon sheath healing, and produce tendon degeneration.
• Educational objective:
• The non-insertional tendonitis of the tendoachilles is most commonly seen in the runners and the jumpers.

subject: Orthopedics
question: High crural index is seen in:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Sudden dorsiflexion of foot may lead to which of the following injuries?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anterior talofibular ligament injury. This ligament of the ankle is injured from an inversion force on the plantarflexed foot.
• Option C. Rupture of deltoid ligament. Deltoid ligament of the ankle is ruptured due to eversion &/or pronation injury, or can be associated with lateral ankle fractures.
• Option D. Tarsal tunnel syndrome. This can be caused from extrinsic factors like poor fitted footwear, valgus/varus hindfoot, lower extremity edema, systemic inflammatory arthropathy or diabetes; and intrinsic factors like tendinopathy, tenosynovitis, or osteophytes.
• Educational objective:
• Sudden dorsiflexion of the foot may lead to avulsion injury of the tendo-achilles.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All are the common sites of tendon rupture except:

explanation.short: Ans. D) Extensor Hallucis longus tendon

question: Ruptured tendon is most commonly seen in:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Game keepers thumb is:
options: Thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, ulnar collateral ligament rupture
Thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, radial collateral ligament rupture
Thumb interphalangeal joint, ulnar collateral ligament rupture
Thumb interphalangeal joint, radial collateral ligament rupture

explanation.short: Ans. A) Thumb metacarpophalangeal joint ulnar collateral ligament rupture
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, radial collateral ligament rupture. Injuries to the radial collateral ligament (RCL) of the thumb are relatively uncommon when compared to the ulnar side. Injury to the radial collateral ligament most commonly results from a sudden thumb metacarpophalangeal adduction or from axial loading of the thumb.
• Educational objective:
• Gamekeeper’s thumb is the rupture of the ulnar collateral ligament on the metacarpophalangeal joint of the thumb due to forceful radial and palmar abduction of the thumb.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A Cricketer while catching a Ball, gets hit on thumb. Which damage should be looked for specifically?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Volar plate. Volar plate is injured when the thumb is hyperextended or hyperabducted. This is called a jammed or sprained finger. Along with the volar plate, the collateral ligament tear or a bone avulsion can also occur.
• Educational objective:
• While catching a ball, the ulnar collateral ligament of the thumb is most likely to be injured in a cricketer.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Regarding No Man’s land in hand surgery, all of the following statements are false except: Results of flexor tendon repair & satisfactory in this area Comprises zone II Extends from distal palmar crease and flexor crease of PIP Extends from distal palmar crease and insertion of flexor superficialis It is for extensor tendons

subject: Orthopedics
question: While examining a patient in supine position, the examiner keeps his hand below the heel of one foot and asks the patient to flex the other hip against resistance, while keeping the weak leg straight. This is known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Waddell’s test. It is consists of tests of malingering. Each test counts as +1 if positive, 0 if negative. Components of test are :
• If 3+ points or more are present, investigate for non-organic cause.
• Option B. O’Donoghue’s test. The cervical spine of a seated patient is passively flexed, extended, laterally flexed to both sides, and rotated in both directions against patient resistance. Pain precipitated by such isometric contraction indicates cervical strain. The test is then repeated without patient resistance. Pain precipitated by passive unrestricted motion signifies cervical sprain.
• Option D. McMurray test. It is used to check for meniscus tear. The patient lies supine with the knee flexed. The examiner holds the knee and palpates the joint line with the proximal hand. With the distal hand, the examiner holes the sole of the foot. From the flexed position, the examiner extends the knee with internal rotation of the tibia and applies a varus stress, then return to maximal flexion and extend the knee with external rotation of tibia and valgus stress. Internal rotation + varus stress tests the lateral meniscus, while external rotation + valgus stress tests the medial meniscus. The test is positive if there is pain, snapping, an audible click or locking.
• Educational objective:
• While examining a patient in supine position, the examiner keeps his hand below the heel of one foot and asks the patient to flex the other hip against resistance, while keeping the week leg straight. This is known as Hoover’s test.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Straight leg raising test is positive in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Spinal stenosis . In spinal stenosis, the SLR test is negative.
• Option B. Spinal abscess . The SLR test can be positive in about 11-13% of cases of spinal abscess.
• Educational objective :
• SLR test is positive in Prolapsed intervertebral disc.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Removal of vertebral disc is done by all these methods except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Laminotomy. In laminotomy, a piece of the bone is removed that is pressing on the nerves. The bone piece can be a bony spur impinging on the nerves. This can be used for prolapsed intervertebral disc or spinal stenosis (MC).
• Option B. Laminectomy. This procedure involves the removal of the entire lamina of the vertebra. Removal of lamina is followed by discectomy to remove the herniated disc. This is the most commonly performed procedure for the herniated disc in lumbar and cervical regions.
• Option C. Laminoplasty. Cervical laminoplasty is an effective and useful technique for the surgical treatment of degenerative cervical myelopathy.
• Option D. Hemilaminectomy. Hemilaminectomy is a minimally invasive surgical procedure used to decompress the neural tissues. Decompression involves the removal of structures/tissues compressing/irritating the spinal nerves. The hemilaminectomy differs from laminectomy surgery in the removal of a very small part of the lamina. The surgery is commonly used to relieve symptoms caused by spinal stenosis and PIVD in the lumbar spine.
• Educational objective:
• The removal of vertebral disc in case of PIVD or spinal canal stenosis is done by laminotomy/laminectomy/hemilaminectomy. Laminoplasty procedure is used for the treatment of cervical myelopathy.

subject: Orthopedics
question: When do you operate for prolapsed disc?
options: Severe pain interfering with activity and not relieved by rest and treatment of 8 weeks
Patient of PID with difficulty in ambulation

explanation.short: Ans. C) Severe pain interfering with activity and not relieved by rest and treatment of 8 weeks

subject: Orthopedics
question: Test used for prolapsed lumbar intervertebral disc is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Active straight leg raising test. The Active Straight Leg Raise is used to assess load transference through the pelvis. It is an important test in diagnosing Pelvic Girdle Pain. The aSLR test is considered positive when it elicits pain in the lumbo-pelvic region.
• Option C. Thomas test. The Thomas Test measures hip flexor length and distinguishes tightness between one joint and two joint muscles.
• Option D. Apley’s grinding test. The Apley grind test or Apley compression test is a maneuver performed to evaluate meniscus injury.
• Educational objective:
• To test prolapsed lumbar intervertebral disc, Lasegue test or straight leg raise test is used.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Lumbar canal stenosis presents as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Scoliotic deformity. This is not seen associated with lumbar canal stenosis.
• Option C. Kyphotic deformity. This is also not associated with lumbar canal stenosis.
• Option D. Radiculopathy. This may or may not be present in some cases with lumbar canal stenosis.
• Educational objective:
• Lumbar canal stenosis presents as Neurogenic Claudication i.e., worsening of symptoms on standing/walking and relieving on sitting/forward bending.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The most common factor causing Lumbar disc prolapse is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Posterior nucleus pulposus. Nucleus pulposus herniation results from a failure in the annulus fibrosis integrity, making the content of the nucleus to protrude into the neural canal, the intervertebral foramen (foraminal) or lateral to the foramen (extraforaminal). Nucleus pulposus protrusion is the less severe scenario of disc herniation, due to partial rupture of the annulus fibrosis, when the annulus structure becomes completely disrupted, the nucleus content may extrude outside the disc space and in some cases a fragment of nucleus pulposus may migrate ( sequestration ).
• Option C. Weak ligamentum flavum . On morphology, there has been no evidence that ligamentum flavum contributes to the development of prolapse intervertebral disc disease.
• Option D. More degenerative forces . The loss of proteoglycan in degenerated discs has a major effect on the disc's load-bearing behaviour. With loss of proteoglycan, the osmotic pressure of the disc falls and the disc is less able to maintain hydration under load. When loaded, they lose height and fluid more rapidly, and the discs tend to bulge. Due to loss of proteoglycan and matrix disorganization, degenerated discs no longer behave hydrostatically under load. Loading thus leads to inappropriate stress along the end-plate or in the annulus. Such changes in disc behaviour may affect function of other spinal structures and predispose them to injury. For instance, due to rapid loss of disc height under load in degenerated discs, apophyseal joints adjacent to the discs may be subject to abnormal loads and eventually develop osteoarthritic changes. Loss of disc height can also reduce the tensional forces on the ligamentum flavum and hence, may cause remodeling and thickening. With consequent loss of elasticity, the ligament tends to bulge into the spinal canal, leading to spinal stenosis, which is seen most commonly in elderly patients.
• Educational objective:
• In the lumbar spine, the most common factor that leads to disc prolapse is the inadequate or less hydration of the disc due to ageing.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Most common site for lumbar disc prolapse is:

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 44-year-old man presented with acute onset of low backache radiating to the right lower limb. Examination revealed SLRT < 40 degrees on the right side, weakness of extensor hallucis longus on the right side, sensory loss in the first web space of the right foot and brisk knee jerk. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Prolapsed intervertebral disc L4-L5
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Spondylolysis L5-S1. Spondylolysis pain feels like a dull ache pain across the low back area. There is soreness and stiffness in the buttocks and back of the thigh. Bending backward or performing twisting movements increases spondylolysis pain in the low back.
• Option C. Lumbar canal stenosis . Classically, LSS presents with axial low back pain, radiculopathy, or neurological claudication aggravated by ambulation and lumbar extension. Neurogenic claudication is an important feature of LSS. Symptoms are typically bilateral but usually asymmetric. Numbness and tingling typically involve the entire leg. Patients may also report walking upstairs being easier than downstairs. The "shopping cart sign” has also been described, wherein forward flexion relieves back pain. The "simian stance," characterized by walking with the trunk and knees slightly flexed, may be observed.
• Option D. Spondylolisthesis L4-L5 . Spondylolisthesis doesn’t usually have any symptoms. It is commonly seen on X-rays and CT scans as an ‘incidental’ finding. Back pain is the most common symptom that is typically worse with activities such as bending and lifting, and often eases when lying down. As the spine attempts to stabilise the unstable segment, the facet joints enlarge and place pressure on the nerve root causing lumbar spinal stenosis and lateral recess stenosis. As there is forward slippage of one bone on the other, there is also narrowing of the intervertebral foramen (foraminal stenosis). Severe nerve compression can therefore occur with pain, numbness and weakness in the legs. Sometimes loss of control of the bladder and/or bowels can occur due to pressure on the nerves going to these structures.
• Educational objective:
• The prolapsed intervertebral disc at L5-S1 may present with symptoms of low back pain radiating to the limb. The SLR test is positive on the affected side, with weakening of the EHL muscle strength, sensory loss on the first web space of the affected side foot and brisk knee jerk.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is not recommended in the treatment of Chronic Low Back Pain?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Bed rest for 3 months
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. NSAIDs. They are usually the first choice of treatment in a low back pain, acute/chronic.
• Option C. Exercises. After some relief of pain with medications and rest, gentle spine strengthening exercises are recommended under supervision.
• Option D. Epidural steroid injection. They can be given if there is no improvement in pain with medications, rest and physiotherapy.
• Educational objective:
• In a chronic low back pain, bed rest is recommended only for about 2 weeks.

subject: Orthopedics
question: All of the following are associated with frozen shoulder except:

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following movements are restricted in frozen shoulder?

explanation.short: Ans. C) All range of movements

subject: Orthopedics
question: Gradual, painful limitation of shoulder movements in an elderly suggest that the most probable diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteoarthritis . Pain in the shoulder joint is the major sign of arthritis. It can be present in the front, side or back of the shoulder. Some people may have pain even at rest or during any activity. It can be present with or without shoulder stiffness. Often the pain is worse with lifting, carrying heavy objects or after exercising. The pain can often radiate into the arm or, if severe, can radiate down to the elbow and wrist. Stiffness of the joint is another sign of progression of shoulder arthritis. There may be loss of range of motion in the shoulder. Crepitus may be felt which may or may not be painful. Sometimes the shoulder may “lock up.”
• Option C. Myositis ossificans . Heterotrophic ossification in shoulder is a rare entity. It may occur following a trauma to the shoulder. The patient may present with pain, tenderness, swelling and decreased range of motion within few days of the injury. There may be a palpable soft tissue mass in the shoulder. The pain and the size of the mass decreases with time. There is also restricted shoulder motion.
• Option D. Fracture-dislocation . The fracture-dislocation presents with pain, swelling and bruising around the affected area. The patient is unable to move the shoulder. There is a crepitus on movement of the shoulder. There is also a presentation of a deformity in the shoulder that does not look right.
• Educational objective:
• Gradual, painful limitation of the shoulder movements in an elderly patient suggests that there is a presentation of frozen shoulder or periarthritis of the shoulder.

subject: Orthopedics
question: de Quervain’s disease classically affects the:
options: Extensor carpi radialis and extensor pollicis longus
Extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus

explanation.short: Ans. D) Extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 50-year-old diabetic/alcoholic patient presented with 15-degree flexion deformity of the little finger. What is the most appropriate management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Subtotal fasciectomy. This is the preferred surgical option but surgery usually do not cure the disease; it just improves the function of the hand.
• Option C. Total fasciectomy. This can be done if a large portion of hand is involved in Dupuytren’s contracture along with contracture of PIP joint volar capsule.
• Educational objective:
• In the given case scenario with 15° flexion deformity of the finger, wait and watch is the usual treatment option as surgery is indicated when there is >30° of MCP and > 15° of PIP joint contracture.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Trigger finger is:
options: A feature of carpal tunnel syndrome
Injury to fingers while operating a gun
Stenosis tenosynovitis of flexor tendon of affected finger

explanation.short: Ans. C) Stenosis tenosynovitis of flexor tendon of affected finger
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A feature of carpal tunnel syndrome. Trigger finger can occur after the carpal tunnel release.
• Educational objective:
• Trigger finger is the stenosing tenosynovitis of the flexor tendon of the affected finger.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Ganglion is: Cystic tumor of hand Solid tumor of hand Treated by enucleation Unilocular Filled with mucinous fluid

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Solid tumor of hand. Solid-appearing ganglia are unusual and may mimic a benign neoplasm or synovitis.
• Treated by enucleation. The treatment for ganglion cyst is usually leaving it unattended as it may resolve spontaneously, or fluid aspiration can be done to reassure the patient. If the patient experiences pressure symptoms on the nerves, then removal of the cyst is done.
• Educational objective:
• A ganglion cyst is a unilocular cystic tumor of the hand filled with mucinous fluid.
• The treatment of these cysts are generally leaving the cyst to resolve spontaneously or aspiration of the fluid for patient satisfaction or if the pressure symptoms occur, removal of the cyst is done.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bakers cyst is a type of:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Pulsion diverticulum of knee joint

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bursa involved in Clergyman’s knee is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Prepatellar bursa . It is involved in housemaid’s knee .
• Option C. Olecranon bursa . It is involved in student’s/miner’s elbow .
• Option D. Ischial bursa . It is involved in weaver’s bottom .
• Educational objective:
• Infrapatellar bursa of the knee is involved in clergyman’s knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: lschial bursitis is also known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Clergyman’s knee. It is the bursitis of the infrapatellar bursa.
• Option B. Housemaid’s knee. It is the bursitis of the prepatellar bursa.
• Option D. Student’s elbow. It is the bursitis of the olecranon bursa.
• Educational objective:
• Ischial bursitis is also known as weaver’s bottom.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Bunion is commonly seen at:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Great toe MTP joint
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Medial malleolus. Bursitis of medial malleolus involves pain over the medial malleolus, outside the ankle. It is seen in ice hockey players due to tightly enclosed footwears.
• Option C. Lateral malleolus. Bursitis of lateral malleolus is called as tailor’s ankle. It is seen in miners sitting with legs crossed in tunnels with low ceilings.
• Educational objective:
• Bunion is most commonly seen at the great toe. It forms at the MTP joint, causing the tip of the toe to get pulled towards the smaller toes while forcing the joint to stick out.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Housemaid’s knee is bursitis of:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Infrapatellar bursa. Bursitis of infrapatellar bursa is known as clergyman’s knee.
• Option C. Olecranon. Bursitis of olecranon is known as student’s/miner’s elbow.
• Educational objective:
• Housemaid’s knee is the bursitis of the prepatellar bursa of the knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Prepatellar bursitis is also known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Clergyman’s knee. It is the infrapatellar bursitis of the knee.
• Educational objective:
• Prepatellar bursitis is also known as housemaid’s knee.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Hallux valgus means:

explanation.short: Ans. A) Outward deviation of great toe
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Inward deviation of great toe. This occurs in hallux varus.
• Option C. Outward deviation of fifth toe. This occurs in the valgus of the fifth toe.
• Option D. Inward deviation of fifth toe. This occurs in the varus of the fifth toe.
• Educational objective:
• Hallux valgus means outward deviation of the great toe.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Which of the following is a false statement regarding patellofemoral stress syndrome?
options: Difficulty in standing from prolonged sitting
Pain on posterior aspect of knee

explanation.short: Ans. D) Pain on posterior aspect of knee
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Difficulty in standing from prolonged standing; Option B. Difficulty in climbing stairs; and Option C. Pain during intense sporting activities are all true regarding patellofemoral stress syndrome.
• Educational objective:
• In patellofemoral stress syndrome, there is pain on the anterior aspect of the knee and not posterior.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 15-year-old Female complains of anterior knee pain which increased on climbing stairs and getting up after prolonged sitting. Diagnosis is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Bipartite patella . Bipartite patella is congenital fragmentation of patella and is usually asymptomatic.
• Option C. Plica syndrome . Plica syndrome presents with meniscal symptoms of locking.
• Option D. Patellofemoral arthritis . Patellofemoral arthritis will be seen at a later age and will have pain in all movements of knee.
• Educational objective:
• The given complaints of the patient of anterior knee pain which increases on climbing stairs and getting up after prolonged sitting are clinically diagnostic of chondromalacia patellae.

subject: Orthopedics
question: K.L. splint is used for:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fracture tibia. For the conservative management of a mid-shaft tibial fracture, a tibia brace is used.
• Option C. de Quervain’s tenosynovitis. To treat de Quervain’s tenosynovitis, thumb splints are used.
• Option D. Tennis elbow. for tennis elbow, tennis elbow brace is used.
• Educational objective:
• KL splint is used for plantar fasciitis.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Impingement syndrome refers to:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nerve entrapped in closed space. Entrapment neuropathy is a condition in which a nerve is compressed or entrapped between two structures (ligament and bone). Repetitive motion can cause the ligament and bone to press or rub against the nerve. Over time, this damages the myelin sheath. This causes symptoms of numbness, tingling, burning, or weakness in the fingers or extremities. Usually not serious, entrapment neuropathy can be painful and can affect function if not treated.
• Educational objective:
• Impingement syndrome refers to the entrapment of the soft tissue between two bony structures.

subject: Orthopedics
question: About Kienbock Osteonecrosis, all are true except: Tenderness over base of 5th metatarsal is present Pain is more on wrist flexion than wrist extension Is the osteochondritis of lunate Is associated with positive ulnar variance More common in elderly

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Tenderness over base of 5 th metatarsal is present. False. Kienbock’s osteonecrosis presents with tenderness at the base of the 3 rd metacarpal when the patient makes a fist. Pain is more on wrist flexion than wrist extension. False. The patient experiences pain more on dorsiflexion or extension of the wrist. ​​​​​​​ Is the osteochondritis of lunate. True. Kienbock’s disease is the osteochonditis or osteonecrosis of the lunate bone. ​​​​​​​ Is associated with positive ulnar variance. False. Kienbock is associated with negative ulnar variance i.e., the ulnar articular surface is shorter than the radial surface. ​​​​​​​ More common in elderly. False. Kienbock’s is seen commonly in 20-40 years of age group.
• Educational objective:
• Kienbock’s osteonecrosis presents with tenderness at the base of the 3 rd metacarpal. The patient experiences pain more on dorsiflexion of the wrist. Kienbock is associated with negative ulnar variance. Kienbock’s is seen commonly in 20-40 years of age group.

question: What causes both destruction of bone and reduction of joint space

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Metastasis . Spinal metastasis has a defining feature of sparing of the intervertebral disc space.
• Option C. Multiple myeloma . This involves spinal cord compression due to vertebral body collapse and extradural extension of the tumor.
• Option D. Lymphoma . This commonly involves the vertebral body or the epidural compartment.
• Educational objective:
• Tuberculosis of the spine destructs both the vertebral body as well as the intervertebral disc.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Tuberculosis with polyarthritis is called as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C. von Gierke’s disease . Glycogen storage disease type I (GSD I), also known as Von Gierke disease, is an inherited disorder caused by deficiencies of specific enzymes in the glycogen metabolism pathway. It comprises of 2 subtypes, GSD Ia and GSD Ib. In GSD Ia, there is a deficiency of enzyme glucose-6-phosphatase, leading to hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis. Patients with GSD 1b have normal G6Pase enzyme activity but have a deficiency of the transporter enzyme, glucose-6-phosphate translocase (G6PT), manifesting as hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis typically around 3 to 4 months of age. In suspected patients, genetic testing is the investigation of choice to confirm the diagnosis. Dietary treatment prevents hypoglycemia and improves the life expectancy of patients.
• Option D. Gordon’s disease . Gordon Syndrome is a rare, inherited disorder that affects movement in the joints of the upper and lower limbs, also known as distal arthrogryposis. Patients can be born with several fingers fixed in a flexed position (camptodactyly), clubfoot, and an opening in the roof of the mouth (cleft palate). Their joints are often very stiff or unable to move. Intelligence is usually normal. Gordon syndrome is caused by genetic mutations in the PIEZO2 gene and can be inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Gordon syndrome is diagnosed by clinical examination and genetic testing. Treatment is directed at the symptoms and includes surgery and physical therapy to loosen the joints.
• Educational objective:
• Tuberculosis with polyarthritis is called Poncet’s disease, a reactive arthritis that occurs during the active stage of TB.

question: A 35-year-old lady came with chronic backache. On X-ray, she had a D12 collapse. But intervertebral disc space is maintained. All are possible except:

• Option B. Osteoporosis . This involves compression fracture of the vertebral bodies.
• Option C. Metastasis . Spinal metastasis has a defining feature of sparing of the intervertebral disc space.
• Educational objective :
• All the given conditions in the question have the intervertebral space maintained except for tuberculous lesions which involves the destruction of the vertebral body as well as the intervertebral space and disc.

question: The most common sequelae of tuberculous spondylitis in an adolescent is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fibrous ankylosis . This is usually seen after the healed tuberculosis of the hip and knee.
• Option C. Pathological dislocation . This can also be seen in healed tuberculosis of the hip in children.
• Option D. Chronic osteomyelitis . This may arise from the septic arthritis in children.
• Educational objective :

subject: Orthopedics
question: Wandering acetabulum is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fracture of acetabulum .  On radiographs, fracture of the anterior and posterior columns is characterized by disruption of the iliopectineal line and ilioischial line, respectively.
• Option B. Hip dislocation . On an AP view of the x-ray, a posterior dislocation shows a smaller femoral head in the acetabulum. In an anterior dislocation, the femoral head appears larger than the unaffected hip.
• Educational objective:
• Wandering acetabulum is seen in the stage 4 of the tuberculosis of the hip as the subluxation or dislocation of the femoral head.

subject: Orthopedics
question: In tuberculosis of spine, which one of the following is not a cause for Paraplegia?
options: Stretching of spinal cord in gibbus deformity

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Paraplegia in spinal tuberculosis can be caused due to stretching of the spinal cord from the gibbus deformity/from compression by the granulation tissue/from edema of the spinal cord. Spinal artery compression is the rarest cause of paraplegia due to tuberculosis of the spine. But when it is the cause, it has the poor prognosis. Spinal artery compression can cause dizziness, vertigo, ataxia, dysarthria, and dysphagia to progressive or acute paralysis, hemisensory loss, and cervical myelopathy.

• Option B. Posterior . This is the least common involvement.
• Option C. Central . This is the 2 nd most common involvement after paradiscal type.
• Educational objective :
• The order of involvement of area of the vertebra in spinal tuberculosis is: Paradiscal > Central > Anterior > Posterior

subject: Orthopedics
question: The ideal surgical treatment for Pott’s paraplegia is

explanation.short: Ans. B) Anterior decompression and bone grafting
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Laminectomy and decompression . Laminectomy is occasionally indicated for posterior decompression in posterior vertebral tuberculosis.
• Option B. Anterolateral decompression . This can be considered in late onset Pott’s paraplegia but is more successful and stable with bone grafting along with it.
• Option D. Costotranversectomy . This can be done for tissue diagnosis in thoracic spinal tuberculosis with predicted end deformity of <70⁰. This procedure is simple and yields a good amount of tissue.
• Educational objective :
• The ideal surgical option of choice for pott’s paraplegia is anterior decompression and bone grafting for spinal stability.

subject: Orthopedics
question: A 30-year-old male HIV positive on antiretroviral therapy has pain in right hip region. Flexion, abduction and external rotation deformity of right hip is present for 2 months. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Avascular necrosis . This is less commonly seen in HIV patients. If present, there is flexion, adduction and internal rotation deformity (FADIR) of the hip.
• Option C. Transient synovitis . This is seen in pediatric patients. Since, here the patient is an adult, this option can be ruled out.
• Option D. Septic arthritis . This is also seen commonly in pediatric patients. Since, here the patient is an adult, this option can also be ruled out.
• Educational objective:
• The flexion, abduction and external rotation deformity of the hip in an HIV positive adult patient is suggestive of TB of the hip. Since AVN of the hip in HIV positive patients show FADIR deformity, and other two options of transient synovitis and septic arthritis are commonly seen in pediatric patients, the best answer for the given question is TB of the hip.

subject: Orthopedics
question: The earliest radiological sign in Pott’s disease is?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Narrowing of intervertebral disc space.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Erosion of vertebral bodies. Erosion of the end plates of the vertebral bodies appears late with the progression of the infection.
• Option B. Collapse and destruction of vertebra. This is a very late feature seen on the radiographs.
• Educational objective:
• The earliest radiological sign seen in Pott’s spine is the narrowing of the intervertebral disc space and loss of curvature of the spine due to paravertebral muscle spasm.

subject: Orthopedics
question: Triple deformity of knee is classically seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The most common cause of triple deformity of the knee is tuberculosis of the knee joint. Triple deformity is more common in TB of the knee than in other types of arthritis because it is a chronic infection and the bacteria slowly destroy the joint tissues, leading to the development of deformities. Another reason is that TB often affects the posterior capsule of the knee joint, which stabilize the joint. When this ligament is damaged, it can allow the knee to become unstable and deformed.
